organisation: Port of Calais Extension and Sea Defence
hazard_cause: Sea level rise, Storms and high winds
hazard_effect: Storm surge, Coastal flooding, Coastal erosion
case_study_text: Doubled port capacity with 3.3km seawall designed for 100-year service life, accounting for sea level rise projections.

sections.challenge: • Calais is the leading passenger port in continental Europe and the 4th largest freight port in France, with an expected growth of 40% by 2030. To meet the increased demand for port capacity and maintain climate resilience to sea level rise, the Port of Calais required expansion and a new sea wall. • The project doubled the total area of the port, with the construction of 65 new hectares of platforms, including 45 hectares reclaimed from the sea. This represents a strong step toward preventing flooding and storm surge impacts on the port, as reclaimed area is capable of blocking water from moving further inland, reducing the impact on the port structures. • The project included the creation of a new 3.3 km-long seawall and a 100-hectare port basin, dug to accommodate larger ships. In addition, a new 44-hectare terminal was built through the re-use of excavated land.
sections.adaptation_measures: • The port extension and new seawall was designed to accommodate more and larger ships while protecting the port from rising sea level and storm surges. • The new 3.3km seawall and 100-hectare harbour provides protection to rising sea levels. • 11,000m of retaining walls with reinforced soil with a VSoL® system were created, giving the soil better cohesion, increasing stability and reducing the chance of erosion. • The reinforced port structures were designed with a 100-year service life to take into account sea level rise and climate change. • A detailed study was undertaken to consider the effects of corrosion on metal reinforcements due to seawater and how it may reduce the strength of the retaining walls. The result was that the sizing of the metal reinforcements took into account possible corrosion over the 100-year service life and the reinforcements had a minimum coating of 55mm to reduce corrosion, 10mm greater than the typical coating value. 3,4 • The stability of the port structures under extreme conditions was important to consider and, therefore, a 120% 100-year return level associated with a 100-year storm surge plus 1.2m elevation was considered, with the stability of the seawall as a critical design requirement. 3,4 Organisation Société des Ports du Détroit Transport sub-sectors Ports, coastal and offshore First order climate hazards (cause) Sea level rise; Storms and high winds; Coastal flooding Second order climate hazard (effect) Coastal erosion Transport asset impacted Port structures Adaptation measures Port extension; Sea defence Location  Calais, France Year of delivery 2021
sections.applicability: • Because the case study focuses on protecting against sea-level rise and storm surge, this adaptation approach is widely applicable to ports in the United Kingdom. For instance, it may apply to the Humber Ports, as the Humber authority areas have been identified as having a medium risk of sea level rise, increasing the likelihood of flood damage to infrastructure. • Consideration of local features such as soil composition, proximity to the sea, land stability and coastline management would be needed to take into account site-specific features. • This case study included climate resilience as part of a major port upgrade, which gave an opportunity to consider the spatial layout and need for storm resilience as part of the overall design, and this would be an approach appliable to UK ports.
sections.financials: Decision-making Information not publicly available. Funding Information not publicly available. Initial costs On completion, the project cost a total of €863 million. Ongoing costs Information not publicly available. Avoided costs Information not publicly available. Economic impacts The duration of the project provided over 2,000 jobs with approximately 3,500 people working on site daily. Over 740 local companies from the Hauts-de-France region were involved through contracts worth €185 million, with €93 million awarded to SMEs.
sections.resourcing: Arcadis were commissioned to lead the design and engineering phase and to oversee the construction of the port infrastructure extension, along with Arcadis' partners Bouygues Construction, Spie batignolles and Jan De Nul Group. Port of Calais during extension and sea wall construction Ferry boat in Port of Calais
sections.co_benefits: Increased Capacity • The new Port of Calais is able to accommodate an increased number of travellers and ships; the integration of rail and road infrastructure into the new terminal optimised passenger and freight transit, thus enabling faster docking and boarding in a maximum of 45 minutes. Operation phase energy consumption • The port infrastructure was designed to minimise energy consumption through increased energy efficiency of buildings and utilisation of renewable energy sources. • The inclusion of a seawater heat pump and a source of wind energy supplying the port reduces operational carbon emissions and minimises energy consumption. • Triple glazing of main buildings increases energy efficiency. Biodiversity • Measures were put in place to limit light and noise pollution in construction. • Work scheduling avoided interventions that may disturb fauna during reproduction or migration periods. • During construction, dredgers were equipped with systems to monitor the quality of extracted materials, to ensure conservation of the marine environment and mitigate hydro-sedimentary impacts. • The port infrastructure incorporated resting places for birds as well as a monitoring observatory. 2 7 • Artificial habitats for marine wildlife were created within the port and along the sea wall, providing benefits to biodiversity. 2,7 Construction phase carbon reduction • The seawall was composed predominately of 16,564 concrete Xblocs®. To avoid approximately 100t of CO emissions from more than 160 trucks transporting the manufactured Xblocs® and VSoL® system scales, a prefabrication plant was installed onsite. The decision provided environmental benefits by reducing the carbon impact of construction. 3,6
sections.evaluation: The port infrastructure has been designed to anticipate the long-term effects of climate change. It will be evaluated over time in its ability to accommodate strong storm surges and future sea level rise while protecting the port and allowing the terminal to stay active.
sections.challenges: The main challenge in the design of this project was that space was too limited to expand the port infrastructure onto the existing land available whilst also maintaining the resilience of the site to sea level rise. This challenge was addressed by reclaiming 45 hectares of land from the sea, which in turn presented challenges during the construction phase. Because the construction of the port extension and seawall required building on the sea, workboats were used to carry construction materials . Special material and design criteria were also necessary to ensure structures submerged in seawater avoided the acceleration of corrosion.
sections.lessons_learned: Information not publicly available.

measure_title: To combat these challenges, PEI Transportation Infrastructure and Energy (PEI-TIE) undertook
measure_description: To combat these challenges, PEI Transportation Infrastructure and Energy (PEI-TIE) undertook

sections.challenge: The Trans-Canada Highway (Highway 2) faced challenges from sea level rise, storms and shoreline erosion as it approached the town of Souris on Prince Edward Island (PEI), Canada. This portion of the highway is only a few hundred metres from the shoreline, crossing the Souris River and running along the low-lying Souris Spit. The causeway into the town is a vital conduit for the local economy, which is heavily dependent on tourism and fishing. With a population of 1,079 based on the 2021 census, any disruption due to storms could significantly impact the community and livelihoods. In January 2016, a winter storm highlighted the vulnerability of the highway to erosion and inundation, eroding the small dune system along the beach and bringing flood waters to the edge of the highway. A post-storm survey indicated that a subsequent storm would be capable of breaching the dunes and allow waves to reach the highway’s edge, leading to the temporary closure of Highway 2 and associated repair costs. The rising sea levels, reduced ice cover, and changing storm patterns associated with climate change exacerbate this risk.
sections.adaptation_measures: To combat these challenges, PEI Transportation Infrastructure and Energy (PEI-TIE) undertook dune restoration and shoreline stabilisation works to reinforce the existing beach-dune system. This included the creation of two intertidal reefs, the first implementation of this solution on the Island. The reefs consist of 2,400 tonnes of PEI sandstone, installed 45 metres offshore, running parallel to the shore; each reef is 30 metres long by 10 metres wide. The reefs are a nature-based solution and are designed to attenuate wave action and promote sediment deposition, thereby enhancing the resilience of the beach and dune system. The reefs also help the beach extend offshore, providing additional protection to the highway infrastructure. This adaptation measure includes an adaptive management strategy that allows for structural modifications to be made to the reefs as needed to respond to the rate of beach and dune replenishment. OrganisationPrince Edward Island Transportation Infrastructure and Energy Transport sub-sectorHighways First order climate hazards (cause)Sea-level rise; Storms and high winds; Coastal flooding Second order climate hazards (effect)Coastal erosion Transport asset impactedRoad pavement Adaptation measureIntertidal reefs Location Prince Edward Island, Canada Year of delivery 2018 This measure would be suitable to support the protection of UK coastal infrastructure in areas vulnerable to storms, inundation and shoreline erosion, especially as sea levels rise.
sections.financials: The initial cost of installing the reefs in March 2018 was $115,000 CAD, (equivalent to £66,585 based on an average 2018 exchange rate of 1 CAD : 0.579 GBP) with funding shared between the Province of Prince Edward Island and the federal government through Public Safety Canada’s Natural Disaster Mitigation Program. Traditional armouring approaches previously used to protect Highway 2, whilst successful in the short-term, required periodic and sometimes costly maintenance and replacement over the long term. They also accelerated the erosion and depletion of the beach either side of the armouring. The intertidal reefs, as naturalised infrastructure, require less costly maintenance and were constructed using PEI sandstone. This material is not only less expensive than traditional imported hard stone, but also blends with the surrounding sediment and biodiversity. The details of avoided costs are not publicly available.
sections.resourcing: PEI-TIE worked closely with Coldwater Consulting to develop and construct the shoreline protection scheme. The town of Souris and the Souris Area Branch of the PEI Wildlife Federation were consulted extensively as part of the design process, through both public sessions and inter-agency consultations.
sections.co_benefits: Coastal protection works often involve armouring shorelines to protect land and infrastructure, however this approach can destroy beaches, impacting neighbouring properties more and disrupting habitats for nesting. From both ecological and societal perspectives, the intertidal reefs were an essential step in the province’s development of resilient and sustainable measures to address coastal hazards and climate change. Although not as durable as the hard stone used in armouring, the locally available sandstone blends and degrades into the natural environment and allows for reduced haulage costs and associated CO2 emissions. The use of PEI sandstone for the reefs provided a natural marine substrate for the development of benthic flora and intertidal pools. The intertidal reefs enhanced beach width and protected dunes, allowing for the continued natural succession of the beach ecosystem as it expanded seaward, whilst increasing the overall resilience of the beach-dune system. The changes to the beach have been met with positive feedback from beachgoers and the Souris community.
sections.evaluation: The adaptation measure has been evaluated using site visits, drone overflights and real-time kinematic global positioning system (RTK-GPS) surveys. The reefs have successfully promoted dune growth and increased beach area, with a section of beach connecting out from the shoreline to the reef structures. Feedback from the Souris community and tourists has been positive, highlighting the project's success. To date, there have been minimal downdrift changes to the Souris beach, and benthic growth in and around the reef structures has been encouraging. No modifications have been required to the structure to adjust the beach’s response. Future beach surveys will continue to inform adaptive measures. In summary, the result is increased beach width and protection of the dunes and coastal / highway infrastructure, demonstrating a successful adaptation strategy against the relevant climate risks.
sections.applicability: Given the focus of the case study is the protection against sea-level rise, storms and high winds, this adaptation approach could be extended to other linear transport infrastructure, such as railways, or broader strategic urban coastlines. However, specific consideration must be made for the extent of climate risk, geographical spread, proximity to the coast and geological composition in each case. Additionally, the effects on wider coastline management, as well as sediment erosion and deposition in more built up areas, must also be taken into account.
sections.innovation_opportunities: In addition to acting as a coastal defence to infrastructure, the design of habitats could potentially also promote resilient coastal ecosystems for species vulnerable to coastal erosion and sea level rise, including migratory bird populations where coastal habitats are affected by climate disruption. Reefs and natural coastal management strategies may also be able support carbon-capturing habitats, such as seagrass beds. Additionally, they could enhance the resilience of offshore renewable energy infrastructure, for example by stabilising sediments over cabling, which in turn could contribute to the stability of local transport energy systems. The opportunities from constructed reefs could apply to suitable UK coastal areas and infrastructure resilience. However, it is crucial to consider the effects on coastal sediment Beach, sand dunes and rock reefs on Prince Edward Island Images source: BANHDHA.media/stock.adobe.com movement and downstream coastal areas, given the more intensively developed nature of the UK coastline.

measure_title: into the design of the Copenhagen Metro
measure_description: into the design of the Copenhagen Metro

sections.challenge: In 2019, Copenhagen Metro opened a new city circle line (Cityringen line M3/M4) that is 15.5km long and includes a total of 17 stations. The new line has given a significant boost to public transport, raising the number of passengers using the metro from 64.7 million in 2018 to 78.8 million in 2019. Flooding caused by heavy rainfall (cloudbursts) and storm surges pose significant threats to metro infrastructure with the potential to disrupt operations, damage critical components and endanger passenger safety. Given the metro’s important role in Copenhagen’s transport network, any interruption to service could have widespread economic and social consequences, affecting thousands of daily commuters and businesses reliant on the network. The decision to incorporate climate adaptation measures into the design of the Copenhagen Metro was driven by the city’s recognition of the increasing risks posed by climate change, particularly heavy rainfall and flooding. Adaptation measures were built into the design of the Cityringen, focusing on safeguarding critical assets including station entrances, ventilation systems, tunnels, and other vulnerable points. Copenhagen's urban infrastructure had already experienced disruptions due to extreme weather events, such as the 2011 cloudburst, which caused widespread flooding. Following this event, the city and its Metro operators recognised the need to enhance the resilience of the metro system to both current and future climate impacts.
sections.adaptation_measures: A wide variety of measures have been identified and incorporated in the design of the metro to improve its resilience to flooding, including: The area around the entrances to the underground stations is designed to ensure the runoff of rainwater is away from the openings. Furthermore, at some underground stations, a step has been incorporated, which requires a step up before you go down to the station. Where the metro runs from the sections above the ground down into the tunnels, a strong drainage grate has been established across the tracks where rainwater accumulates and is OrganisationCopenhagen Metro (Metroselskabet) Transport sub-sectorRailways First order climate hazards (cause)Heavy rainfall; Surface water flooding; Storms and high winds; Sea level rise Second order climate hazard (effect)Water damage Transport assets impactedTrack; Signalling and telecommunications; Buildings and stations Adaptation measuresElevated tracks; Waterproof tunnel designs; Improved drainage Location Copenhagen, Denmark Year of delivery2019 pumped out so it does not get into the tunnels. In the above-ground metro system, drains are installed along the tracks leading the water out into the local sewer system. All underground stations have pumping capacity, so if the tunnel is flooded, the water will be automatically pumped away. Installations of waterproof outer doors in the equipment rooms at several stations as well as in electrical and mechanical installations have made them waterproof. Waterproof walls (combination of concrete and gabion walls) up to 2.3m high have been installed in M1 and M2 lines protecting against sea waves along the exposed above-ground metro sections. In the new and planned lines height of the walls might be higher, reflecting the considered increased climate-proof requirements. These interventions were chosen based on climate modelling that highlighted long term climate changes and increasing risks of extreme weather events. The 2014 report from the Danish Meteorological Institute (DMI) on Denmark's future climate, projecting changes up to 2100, indicates that the country will face rising sea levels and increasingly frequent and intense extreme weather events. These findings are derived from the AR5 IPCC scenarios, with climate simulations and uncertainties refined using projections generated by a collection of Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 5 (CMIP5) climate models. The decision-making process included extensive risk assessments, consultations with climate experts, and collaboration with local authorities to ensure that the metro system would not only be resilient to current weather conditions but also prepared for more extreme events in the future. Unlike reactive solutions implemented after a disaster, these proactive measures were integrated during the planning and design phase of the metro line’s development, ensuring long-term resilience and cost efficiency from the start.
sections.applicability: The proactive and integrated climate adaptation planning implemented in the Copenhagen Metro offers examples for other transport sectors vulnerable to climate risks and are applicable to UK transport infrastructure facing similar climate-related challenges from flooding, severe storms and coastal erosion. Early climate risk assessments, as done in Copenhagen, can inform planning and help prioritise vulnerable routes for climate adaptation. Transport asset owners and operators, in particular in the road, heavy rail and light rail sectors, can apply the Circle line adaptation design approaches within UK industry guidance and design projects by incorporating flood defences, improved drainage systems, and weather-resistant materials into infrastructure. The examples from this scheme in Copenhagen may be directly transferable to underground transport infrastructure including the London Underground and tunnelled and subterranean rail and road assets, both in coastal locations and inland. While this adaptation measure has been applied to a new scheme, the specific measures may also be applicable for retrofitting to existing vulnerable assets, in particular ones that have already experienced flooding. Incorporating drainage systems, flood defences, and heat-resistant materials into road and rail upgrade and maintenance programmes could prevent route closures and delays currently caused by weather events. By proactively integrating climate risk assessments into the planning and design stages of public transport associated with port infrastructure, like Copenhagen Metro, risks to staff and passengers using transport links due to rising sea levels or extreme weather can be managed.
sections.financials: Funding Funding for the adaptation measures was sourced from multiple avenues, including the Danish government, the City of Copenhagen, and the Copenhagen Metro’s budget. Additional funding came from EU climate adaptation grants and loans. Initial costs The initial costs of integrating climate adaptation measures into the Copenhagen Metro were estimated to be €30 million. It was included in the overall cost of the metro extension project, which was approximately €3 billion. These costs covered enhancements to flood protection, including the installation of water barriers, improved drainage systems, and the reinforcement of underground structures to withstand heavier rainfall and potential flooding. Ongoing costs The primary maintenance tasks involve ensuring the drainage systems and water barriers are functioning correctly, which will be incorporated into the metro's routine maintenance cycle. These costs are expected to be small relative to the total operation and maintenance budget of the Copenhagen Metro, which also covers train operations and station upkeep. Avoided costs The avoided costs are difficult to estimate precisely, but significant savings are expected in terms of avoiding flood damage to metro assets and infrastructure. The flooding event is projected to have 5% chance of happening in the metro life (100 years). In the event of extreme rainfall, the costs of repairing flood-damaged stations, tracks, and trains would be substantial. By investing in proactive measures like waterproofing, flood barriers, and enhanced drainage, Copenhagen Metro has effectively mitigated these risks. Economic impacts The economic impact of these adaptation measures has been overwhelmingly positive. Although there is no detailed breakdown of economic impact figures, the ability to maintain transport services without major disruption results in long-term cost savings and operational stability. Additionally, this resilience contributes to the broader attractiveness of Copenhagen as a climate- adaptive city boosting its reputation for innovation and sustainability.
sections.resourcing: The project relied on a collaborative approach involving multiple stakeholders, including public authorities, urban planners, engineering firms, and climate adaptation specialists. The Danish government and local municipal bodies played a significant role in project funding and oversight. External contractors and consultants with expertise in flood protection and infrastructure resilience were engaged to design and implement the adaptation measures.
sections.co_benefits: The adaptation measures deliver several co-benefits in addition to mitigating flood risks. One key advantage is the enhanced safety and reliability of the metro system, ensuring uninterrupted service even during extreme weather. This reliability strengthens public trust in the system, encouraging greater use of public transport and supporting Copenhagen’s broader sustainability goals by reducing reliance on private vehicles. Additionally, the measures minimise the need for emergency repairs and maintenance, indirectly benefiting the environment by reducing construction-related disruption and interventions. While the project did not explicitly include nature-based solutions, its focus on engineered adaptations highlights how urban infrastructure can deliver broader societal benefits.
sections.evaluation: The effectiveness of the adaptation measures is assessed on the metro’s ability to operate without disruption during extreme weather events. Success is primarily measured by the avoidance of flooding, reduced service interruptions, and passenger safety during adverse conditions. Ongoing monitoring of water levels, infrastructure integrity, and drainage system performance ensures the continued success of these measures. The metro’s performance during past and future weather events serves as a practical benchmark for measuring the success of these adaptations. Based on the city's experience and the anticipated benefits, the number of incidents like those seen in the 2011 cloudburst, which caused severe disruptions, will likely be minimised, as these were the events that directly influenced the implementation of the adaptation measures.
sections.challenges: The project faced several challenges, including integrating flood protection measures into the metro’s existing technical and operational framework. Designing elevated entrances and reconfiguring drainage systems required careful coordination with urban planners to ensure functionality and compliance with city regulations. Ensuring flexibility in the design to account for updated climate projections also posed a challenge.
sections.lessons_learned: Key lessons learned include the importance of incorporating climate adaptation measures during the early design phase thereby avoiding the higher cost and disruptions associated with retrofitting. Additionally, the project underscored the need for multidisciplinary collaboration, involving engineers, urban planners, climate scientists, and policymakers to create effective and resilient solutions.
sections.innovation_opportunities: Copenhagen has taken a wide approach to the mitigation of flood risks caused by cloudbursts and rising sea level which has seen the construction of new and enhancement of existing infrastructure across the city. This approach is innovative as both the risk and potential solutions are treated in a more holistic rather than project specific way.

organisation: New locks in the Albert canal in Flanders, Belgium
measure_title: • Archimedes screws are hydraulic machines that consist of a screw inside a hollow shaft
measure_description: • Archimedes screws are hydraulic machines that consist of a screw inside a hollow shaft

sections.challenge: • The Albert Canal is an 80 mile long waterway that connects the harbour in Antwerp with the industrial zones in Liège in Belgium . T • he canal is an important economic waterway and transports 40 million tonnes of goods per year via containers 2,8,9 ; however, the canal becomes less suitable for commercial shipping during periods of low water flow from its source, the River Meuse 3,6 . • The Albert Canal experiences fluctuating water levels due to alternating periods of heavy rainfall and extended periods of drought 4,11 , which is expected to be exacerbated in future by climate change 1,6 and there is uncertainty about the level of ongoing water supply from the River Meuse 5,14 . • In recent years Flanders has experienced prolonged droughts that have increased the strain on its water supply and canal system 11 . • Archimedes screws were installed along the Albert Canal to avoid economic losses from reduced traffic due to reduced water availability and low river discharge 2,3,6 .
sections.adaptation_measures: • Archimedes screws are hydraulic machines that consist of a screw inside a hollow shaft . They are designed to raise water up through the shaft and are often powered by motors . • During periods of drought, the screws pump water back up the canal that is lost due to a ship passing through a lock . The screws were designed to ensure the safe navigation of ships through the canal during droughts by maintaining water levels 4,9 . • The screws were also designed to generate renewable energy in times of high water levels, mainly in winter from the fall of excess water from the canal , . • The unique design combines a pumping plant and hydroelectric power plant 3,9 to reduce the visual impact and ensure cost efficiencies 10 . • Installation of the screws began in 2012 at the Ham lock system , and these are one of the largest Archimedes screws in the world 11 . Organisation De Vlaamse Waterweg (Flemish Waterways) Transport sub-sector Ports, coastal and offshore First order climate hazard Drought Second order climate hazard N/A Transport asset impacted Canal Adaptation measures Archimedes screws installed in pumping and hydroelectric power stations Location  Antwerp to Liège, Belgium Years of delivery 2012 - 2024 • Archimedes screws were installed at six lock systems along the Albert Canal . • The installation of the screws was preceded by extensive engagement with stakeholders including shipping representatives, power companies, nature protection organisations, and drinking water supply companies . The Archimedes screws were identified as the preferred option after the effectiveness and costs were analysed and environmental considerations were assessed, particularly the preservation of fish migration and noise mitigation (see the co- benefits section below for further detail). The cost-benefit analysis considered a 40-year lifetime .
sections.applicability: The technology and approach to this adaptation measure would be applicable to the UK, however we typically have a lot less commercial movement of freight via our canal system, especially in comparison to European waterways such as the Albert Canal. In 2020 there were 24.7 billion tonne-kilometres of goods moved by water in the UK 15 . In comparison, there were 7.6 billion tonne- kilometres of goods shipped along Belgian waterways in 2022 16 . The value of using this measure may become more apparent on a case by case basis as climate change decreases the quantity of summer rainfall the UK receives, especially in central and the SE of England; however, it may still be of limited overall value should commercial waterways traffic remain relatively low. When thinking about the broader context of climate change in the UK, the technology could potentially provide combined transport and water resources benefits where cross-catchment transfer of water via rivers and canals is being considered as part of long term regional water supply strategies, for example along the Grand Union Canal 17 . The combination of hydroelectric generation with this adaptation measure would be appliable to the UK, should this approach be considered commercially beneficial for waterways.
sections.financials: Funding Information not publicly available. Initial costs It cost €7 million to €7.5 million to install the screws at each lock system 2,3,10,11 . Ongoing costs Information not publicly available. Avoided costs Information not publicly available. Economic impacts Information not publicly available.
sections.resourcing: • De Vlaamse Waterweg (Flemish Waterways) are responsible for the maintenance, operation, management, and commercialisation of inland waterways in the Flemish Region 12 . • Flemish Waterways is the hydropower operator along the Albert Canal and it collaborated with other organisations, such as Tractebel Engie, for the design and construction of the screws 10 . • The combined pumping and hydropower plant was built in Genk by Temporary Trade Association Eureka and the propellers were built by Vandezande company from Diksmuide 10 .
sections.co_benefits: There were four main co-benefits from the installation of the screws: 1. By maintaining safe water levels for navigation, the screws support the safe passage of ships through the canal when water levels are low . This helps to maintain the economic benefits from the transport of over 40 million tonnes of goods per year via inland shipping and prevents traffic congestion in the canal . 2. The screws limit water consumption as water that is used for the operation of the locks is pumped back to the upper section of the canal, minimising water loss during droughts . 3. When water levels are high, the screws can pump 5m of water per second, generating enough hydroelectricity for between 1,000-1,500 households 3,9,14 . 4. The screws installed at Ham were designed to be closed Archimedes screws, rather than open, meaning the housing is attached to the blades . This design prevents the rich fish stocks in the canal from being squeezed between the blades and allows them to freely migrate which has biodiversity benefits for local and migrating fish 2,3,5,6 .
sections.evaluation: Four screws were initially installed at Ham in 2012, and a further three were installed in Olen in 2012 . After their functionality and effectiveness had been confirmed, the installation of the screws continued along the Albert Canal’s locks in 2018 . Monitoring of the screws in the years after their installation identified that the screws function as an electricity generator for 10 months of the year, and that the screws generate more power than they use . The combined maximum power output for each of the screw facilities is 1.2MW . The screws generate 3 million kWh of electricity each year, which is equivalent to the annual electricity consumption of 1,000 families . In 2018, the EU-funded Fishfriendly Innovative Technologies for Hydropower (FIThydro) project evaluated the impact of the Archimedes screws on fish . The study found that around 13% of wild migrating fish were badly injured when passing through the screws, and 3% of downstream migrating eels were heavily injured or died at the site in Ham .
sections.challenges: The size (4.3m diameter and 28m long) and weight (85 to 95 tonnes) of the screws posed a challenge for the installation process, as did the proximity to the lock systems 3,4 . To address these challenges large cranes were required to position the screws in their channels .
sections.lessons_learned: Information not publicly available.
sections.innovation_opportunities: The system could be refined to reduce damage to migrating fish and eels.

measure_title: Slope stabilisation, rockfall barriers, early warning systems
case_study_text: Comprehensive physical and predictive technology adaptations across the Alpine rail network. Site-specific assessment more effective than blanket solutions.

sections.challenge: • Austrian Federal Railways (ÖBB) implemented climate adaptation measures to address the risks posed by hazards such as landslides, rockfalls, floods, and avalanches (5) . • These hazards, likely to be exacerbated by climate changes to precipitation patterns and increasing seasonal temperatures, threaten critical railway assets, including tracks, tunnels, and bridges. • The mountainous terrain in Austria makes the railway network particularly vulnerable, with events like heavy precipitation, freeze-thaw cycles and snowmelt increasing the likelihood of slope instability and flooding. • These hazards disrupt rail services, causing delays, damage and safety risks, while also impacting local communities that rely on the railway for commuting and freight transport (4) .
sections.applicability: • Parts of the UK rail network face similar hazards and are affected by slope and earthworks embankment and cutting instability. Roads in upland areas can additionally be vulnerable to landslips, especially where the geology can be unstable, for example in the Peak District. • Some of the adaptation methods employed by ÖBB could be effectively deployed in the UK, e.g. rockfall protection systems, drainage management and landslip prevention.
sections.adaptation_measures: Measure(s) implemented Physical adaptations: • To mitigate landslide and rockfall risks the following measures were employed: o slope stabilisation o rockfall barriers o protective nets o reinforced embankments Organisation Austrian Federal Railways (ÖBB) Transport sub-sector Railways First order climate hazards (cause) Heavy rainfall; Fluvial flooding; Storms and high winds Second order climate hazards (effect) Rockfalls, landslides, avalanches; Water damage Transport assets impacted Track, signalling and telecommunications; Bridges, culverts; Earthworks; Level crossings Adaptation measures Slope stabilisation; Rockfall barriers; Flood defences; Drainage systems; Early warning systems; Real-time monitoring Location  Alpine regions, Austria Year of delivery 2005 - present • Installation of improved drainage systems and flood retention basins to manage water runoff during extreme precipitation events. • Between 2022 and 2023, ÖBB raised the length of rockfall and avalanche barriers installed from 204 to 212 km and delivered 0.3 km of new torrent control barriers bringing the total installed length to 2.1 km (5) . • A functional and stable protective forest is an important safeguarding measure protecting infrastructure against landslides, mudslides or avalanches. ÖBB focuses on preserving the area of managed protected forest, which in the period 2021 to 2023 remained constant at 3,370 hectares (1)(5) . Predictive technology adaptations: • Early warning systems and real-time monitoring technologies, such as geotechnical sensors and weather forecasting tools, are used to predict hazards and allow timely interventions (5) . • By combining meteorological data and physical references, hazard corridors have been identified where the probability of a weather related damage event can be estimated. This enables future preventative actions to be taken (1) . Other options assessed • ÖBB assed the use of both physical adaptations and predictive technologies (5) . • The chosen measures were prioritised over others due to their ability to address the specific hazards of Austria’s Alpine region and their proven effectiveness in mitigating risks (1) . • Measures were chosen on a site-specific basis after considering local conditions, damage assessments from previous events and an analysis of vulnerable railway assets (5) .
sections.financials: Funding • Funding for the adaptation measures came primarily from ÖBB’s annual infrastructure budget, supported by national government allocations for public transport and infrastructure resilience (2) . West Virginia train derailment: Freight train strikes rockslide, spills diesel into river Slope stabilisation for road or rail embankments • Additional financial support was secured through European Union funding programs, such as the Connecting Europe Facility (CEF) and EU Structural Funds, which prioritise investments in climate-resilient infrastructure (3) . Initial costs • Exact costs are not publicly available. However, ÖBB’s infrastructure investment budget, which exceeds €3 billion pa, includes allocations for climate adaptation projects (2) . Ongoing costs • The ongoing costs associated with these interventions is not publicly available. • ÖBB’s 2023 annual operations and maintenance budget totalled €21.1 billion (2) . Avoided costs • Avoided costs are not publicly available. Economic impacts • Economic impacts are not publicly available.
sections.resourcing: • The project involved multiple stakeholders, including ÖBB, local governments, environmental agencies, and engineering firms. • Partnerships with research institutions and universities contributed expertise in hazard modelling and climate projections (4) . • External contractors were engaged to construct protective barriers and implement monitoring technologies. • ÖBB’s internal teams managed the coordination and oversight of the adaptation projects.
sections.co_benefits: • Improved safety for passengers, workforce and freight services (4) . • Enhanced reliability of the railway network. • Reduced economic losses from service disruption and asset repairs. • In 2023, regulations were updated to contain a new provision stating that precipitation water should primarily be infiltrated on site before it is discharged into the sewerage system. As a result, the water is fed directly into the natural water cycle. Numerous studies and observations have shown that the railway’s various retention and infiltration basins are important wetland habitats in the landscape (5) .
sections.evaluation: • The success of the adaptation measures is evaluated based on their ability to prevent disruption during hazard events (5) . • There is no publicly available data on the performance of the adaptations.
sections.challenges: • Delivering adaptations in a challenging physical environment. • Identifying the exact scale of future climate impacts was difficult, adding uncertainty to the planning process. • Integrating new adaptation measures into the existing infrastructure while maintaining ongoing operations was a logistical challenge (5) . • Balancing the level of initial capital investment against long-term benefits.
sections.lessons_learned: • Proactive measures are important for operational resilience e.g. removal of trees with a high risk of toppling onto the railway in severe weather and their replacement with suitable species (5) . • Collaboration between stakeholders, such as government bodies, construction firms, and regional authorities was essential for effective implementation. • Real-time monitoring proved invaluable for detecting hazards early, and flexible solutions were necessary to adapt to evolving climate risks (5) . • Securing EU and national funding was critical in covering the high costs of these adaptation measures, highlighting the importance of leveraging available public financial support (3) .
sections.innovation_opportunities: • There are opportunities to extend remote condition-based monitoring of infrastructure and, in the case of rockfall prediction, ground stability monitoring. This could be achieved through the greater use of sensors embedded in high risk areas monitored through telematics. • Systems for ground movement monitoring can also be valuable for areas prone to landslip, both in early warning but also in their use alongside rainfall data and earthworks condition surveys to better refine understanding of the linkages and potential future risk levels under changing rainfall patterns.

hazard_cause: Sea level rise, Heavy rainfall, Storms and high winds

sections.challenge: • MTA's transportation network in New York City, encompassing subways, buses, railroads, bridges and tunnels faces significant risks from climate hazards such as sea level rise, coastal storm surges, extreme winds, rising temperatures and heavy precipitation. • The devastation caused by Superstorm Sandy in 2012, which inflicted approximately USD$5 billion (£3.15bn) in damage, underscored the vulnerabilities of MTA’s transit infrastructure (1) . • Key assets suffered severe impacts, including corrosion, electrical and mechanical system failures leading to prolonged service disruption. • In response, MTA prioritised flood resilience and climate adaptation measures to safeguard its infrastructure.
sections.adaptation_measures: Measures implemented • MTA has implemented a range of adaptation measures to safeguard its transport network from climate hazards, including flood protection systems such as flex gates, marine doors, and over 3,000 flood barriers installed at subway stations and fan plants (1) . o Flex gates – A high strength rugged textile sheet that is deployed horizontally across station entrances like a shutter or hatch. The device can be installed by 1 or 2 people in less than 15 minutes (9) . o Marine door – metal doors sealed by inflatable gaskets to prevent water ingress. • Additional measures include elevating critical equipment, waterproofing facilities, enhancing drainage systems, installing resilient cabling and reinforcing pump systems. • Emergency generators, under-river tunnel rehabilitation, and flood walls around key areas have also been deployed. Organisation Metropolitan Transportation Authority (MTA) - New York City Transit Transport sub-sector Rail First order climate hazards (cause) Sea level rise; Heavy rainfall; Storms and high winds; Coastal flooding; High temperatures Second order climate hazards (effect) Water damage; Storm damage Transport assets impacted Track; Signalling and telecommunications; Structures (subway stations) Adaptation measures Installation of flood protection devices; Rehabilitation of under-river tunnels; Hardening of critical facilities; Elevating of critical equipment Location  New York City, USA Years of delivery 2012 - Present • Equipment, such as flood barrier logs (stop logs) and panels, designed to protect assets from damage from storm surges, heavy rains, and flooding was installed at the Clifton Shop and St. Goerge Terminal as part of the MTA’s remediation efforts (1) . o Stop logs and panels – deployable metal logs or panels which are installed vertically in entrance ways to protect against water intrusion, especially where it is not feasible to install swinging or sliding doors. • For St. George Terminal, two major repair and upgrade contracts were carried out in 2014 and 2019 to address climate challenges such as hurricanes, rain, and extreme heat: T-80276 Contract (St. George, 2014) (10) : o Constructed a new signal room elevated 12 feet above ground to withstand a Category 2 hurricane and 3 feet of additional flood protection. o Upgraded drainage systems to improve resilience. o Raised relay rooms by 1 foot and sealed relay room (which contains all the track system and switch system) computers with waterproof materials to combat against flooding. T-80279 Contract (St, George 2019) (10) : Focused on flood protection against storm surges from hurricanes classified as SLOSH (Sea, Lake, and Overland Surges from Hurricanes) Category 2 and 3: o Installed flood walls, deployable flood barriers, and gates with a barrier top elevation of +20.6 feet above ground level. o Built a flood wall spanning 1,111 feet in total length. o Extended sanitary sewers by 365 feet and storm sewers by 225 feet in length. Other options assessed • The federal government made the decision on which adaptation measures the MTA should pursue (10) . • Supply chain constraints limited the adaptation solutions available (10) . • No further information was available from the interviewee.
sections.applicability: This case study is of primary relevance to the rail and light rapid transit sectors in the UK, as well as to organisations that oversee large, interfaced transport networks, particularly where those networks are in built up urban settings. The adaptation measures implemented as part of MTA’s strategies, such as the different types of flood protection chosen to provide location and vulnerability specific protection are broadly applicable to transport sector organisations in the UK that are at risk from climate change impacts including increased flooding, high winds and high temperatures.
sections.financials: Funding • Both St. George contracts were entirely funded by the MTA. • Following Superstorm Sandy, the MTA received USD$886.3 million (£558m) from the Federal Transit Administration (FTA) for storm-related repairs. • The Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) contributed an additional USD$329 million (£208m) for repairs and USD$74.5 million (£47m) for hazard mitigation (5,6) . • During the period 2020-2024, MTA’s USD$51 billion (£37bn) Capital Program was funded through various sources: USD$6 billion (£4.4bn) from New York State and City (USD$3 billion (£2.2bn) each), USD$10.5 billion (£7.6bn) in federal funding, USD$25 billion (£18.2bn) from revenue-generated capital and USD$9.8 billion(£7.1bn) through Pay-as-You-Go (PAYGO) bonds (7) . • While funding for specific adaptation measures isn’t specified, these financial resources underpin the MTA’s recovery and resilience efforts. Initial costs The St. George terminal: • In 2012, an initial allocation of approximately USD$3 billion (£1.9bn) was dedicated to resiliency projects, including the installation of flood barriers, elevation of critical equipment and waterproofing of facilities (2) . • The T-80276 contract cost USD$100.86 million (£61.3m) (10) . • The T-80279 contract cost USD$57.02 million (£44.6m) (10) . • In 2021, MTA committed nearly USD$8 billion (£5.8bn) to flood resilience capital improvements. This includes installing over 3,000 flood protection devices at 31 stations, rehabilitating 11 under-river tunnels, constructing flood walls around subway yards, hardening critical facilities and elevating essential equipment (1) . Ongoing Costs • Information not available in the public domain. Avoided Costs • While MTA has not disclosed specific figures for avoided costs, these adaptation measures are expected to significantly reduce the risk of future damage to infrastructure and equipment, such as the approximately USD$5 billion (£3.15bn) in damage caused by Superstorm Sandy in 2012. • By mitigating the impacts of extreme weather events, MTA anticipates avoiding substantial repair and replacement expenses over the long term.
sections.resourcing: (2) Collaborative effort involving: • MTA Construction and Development • New York City Transit • Staten Island Railway Partnerships with federal and city agencies include: • Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) • NYC Emergency Management • The Mayor's Office • New York City Department of Transport
sections.co_benefits: • Environmental: 12,000 linear feet of track and 7,500 wooden rail ties were upgraded with high- density plastic ties made entirely from 100% recycled materials (10) . • Economic: MTA's infrastructure is an integral part of the region's economy and economic growth, with 1.58 billion train rides taken in NYC in 2023 (10) . In 2023, NYC’s contribution to the US GDP settled at around 4.0% (8) .
sections.evaluation: Success metrics: • Reduced service interruptions • Fewer repairs after storms • Overall improvements in operational resilience • Performance during subsequent weather events, such as Hurricane Ida: o The quick restoration of transit services and the minimal infrastructure damage highlighted the effectiveness of measures like flood barriers and elevated equipment. • Audits - including one by the New York State Comptroller o Identified successes and areas for improvement, such as enhancing inspection documentation and activating operational plans more consistently during extreme weather (1) .
sections.challenges: (10) • Vendor limitations due to U.S. regulations requiring contractors to be domestic, reducing the pool of qualified suppliers. • Tunnel debris removal, particularly in flooded areas, which proved time-consuming and resource intensive. • Ballast replacement, complicated by the presence of naturally occurring asbestos, posing significant health risks to workers. • Maintaining train services during construction at the high-traffic St. George Terminal, which required careful coordination to minimise disruption.
sections.lessons_learned: • More precise coordination in material procurement, which should ideally be initiated earlier in the design phase to avoid delays. • Adapting to the use of new materials and equipment, such as updated signals and track types, required flexibility during the design and construction stages. • Outsourcing the design introduced additional complexities as many decisions during construction required the approval of the original designer. Streamlining this process or improving communication with design consultants could enhance efficiency in future projects (10) .
sections.innovation_opportunities: • There are opportunities for innovation in the development of new technologies and materials for flood protection devices, as well as the use of advanced data analytics and modelling to predict and respond to extreme weather events. • The integration of nature-based solutions and the use of the Enterprise Asset Management (EAM) system for asset management can also be further developed to enhance the resilience of transport infrastructure (1) .

hazard_cause: Sea level rise, Storms and high winds, Coastal
measure_title: In 2007, the Government of the Cook Islands developed a 20-year Preventative Infrastructure
measure_description: In 2007, the Government of the Cook Islands developed a 20-year Preventative Infrastructure

sections.challenge: • The Cook Islands are highly vulnerable to tropical cyclones, which bring storm surges and high winds that seriously deteriorate the structural integrity of coastal infrastructure . • Sea levels are also rising, having risen 14cm on Rarotonga, the Cook Islands’ most populated island, from 1993 to 2024, which has significantly increased the island’s risk of coastal flooding . By the end of the century, the Cook Islands are likely to experience over 100 days of flooding annually . • Avatui Port, a 260m long facility, handles 90% of Rarotonga’s food imports and all its fuel supply . More severe and frequent climatic events had been increasing the likelihood of catastrophic damage to the port, which would have left the island population’s supply of food at risk . • To adapt to these increasing hazards, the wharf has been reinforced with updated infrastructure that is more resilient to waves and erosion, and designed with the capacity to be raised as sea- levels continue to rise.
sections.adaptation_measures: In 2007, the Government of the Cook Islands developed a 20-year Preventative Infrastructure Master Plan (IMP) which identified Avatui Port as at risk of catastrophic failure . Due to existing structural flaws in the port (such as gaps in the wharf face, resulting in sea water intruding under the wharf deck), significant and regular repairs were required to keep the port serviceable . In 2007, it was confirmed that issues with the wharf’s structural integrity had resulted in serious deterioration of reinforcing steel . This left the port highly vulnerable to further deterioration from wave action, erosion, and flooding from storm surges. While a regular maintenance programme was initially costed, the Cook Islands Ports Authority (CIPA) wanted to implement a flexible and futureproofed solution that would minimise disruption. As a result, it opted for a series of refurbishments with a 50-year design life , including: • A realignment of the wharf deck to increase mooring bollard strength. This enhanced the ability for larger vessels to moor at the wharf without risking further structural damage, particularly during poor weather conditions. Organisation Cook Islands Ports Authority (CIPA) Transport sub-sector Ports, coastal and offshore First order climate hazard (cause) Sea level rise; Storms and high winds; Coastal flooding Second order climate hazards (effect) Water damage; Coastal erosion Transport assets impacted Port structures; Port services Adaptation measures Wharf height increase; Rock armour Location  Cook Islands Years of delivery 2009 - 2014 • Rock armour was utilised to extend the western breakwater by 92m . This dissipates waves and protects fishing and tourist boats moored in the port’s western marina from erosion and storm surges. • Raising the main berth by 0.5 metres to adapt the wharf to rising sea levels. Immediate relief from sea level rise was supplemented by the structure having flexible adaptation of a further 0.5 metres. • Designing the anchoring points for the fendering system with significant flexibility, so that fenders could be set to a higher level where required with rising sea levels. Additional improvement works were conducted to increase the harbour’s capacity, such as widening the harbour to 10m and dredging to 7m, to increase the turning space for large vessels. Cargo storage sheds were also rebuilt and their overall storage capacity increased. Other options assessed The strategic importance of Avatiu port was included within the IMP, which identified that a lack of investment into maintaining key infrastructure may inhibit the Cook Islands economic growth . While an initial investment of NZ$3.5m (equivalent to GBP 1.6m based on the average 2015 exchange rate) was suggested to conduct periodic maintenance on the wharf, investing in improving resilience in the longer-term was recognised as a more effective means to secure private-sector economic growth, particularly as this could be delivered at the same time as increasing overall port capacity .
sections.financials: Funding • The ADB were engaged by the Government of the Cook Islands to finance most of the project. At completion, ADB loans totalled US$ 20.71m (equivalent to GBP 13.55m based on the average 2015 exchange rate) 10 , which made up 84% of the total project cost . • Supplementary funding was provided by the Government of the Cook Islands, which totalled US$ 3.91m (equivalent to GBP 2.56m based on the average 2015 exchange rate) 10 . Initial costs • The total cost of the project totalled US $24.62m , equivalent to GBP 16.11m 10 . Te Kukupa patrol boat at Port of Avatiu, Rarotonga, Cook Islands Cyclone swept coconut trees, Rarotonga, Cook Islands • Port improvement and civil works made up 92% of the total project cost, at US$ 23.01m (equivalent to GBP 14.35m based on the average 2015 exchange rate) 10 . • Consulting services made up US$ 1.42 million (equivalent to GBP 0.885m based on the average 2011 exchange rate) 10 . Ongoing costs Ongoing costs are not known. However, to keep the project operational over its 50-year design life, the ADB recommended that maintenance of the port should continue to be a strategic priority of the CIPA. Avoided costs By pursuing a long-term redevelopment, projected emergency repair costs of NZ$ 20.06m (equivalent to GBP 9.89m based on the average 2015 exchange rate) 4,9 were avoided for the period 2009-2036 . Economic impacts As a result of the project, Avatiu experienced an increase in traffic volumes of 5.6% from 2012- 2013 and 5.9% from 2013-2014 . Port revenues are projected to increase by 2.5% year-on-year from 2014 onwards, leading to shared economic benefits with local communities .
sections.resourcing: Due to the scale and complexity of the project, the CIPA oversaw the development, and procured a consultancy to deliver project management, procurement, and environmental and social monitoring services. McConnell Dowell was employed to conduct the civil works .
sections.co_benefits: • Improving the overall resilience of Avatui Port has enabled food to continue to be delivered as the climate changes, which enhanced food security for the 13,000 people who lived on Rarotonga in 2016 11 . • The project was highly circular and followed socially sustainable principles. 167,000 cubic metres of material was dredged during the project of which 10% was used for backfilling onsite. • 60,000m of material was used to reclaim land around the Punanga Nui Market, which is a key attraction for Raratonga’s local tourists. Any remaining material was made available at no extra cost to local communities for use as filling on construction projects .
sections.evaluation: As part of the Project Completion Review Mission (PCRM) in 2015, the ADB assessed the overall sustainability of the new wharf . An assessment of the port also identified that it was structurally sustainable, with any initial wear on the port caused by concrete shrinkage, rather than resulting from anything structurally unsound. The CIPA is continuing to monitor any cosmetic or structural changes and undertake routine maintenance, so that the port is operational over its 50-year lifecycle.
sections.challenges: The project faced limited challenges from an implementation perspective, with the project completed just 4-months later than initial estimates. However, several investment-related challenges were identified during evaluation. As exchange rates between US$ and NZ$ were underestimated, 55% of supplementary financing was attributed to exchange rate changes .
sections.lessons_learned: Ensuring effective time is taken to assess costs and collate data in regions with lower administrative capacity, like the Cook Islands, should be a key consideration for adaptation projects financed across international borders.
sections.applicability: Many port projects in the UK already consider sea level rise when appraising major upgrades or repairs (for example, Associated British Ports’ portfolio 12 ). However, UK-based ports could learn from the adaptive capacity embedded within Avatiu Port, which will enable the CIPA to increase the port’s height as sea levels increase.
sections.innovation_opportunities: The port redevelopment relied heavily on hard engineering and processes, such as dredging and rock armour. This project could be made more sustainable for marine life by considering the use of soft engineering, such as protecting existing coral reefs and creating artificial ones to reduce wave energy and protect against coastal erosion.

hazard_cause: Heavy rainfall, High temperatures, Low temperatures

sections.challenge: • Rail infrastructure faces significant challenges due to climate-related hazards, including rising temperatures and heavy rainfall. These events accelerate the deterioration and premature ageing of rail systems, leading to increased maintenance requirements, renewal works, and crisis management - all of which impose considerable financial burdens. • High temperatures can cause track deformation, such as bending and buckling, and disrupt electrical systems, including signalling and electric traction. • Critical infrastructure elements like tracks, bridges, earthworks, and hydraulic structures are particularly susceptible to damage from heavy rainfall. • To address these challenges, Infrabel, the owner and maintainer of the Belgian railway network, has implemented a range of climate adaptation measures designed to mitigate damage and enhance the resilience of their rail infrastructure.
sections.adaptation_measures: To mitigate the effects of rising temperatures, Infrabel has implemented a series of targeted measures, to collectively enhance the resilience of rail infrastructure to climate-related challenges. These include: • Installing tension release devices to prevent rail tracks from buckling during high temperatures or fracturing during low temperatures. • Seasonal quality control of track beds, along with inspections of joints and switches, to maintain infrastructure integrity. • Deployed weather stations to monitor rail temperatures in real time, sending automated alerts to maintenance teams when temperatures exceed critical thresholds. These stations also manage automatic point heating systems, which prevent switch rails from freezing and reduce snow accumulation. • Identified flood-prone areas and constructed storm basins to mitigate potential flooding. • Conducting comprehensive assessments of railway embankment stability under flood conditions. Organisation Infrabel Transport sub-sector Railways First order climate hazards (cause) Heavy rainfall; High temperatures; Low temperatures; Surface water flooding Second order climate hazard (effect) None identified Transport asset impacted Track Adaptation measures Tension release devices; Storm basins Location  Belgium Year of delivery 2022 • Evaluating the resilience of bridge abutments against river erosion. Typically existing abutments date back to the 19 th century, however many of them are yet to be replaced as there are debates as to who is responsible for these repairs.
sections.applicability: By fostering open communication with local maintenance teams and gradually introducing changes, Infrabel has ensured the seamless integration of new systems while maintaining a strong focus on employee expertise. This approach broadly replicates the approaches that UK transport subsectors take, and may be able to add lessons learnt from tested methods and alternate ways of monitoring and responding to long term climate changes and extreme weather events. In addition to be comparable (with potentially transferable approaches) to rail owners, the approaches may be applicable to other transport sectors including highways and inland waterways that have infrastructure of a similar age. The company’s dynamic approach to problem-solving, supported by data-driven insights, has enabled it to respond effectively to evolving challenges, such as climate impacts and the transition to digital infrastructure. This adaptability not only supports current initiatives but also lays a solid foundation for embracing future advancements, including predictive maintenance and enhanced energy efficiency.
sections.financials: Funding Not publicly available. Initial costs Not publicly available. Ongoing costs Not publicly available. Avoided costs Infrabel has brought in these adaptation measures as a response to the effects of rising temperatures and heavy rainfall. As a result of these events, there has been an increase in the rate of observed damage to railway infrastructure, leading to increased financial burdens to accommodate for the additional maintenance and repair requirements. Through implementing these measures, the resilience of the railway should be improved, therefore potentially reducing future maintenance and repair costs, for example the potential to save the company up to €2 million during a harsh winter.
sections.resourcing: Infrabel recognised the importance of implementing changes while maintaining close communication with local maintenance teams. This approach ensured that their expertise and on- the-ground knowledge, enhanced by data-driven insights, played a central role throughout the railway digitisation process.
sections.co_benefits: Infrabel's intelligent system for automatically managing point heating is expected to reduce energy consumption by an additional 20–30%, reinforcing the company's commitment to both operational efficiency and environmental responsibility. By implementing weather stations, Infrabel has optimised the heating of rail infrastructure during the winter, avoiding unnecessary energy usage.
sections.evaluation: Infrabel has successfully demonstrated the effectiveness of its weather stations, particularly during the coldest week of the winter of 2022–2023. The data collected from these stations was instrumental in controlling automatic point heating, resulting in an estimated energy saving of 3.2 GWh and a cost reduction of approximately €500,000.
sections.challenges: The success of the project has been attributed to positive collaboration with field teams and a dynamic, responsive implementation strategy. Although no challenges have been publicly identified, moving forward, the challenge lies in advancing this digital transformation to develop predictive maintenance capabilities, further enhancing the efficiency and resilience of Infrabel’s railway infrastructure.
sections.lessons_learned: The installation of weather sensors and automatic point heating has been a key component of Infrabel’s railway digitisation project. This initiative has underscored the importance of fostering an employee mindset that is open to process changes and refining processes to ensure the seamless adoption of new systems. Infrabel emphasised the value of implementing changes gradually and maintaining ongoing consultation with the teams impacted by the project. This approach ensured that the project remained grounded in their expertise and practical knowledge.
sections.innovation_opportunities: • Infrabel has significant opportunities to drive innovation by advancing predictive maintenance systems with AI and machine learning, integrating IoT-enabled sensors for real-time monitoring, and leveraging digital twin technology for simulation and planning. • Enhancing energy efficiency through renewable solutions and optimised heating systems can further reduce costs and emissions. High speed passenger train on railroad track in motion Trains at busy train station peak hour • AI-powered weather analytics and dynamic resource allocation tools can improve resilience to extreme conditions, while sustainable materials and infrastructure upgrades can enhance durability. • Collaboration with external partners and the development of gamified training programs can support workforce adaptation and accelerate innovation.

hazard_cause: High temperatures, Storms and high winds
hazard_effect: Vegetation dieback, Storm damage, Track overheating
asset_type: Tracks, Trains, Overhead lines, Lineside vegetation
measure_title: Air-conditioned rolling stock, IoT sensors, vegetation management
measure_description: Phased adaptation with rolling stock, sensors and vegetation.
case_study_text: Phased adaptation combining air-conditioned rolling stock, AI-assisted vegetation management, and IoT sensor networks. Vegetation management delivered 25% reduction in storm damage.

sections.challenge: • Deutsche Bahn (DB), the national railway company of Germany, is implementing measures to adapt its passenger trains and tracks to increasing temperatures, increasing frequency and intensity of storms, and vegetation die back due to climate change. • DB commissioned a study by the Potsdam Institute for Climate Impact Research (PIK) into the expected impacts of climate changes in Germany on DB track infrastructure (1) . The study identified the potential for significantly more hot days and extreme weather conditions including an increase in the intensity of storms. • In response to these findings and the simultaneous expansion of rail transport in Germany, DB InfraGO's (DB Group's infrastructure division) natural hazard department identified areas to adapt to climate change: heat prevention and storm damage prevention (1) .
sections.applicability: • These adaptation measures could be applicable to rail carriages and rolling stock in the UK to reduce overheating and improve efficiency, pending evaluation by UK rail operators. • The best practice approaches in vegetation management should also be applicable to the UK; they would be most relevant to rail infrastructure but could be considered for road infrastructure as well. This would be particularly relevant in the South and East of England where temperatures are projected to reach higher peaks and drought conditions are projected to be more prolonged and severe.
sections.adaptation_measures: Measures implemented Air-conditioned passenger trains: • To mitigate the risks to passengers in extreme temperatures, Deutsche Bahn purchased the ICE 4 long-distance trains (2) . • The ICE 4 fleet was completed in 2024, comprising 137 trains (3) . Organisation Deutsche Bahn Transport sub-sector Railways First order climate hazard (cause) High temperatures Second order climate hazard (effect) Vegetation dieback; Storm damage Transport assets impacted Tracks; Trains Adaptation measures Air-conditioned trains; Vegetation management; Temperature sensors Location  Germany Years of delivery Phased implementation: 2007 - 2024 • ICE 4 provide a stable indoor climate even in high temperatures, with air-conditioning systems capable of coping with external ambient air temperatures of up to 45°C. These systems are tested at least every six months (2) . Sensor technology: • DB Group makes use of digital monitoring systems for predictive maintenance, allowing the detection of degradation and faults through real time monitoring (4) . • DB Group and its partners are currently testing use cases for implementing Internet of Things (IoT) sensor technology; environmental sensors are already available to Group partners through DB’s internal Digitalportal (4) . • The sensors allow continuous monitoring of environmental conditions such as ambient temperature, particle load and humidity, allowing preventative actions to be taken and reducing the risk of malfunctions due to environmental conditions (4) . Vegetation management: • DB Group has increased investment in vegetation management to mitigate the impacts of storms and falling trees on their infrastructure (5) . • Vegetation six metres either side of the tracks is cut back; vegetation beyond this pruning zone is also monitored and trees or plants that are susceptible to storm damage are cut back or removed (5) . • DB Group employs hundreds of track maintenance workers and foresters to inspect and maintain the forests near the tracks while remote sensing techniques are also used (5) . • Satellite data is processed via artificial intelligence to produce vegetation maps, which allow the identification of trees that are vulnerable to storm damage (5) . • Various technologies, such as a spider excavator, helicopters and flying saws, and remote- controlled mulchers, are deployed to address the risks identified (5) . • DB Group have launched the DB Climate Forest project in the Pfungstadt Forest to counteract the progressive forest dieback resulting from heat stress and climate change. A 500-hectare area, Pfungstadt Forest is being converted into a climate-stable DB climate forest by planting groups of site-appropriate native tree and shrub species. In 2022 and 2023, more than 26,000 deciduous trees were planted (6)(7) .
sections.financials: Funding • The source of funding is not stated explicitly in publicly available reports. Initial costs • DB Group invested €6 billion in the 137 ICE 4 trains (8) . • As part of the vegetation management programme, DB invested €625 million to thin out forests in a six-metre cutting zone on both sides of tracks, to reduce disruption from falling trees (9) . • The costs of temperature sensors are not specified in public reports; however, DB Group have reported that budget for preventative maintenance will increase by €2.6 billion (1) in the period 2024 to 2030. Ongoing costs • DB Group spend tens of millions of euros per year to guarantee the stability of air-conditioning systems in the long-distance trains (2) . The specific amount has not been disclosed to the public. • They have increased expenditure on vegetation measures from around €100 million to about €150 million per year since 2014, specifically to reduce acute physical risks (1) . Avoided costs • DB are currently working on detailing the financial impact of extreme weather in the context of major damage events (1) . Economic impacts • As detailed in their 2023 annual report, costs to DB from extreme weather include revenue losses and contractual penalties associated with damages to infrastructure, as well as capital expenditures for repairing damages and implementing preventative measures (1) . These financial impacts are not publicly available but are considered in mid-term planning (1) .
sections.resourcing: • DB Group worked with Siemens to deliver the ICE 4 fleet (8) . • The research into air-conditioning has been part of a collaboration between DB Systemtechnik and the German Aerospace Centre (DLR). This research collaboration started in 2020 and was extended for a further three years in June 2024 (3) . • To convert Pfungstadt Forest into the DB climate forest, the Hessen Ecoagency creates over 1000 plant nests annually in cooperation with the city of Pfungstadt, on behalf of DB Group (7) .
sections.co_benefits: Energy savings: • ICE 4 trains have 22% lower energy consumption per seat compared with the older ICE 1 (10) . Ecological benefits: • The vegetation management measures include habitat creation; when storm-susceptible trees are removed, the usable wood is sold while the remaining logs and branches are returned to the forest where they provide habitats for small animals (5) .
sections.evaluation: • Detailed information on how these measures have been evaluated is not publicly available; however, it has been reported that as a result of vegetation management, storm damage to tracks and overhead line equipment decreased by 25% between 2018 and 2020 (11) . • The use of ICE 4 trains appears to have reduced overheating risks; ICE 4 have been in regular operation since 2017 (12) , and between 2015 and 2020, heat-related disruptions fell by 20% and failures of heating, ventilation and air-conditioning (HVAC) systems on trains decreased significantly (9) . • The temperature sensors record whether measures taken in response to their readings were successful (4) . The rollout of these sensors is ongoing and, as such, data on their effectiveness is not yet available.
sections.challenges: DB Group saw a significant increase in maintenance costs in 2023, citing vegetation maintenance as one of the contributing factors (1) .
sections.lessons_learned: Despite the success of these measures, additional work is needed to improve track infrastructure which is outdated and prone to failure. This, combined with extreme weather events, which occurred more frequently than ever in the first half of 2024, has a significant impact on rail traffic. In June 2024, an average of 400 long-distance trains each day were affected by damage to infrastructure from external factors such as landslides and flooding (3) .
sections.innovation_opportunities: • This case study demonstrates best practice and innovation in rail car technologies. For example, in 2020, a regular ICE rail car was converted into a “Demonstrator Vehicle for Innovations in Passenger Comfort and Air-Conditioning,” for research into technologies that improve passenger comfort and reduce the energy requirements of air-conditioning (3) . These approaches could be rolled out more widely in rail infrastructure to improve passenger comfort during hot weather events. • Identification of vegetation at risk of dieback could incorporate train based AI systems that can identify diseases such as Ash dieback. As the climate changes, pests, diseases, fungi and invasive species may become more prevalent, or new causes of dieback may be introduced to an area. Monitoring via train-cab camera and AI systems would assist in identifying areas of vegetation for additional targeted management.
