measure_title: The LFAS combined Natural Flood Management (NFM) techniques with traditional engineering
measure_description: The LFAS combined Natural Flood Management (NFM) techniques with traditional engineering

sections.challenge: The area of Kirkstall Bridge in Leeds is regularly flooded by the River Aire (1) , as well as many other places across the catchment (14) . There is a 35% chance that disruptive flooding in this area will occur in any given year, which has caused significant disruption to the transport network serving West and North Yorkshire in recent years (1) . The Leeds Flood Alleviation Scheme (LFAS) was designed to reduce the risk of flooding to key transport infrastructure in Leeds, including the railway at Kirkstall which connects large parts of Yorkshire (5) . There has previously been regular disruption to rail travel across the region due to flooding (5) , particularly during Storm Eva in 2015 (3) and peak flows experienced during flood events in 2000 and 2007 (4) . The flood in 2015 occurred before the construction work was completed and resulted in 4,712 flooding incidents being reported across the city (4,5) .
sections.adaptation_measures: The LFAS combined Natural Flood Management (NFM) techniques with traditional engineering methods to reduce the risk of flooding in Leeds (2,9) . The LFAS provides a 1 in 200-year level of protection against extreme flooding for Leeds and its railway up to 2069 (1,2,4,5,8) . Traditional engineering The first phase of LFAS was completed in 2017 and involved the installation of moveable weirs, and the construction of flood walls and embankments over 4.5km from Leeds city centre to Woodlesford (3,4) . This project was the first in the UK to use moveable weirs to reduce flood risk (3) . The moveable weirs were designed to lower the river level if there is a risk of flooding (3) . The second phase of LFAS was directly relevant to the transport sector, with a business case based on protecting road and railway infrastructure in addition to homes and businesses (11) . It was completed in November 2024 and involved the building of defences 14km upstream along the River Aire, including new raised flood walls, embankments, structures to control water flow, and the removal of obstructions (3) . A new flood storage area was built at Calverley which can hold up to 1.8 million m of floodwater in the event of extreme rainfall and high river levels (3,5,11) . OrganisationLeeds City Council Transport sub-sectorRailways; Road pavement First order climate hazardsHeavy rainfall; Fluvial flooding Second order climate hazardWater damage Transport assets impactedBuildings and stations Adaptation measuresNatural Flood Management; Traditional engineering approaches Location Leeds, UK Years of delivery2014 - 2024 Natural Flood Management (NFM) The LFAS is the first NFM programme to be implemented at catchment scale in the UK (2) . In the upper catchment of the River Aire, over 500,000 trees were planted over 1,240 hectares (5,11) , and over 500 hectares of soil and land management was undertaken, including soil aeration and planting hedgerows and buffer strips (1) .
sections.applicability: This adaptation measure is applicable to other transport sectors that are vulnerable to flooding. It has been successful at a catchment scale as it has considered multiple interventions through the city centre and rural upper catchment. It would be applicable to catchment scale flood risk reduction as well as for individual transport infrastructure schemes, tailored to the level and type of flood risk, local topography and built environment, extent of risk exposure, land use options, budgets, and materials available. The moveable weirs could be applicable to other locations where fixed weirs have historically been used to maintain navigable waterways in normal flow conditions but contribute to flood risk in more extreme conditions (11) . Rail flooding following Storm Eva Railway flooding at Kirkstall following Storm Eva Railway flooding following Storm Eva Knostrop Weir and Foot and Cycle Bridge
sections.financials: Decision-making Planning permission for the moveable weirs in the first phase of LFAS was secured based on computer modelling of their effectiveness at reducing flood risk (3) . The business case for the second phase was bolstered by the completion of the first phase, and the 2015 flood during Storm Eva, which caused £500 million in recovery costs to the city region, including £37 million in direct damage costs to properties and infrastructure (4,5) . Funding Funding for the LFAS came from multiple schemes including government Grant in Aid funding, local funding provided by Leeds City Council, and over £20 million of regional funding provided by the West Yorkshire Combined Authority (5) . In the second phase, Network Rail contributed £1.4 million (13) to a partnership it formed with Leeds City Council (1) . In total the government provided over £165 million to Leeds through the Environment Agency to implement LFAS including additional tree planting that was undertaken in the Aire Valley (7) . Initial costs The LFAS required £200 million of investment (2,4,5) . The first phase of the scheme was a £50 million project and required multiple funding streams, including £35 million of government Grant in Aid funding and £10 million of local funding provided by Leeds City Council (6) . In phase one, £6 million was avoided through the reuse of 180,000 tonnes of material excavated at Knostrop Cut on a local development site (6) . Ongoing costs A not-for-profit company was formed to manage the long-term maintenance and monitoring of the NFM element of LFAS, namely the Aire Resilience Company, and it is primarily funded by the private sector (5,7) . Avoided costs The 2015 flood during Storm Eva caused £500 million in recovery costs to the city region, including £37 million in direct damage costs to properties and infrastructure (4,5) . Further information is not publicly available.
sections.resourcing: The first phase of LFAS was developed by Arup and focused on the river downstream of Leeds train station (4) . A joint venture between Mott MacDonald and Bam Nuttall (BMM) was awarded a £50 million design and build contract to implement the scheme (4) . This project was managed by Leeds City Council in partnership with the Environment Agency, White Rose Forest, and the Yorkshire Wildlife Trust (3,11) . In the second phase, Network Rail formed a partnership with Leeds City Council and contributed funding (1) . The NFM strategy was developed by Mott MacDonald, contributing an award winning digital tool to oversee the prioritisation of strategic locations for NFM and providing a single source of truth for multiple partners to deliver the NFM interventions (14) . This was informed by hydrological screening of the River Aire undertaken by Thomas Mackay Ltd (7) . Other organisations involved in the two phases of LFAS include AECOM, Bradford Council, Yorkshire Water, and the Yorkshire Wildlife Trust (3,4,5) .
sections.co_benefits: Improved water quality Improvements in water quality were mainly achieved through a Catchment Nutrient Balancing (CNB) approach (7) . CNB is regulated by the Environment Agency and enables water companies to pay the agricultural sector to implement measures to improve water quality beyond their regulatory fair share by removing phosphorus, to offset any impacts from Wastewater Treatment Works (7) . Biodiversity benefits The weirs in phase one were designed to include fish passes which removed the obstruction to fish migration created by the Victorian-era structures that were replaced as part of this project (4) . Enabled species such as otters and salmon to return to the River Aire for the first time in 200 years (3,7) . A 2.4-hectare wetland habitat was created on Kirkstall Meadows (5) . Over 5,000 trees were planted in phase two which provide important habitats for local wildlife and help to sequester carbon (9) . Carbon reduction Reduced height of downstream floodwalls by 100mm to 200mm over an 8km stretch (4,11) . Use of recycled materials and minimal transportation during the construction (3) . Use of nearby clay to construct the embankments in the new flood storage area saved over 5,000 lorry journeys (5) . Estimations that the LFAS will deliver a net carbon saving of over 6,500 tCO2e if it protected Leeds from a single 1 in 200-year flood event (8,10) . Community benefits NFM demonstration site was developed in Leeds to educate the wider public about hydrological processes and flood risk (14) . The weirs installed at Knostrop as part of phase one reconnected the Trans Pennine Trail to the north bank of the river through the construction of a new footbridge, enabling active transport into Leeds (6) and recreational benefits. 700 trees were planted along the Trans Pennine Trail and otter ramps and holts were installed, creating social and environmental co-benefits for local communities (6) . Economic benefits A significant economic benefit from the LFAS project was to bring land that was previously unsuitable for development back into potential use by reducing the level of flood risk (5) . Opening up this land to support the growth of the city is estimated to provide £774 million in regeneration benefits over 10 years and could support over 3,000 new jobs (5) .
sections.evaluation: Since it was finished in 2017, the weirs constructed in phase one have been activated to reduce peak water levels in the city centre nine times (5,11) . A 2024 report by the UK Government’s Department for Transport (DfT) estimated that the NFM element of the scheme would reduce peak flow by 5.6% in Leeds by the end of 2025 (1) . Phase one and Phase two of the scheme have been awarded a ‘Very Good’ and ‘Excellent’ rating respectively by the BREEAM science-based sustainability assessment, with maximum scores for the categories of community, stakeholders, resilience, and pollution (4,5,11,13) . Following the completion of the second phase of LFAS in 2024, it is estimated that the scheme will lead to regeneration and employment benefits (4) . Since 2017 these benefits have started to be realised, such as the developments around Wellington Street and Holbeck that have occurred since phase one was completed (5) .
sections.challenges: A challenge was to replace the existing weirs at Knostrop and Crown Point with moveable weirs, and to maintain the river flow throughout for navigation (4) . For example, it was necessary to construct temporary weirs to manage the risk of significantly reduced water levels that could cause environmental harm and damage to riverside structures (11) . In December 2015, an existing embankment was washed away and BMM’s cofferdam, an area pumped dry to facilitate construction work below the waterline, were flooded during Storm Eva. Damage to the construction site delayed the project by two months and added an additional six projects due to the required repairs (4) . The design and construction of phase two had to address many challenges resulting from the wide variety of conditions and constraints encountered along the scheme (11) . These ranged from co-ordination with major re-developments, listed structures at Armley Mills, buried structures associated with historic use of the river, and the agreement of defences with individual householders (11) . A zonal approach was followed in the design and environmental work required to discharge planning conditions (11) . This allowed construction work to commence on site early in the contract, whilst promoting the gathering of additional site information, buildability reviews and detailed design as parallel activities (11) . One of the challenges of this approach was the sequencing of the works and demonstrating to the Environment Agency that temporary flood risk was not temporarily increased at any time (11) . A significant engineering challenge was Apperley Lane Bridge at the upstream end of the scheme (11) . This historic masonry-arch bridge would be subject to increased flood loading during operation of the Calverley flow control structure (11) . It was therefore necessary to significantly strengthen the structure by threading concrete piles through the piers and abutments and constructing a new reinforced concrete deck and parapet (11) . These works were successfully achieved under a Listed Building Consent, leaving the bridge visually unaltered (11) .
sections.lessons_learned: Co-location in a project office, with key client, contractor, designer and technical advisor staff all intermingled, promoted a strong collaborative team that was able to continue to function when forced to separate during the COVID-19 lockdown (11) .
sections.innovation_opportunities: This scheme has included multiple innovation elements, including the moveable weirs and biodiversity enhancements both in Leeds city centre and in the upper catchment as part of the NFM measures. It has incorporated the innovation elements to fit within the public realm alongside built environment needs and heritage structures, and to complement rural land uses within the upper catchment using GIS catchment mapping and stakeholder engagement of options.

measure_description: • In 2016 Westminster City Council (WCC) set up the Marylebone Low Emission Neighbourhood

sections.challenge: • The Marylebone flyover faced climate hazards from heavy rainfall, particularly surface water flooding and overflowing of combined surface-foul drainage. These hazards had become more prevalent over time due to more frequent heavy rainfall events. • The Marylebone flyover is a 3.5-mile elevated dual carriageway section of the A40 in west London that was constructed between 1964 and 1970. • The stairs and ramps that were historically used to access a disused underpass were transformed into Sustainable Drainage Systems (SuDS).
sections.adaptation_measures: • In 2016 Westminster City Council (WCC) set up the Marylebone Low Emission Neighbourhood with the objective of reducing traffic levels and introducing green infrastructure into the local area. • The closure of the disused subway station by Transport for London (TfL) in 2018 led to proposals to repurpose the site to enhance the resilience of local roads and urban drainage against future flooding. • The SuDS were designed to be retrofitted to existing infrastructure to minimise disruption to the busy highways in Marylebone, and reduce the costs and risks associated with deep excavations in the middle of a busy carriageway. • TfL installed over 3,500m of rainwater collection space at the intersection of the A5 and the Marylebone flyover in central London, where the disused subway station was located. The ramps and stairs leading to the underpass were transformed into porous rain gardens. • Nine rain gardens (bioretention areas) were installed in existing gully locations, and new sewer connections added only where necessary to minimise road disruption. • The gardens included 11 trees, 95 shrubs, over 1,100 perennial plants, and almost 2,300 bulbs. Completed in October 2024, these SuDS feature vegetation such as pink geraniums, orange kniphofia, and blue wood-aster, chosen for their support of pollinator species, minimal maintenance needs, and ability to thrive in the local climate and soil conditions, tolerating both extreme wet and dry conditions. Organisation Transport for London Transport sub-sectors Highways First order climate hazard (cause) Heavy rainfall Second order climate hazard (effect) Surface water flooding Transport assets impacted Road pavement Adaptation measure Sustainable Drainage Systems Location  London, England Years of delivery  2018 - 2024 • The SuDS were installed to improve the surface drainage system and prevent a busy road junction from flooding, with the rain gardens designed to cope with a range of storm events from 1 in 5 years to 1 in 20 years.
sections.financials: • The design of the SuDS, site supervision and monitoring cost £65,000. • Total design fees were £729,000 and the total construction costs were £1.7 million. • Part of the project was funded by Thames Water and the Greater London Authority under the Green Infrastructure Fund programme, which committed £1 million for greening projects on TfL’s road network. • In its Business Plan, TfL committed £6 million of further investment up to the fiscal year 2027/28 to enhance existing projects through the installation of green infrastructure and SuDS. • Funding and maintenance of the gardens has been committed by the Marble Arch London Business Improvement District.
sections.resourcing: The Marylebone flyover rain garden project was a collaboration between TfL, the Greater London Authority, and Thames Water. WSP designed the SuDS and contractors were hired for the construction of the rain gardens, namely Tarmac and Kier in a Joint Venture.
sections.co_benefits: • Improved pedestrian safety and access to the area was achieved through a collaboration between TfL and WCC as part of the Safer Junctions Programme. This included the installation of signal-controlled pedestrian crossings, the extension of the pavement outside Edgware Road Underground Station, the removal of a left turn slip lane, and the rain gardens served as a buffer between the pavements and roads. • Biodiversity co-benefits included attracting pollinator species such as bees, moths, and butterflies, improving the local area’s aesthetics, improving local air quality, and achieving a net biodiversity gain. • Significant improvements were made to the local drainage system and downstream water quality through the installation of silt traps in kerb inlets. • Community education on urban flooding and water management was promoted through information boards next to the rain gardens, and local school children were engaged in planting flowers. • Sustainable transport modes were supported through the installation of additional cycle parking facilities.
sections.evaluation: • After the rain gardens were installed, WCC monitored their impact from late 2019 to early 2020. Due to the effectiveness of the SuDS, WCC did not report any flooding in the surrounding streets during storm events. • The SuDS installed at the Marylebone flyover form the largest sustainable drainage system in central London. TfL had a goal to install 5,000m of SuDS each year on its road network; the Marylebone flyover project meant that TfL met 70% of its 2024 annual target. Challenges and lessons learned • The project had a tight timeframe with only five weeks from tender submission to the completion deadline, and the lead time for the plants from nurseries was two to three weeks. • The project received media scrutiny which added pressure for the successful delivery of the project. • The project site was located near a busy high street with heavy pedestrian and vehicular traffic. • There were a number of basements and utilities located below or near to each of the rain garden sites, which meant that some of the gardens had to be installed shallower than intended and were lined with an impermeable membrane to protect the underlying services and basements. A lesson learnt was that undertaking basement surveys and archaeological surveys early in the project can minimise delays to construction. • This project could serve as a model for addressing climate effects from heavy rainfall in other transport sectors, particularly the rail, light rail, and aviation sectors if SuDS were installed near concreted areas such as stations and car parks.
sections.innovation_opportunities: SuDS encompass a range of well-developed approaches to manage flood risk; innovation could come through the continued application of these methods to a wide variety of environments and sectors. Scaling SuDS beyond a local application would enable a city-based approach and collaboration between a wider number of stakeholders, including opportunities for local communities to be involved in the upkeep and maintenance of SuDS. Innovation could additionally come through the retention of summer flash flood runoff for urban vegetation irrigation, especially as average summer rainfall reduces but intensity of downpours and resultant flash flooding become greater.

measure_title: The trial ran over two summer periods and involved using stormwater to irrigate a 4-hectare site
measure_description: The trial ran over two summer periods and involved using stormwater to irrigate a 4-hectare site

sections.challenge: Adelaide Airport Limited (AAL) is situated in a hot and dry landscape, the high air temperatures decrease air density, which can lead to several operational challenges. This includes reduced load capacity on aircrafts, increased fuel consumption during take-off and landing, higher energy use in cooling towers, dust generation and increased erosion . This places an increased strain on airline and airport services, impacting both employee and passenger experience, as well as flight efficiency. A trial was conducted at Adelaide Airport to test the effectiveness of irrigation of land in and around the airport to locally reduce high air temperatures . This trial collected and analysed projected data for both a base case (non-irrigated) and future option (200-hectares of irrigated land) to understand potential outcomes on air temperature and the financial and economic benefits of using irrigation to mitigate high temperatures . Additionally, the trial anticipated the feasibility of growing and harvesting a saleable crop on the irrigated land, with the profits potentially funding the irrigation process .
sections.adaptation_measures: The trial ran over two summer periods and involved using stormwater to irrigate a 4-hectare site within the airport grounds . The aim was to understand the benefits of maintaining appropriate vegetative cover to help reduce surface and air temperatures and the effects on airport operations . Planes at Adelaide Airport Adelaide City skyline view taken from the Adelaide International Airport OrganisationAdelaide Airport Transport sub-sectorAviation Climate hazard (cause)High temperatures Transport asset impactedAirport services; Airline Services; Buildings; Ancillary features Adaption measureIrrigation Location Adelaide, Australia Years of delivery2015 - 2017 Stormwater from an adjacent aquifer storage and recovery system was used to irrigate, leveraging existing infrastructure and avoiding reliance on mains water . Instead of maintaining airfield grass, Lucerne was chosen as the crop to trial, with the goal of using the land productively rather than just maintaining the airfield. Lucerne is a high water use crop with high evapotranspiration rates and hence has a higher potential to contribute to temperature reduction compared to other crops . To model the effects of irrigation, daily temperature readings were recorded across the test site (irrigated) and a control site (non-irrigated) . Additional data on temperature, humidity, thermal comfort, and evaporative and sensible heat fluxes were collected . Analysis of surface temperature collected across the trial periods enabled modelling of potential aircraft operation benefits and energy savings in adjacent buildings . The trial indicated that the air temperature in the irrigated area was more than 3 ̊C cooler than the non-irrigated area on hot days . The cost benefit assessment to date revealed that, to replicate this across 200-hectares of land at this site, airlines would experience over $1,000,000 in total potential benefits each year due to fuel cost savings and auxiliary power unit maintenance savings . Additionally, the likely outcome of the Lucerne production is under $2,000,000 to AAL over a 25 year period .
sections.applicability: The project's innovative potential could also extend to other airports and cities, to create cooler air zones for nearby suburbs, protect travellers and airport staff from extreme temperatures, and generate economic savings by improving aircraft operations . This project can be applied to any open space areas with irrigation options . This project is especially applicable to other airports where a single corporation can manage both the costs and benefits of the irrigation scheme . The operational benefits of reduced temperatures can help offset the costs associated with the irrigation system. Airports that are experiencing hotter days throughout summers, in particular in southern and central England, may be able to apply this project to their airfield. The applicability for an airport will be dependant on the availability of a water source, capacity for water storage, existing infrastructure, compliance with aviation and safety requirements including bird strike risks, and the practicality of managing vegetation (i.e. tractor access to airfield areas).
sections.financials: Funding Information not publicly available. Initial costs Base case: $52,000 worth of capital equipment . With no irrigation of the airside area, site management must adhere to aviation safety regulations to minimise the risk of bird strikes, including herbicide / pesticide spraying for weed and insect control. The capital cost of equipment required for mowing and herbicide / pesticide spraying is assumed to be $35,000 for the tractor, $10,000 for the slasher, and $7,000 for the boom spray . Future option: $2.448 million for 187 hectares of irrigation infrastructure (approx. $13,000/ha) . This allows for the implementation of an irrigation system to cover the required airside area. As well as the provision of Class B water (recycled water that is treated to a level that makes it suitable for irrigation of non-food crops) and the cultivation and harvesting of Lucerne in the irrigated zone . Ongoing costs Base case: $120,000 annual labour expenses and $5,035 operational costs . Future option: $150,000 annual labour, operational and maintenance costs related to Lucerne production . Avoided costs Information not publicly available. Economic impacts Economic impacts were measured through a process that included defining the base case and future option, identifying and quantifying significant costs and benefits, and evaluating indirect economic impacts on airport customers, operators, and businesses . The financial analysis indicated that the airport would see positive financial benefits after seven years of operation . The potential benefit to the airlines and airport operators would primarily be the ability to maintain payloads on hot days, with the additional small benefit from fuel savings during take-off and landing . Information on whether the above figures are in AUD or USD is not available.
sections.resourcing: The irrigation project was a collaboration between South Australia Water, Adelaide Airport, the Cooperative Research Centre for Water Sensitive Cities (CRCWSC), and Monash University . Due to the complexity of the operational environment, specialised contractors were required, operating under restricted access times and security clearances. Collaboration with South Australia Water and AAL ensured a comprehensive trial, with the goal of assessing both the cooling impacts and agricultural viability of the project.
sections.co_benefits: This project has increased industry knowledge about the relationship between irrigation and cooling effects on surfaces and air, which can be applied to other open spaces across South Australia, thanks to South Australia Water's involvement. Irrigating these areas can be seen as crucial for managing urban heat island effects in cities . Social benefits included potential temperature reductions that could improve airside worker comfort , opportunities to apply the harvesting method at other airports, and reputational benefits for Adelaide Airport and South Australia Water. The study did not account for typical green space benefits such as enhanced property values or improved mental health, as the area was not accessible to the public and was surrounded by traffic corridors. Therefore, these benefits typically identified with development of green space were not expected to be significant in this case. If the project expands, there is intention to use recycled water from existing infrastructure in the area (i.e., Glenelg Wastewater Treatment Plant) 1,2 .
sections.evaluation: The success of the trial was demonstrated by the conclusion that irrigation across 4 hectares can lead to an air temperature reduction of over 3 ̊C on hot days compared to unirrigated areas . A team of 40 data loggers took measurements both within and outside the irrigated area to understand the extent of the cooling effects . The trial results indicate that non-irrigated open spaces can be as hot as pavement . If further implemented across vegetated airside areas, the reduction in air temperatures and the ability to cultivate and harvest a marketable crop are expected to yield numerous benefits.
sections.challenges: The trial and study identified several risks that might be considered as challenges when deployed on a larger scale . Due to the increased vegetation, there is an increased risk of bird strikes due to the exposed soil and seed across Lucerne growth. The concentration of dried Lucerne hay in certain windows increases fire risk across the airport. The marketability of crops grown adjacent to consistent aeroplane activity are likely to be more contaminated than crops grown in rural environments, this may impact the profitability of the solution.
sections.lessons_learned: The CRC for Water Sensitive Cities (CRCWSC) is collaborating with SA Water and Adelaide Airport to enhance and expand this research by utilising advanced monitoring equipment and modelling techniques . Enhanced temperature monitoring with additional data loggers, weather stations, and advanced systems, including drones, could provide comprehensive data on environmental conditions . Modelling the benefits of surface temperature data could help understand the broader impacts on aircraft operations and energy savings. The trial of Lucerne instead of grass shows promise for productive land use, with economic modelling indicating a viable payback period.
sections.innovation_opportunities: Should this adaptation measure be suited to airports or other areas of land adjacent to transport infrastructure, it may be possible to gain multiple climate adaptation benefits such as ground stability through drought seasons or slowing rain runoff uphill of flood vulnerable infrastructure.

sections.challenge: • Gatwick Airport Limited (GAL) is built across the low relief, impermeable clay flood plain within the River Mole Catchment in the Thames River Basin District. • The impermeable geology gives the Mole a naturally rapid (“flashy”) flooding response to rainfall events; precipitation flows as surface run-off into streams and channels. • The area has suffered significant flooding in 1947, 1960, 1968, 1974, 1990, 2000 and the winter of 2013 to 2014 (2) . • In 2000, the A23 under GAL’s South Terminal was closed for three days due to flooding and 109 homes were affected in Crawley (1) . • The 2013-14 storms and flood events severely impacted GAL’s airfield operations: o Three electrical sub-stations serving the airfield were affected by flooding, and 35 inbound flights were diverted away from Gatwick due to high winds (3) . o Due to the high winds, unsecured aircraft baggage containers blew onto the airfield, resulting in the damage of the runway instrument landing system (3) . o Flooding of GAL’s North Terminal electrical systems ultimately led to the cancellation of 145 flights, 77% of planned departures, and further delays; impacting over 13,000 passengers on Christmas Eve (3) . • The Airport shares responsibility for managing the river with other land users and the Environment Agency. • Wetter winters and more intense rainfall events leading to flash floods are projected to increase across the UK under climate change, increasing in intensity through the century.
sections.applicability: • Clay geology and other geology and terrain types, including urban paved environments, that encounter rapid runoff flash flood conditions occur in numerous areas across the UK. Organisation Environment Agency Transport sub-sector Aviation First order climate hazards (cause) Fluvial flooding; Surface water flooding; Storms and high winds Second order climate hazard (effect) Water damage Transport assets impacted Mechanical and electrical equipment; Buildings; Airport services Adaptation measure(s) Flood storage reservoirs (dams) Location  Gatwick, United Kingdom Years of delivery 2014 - 2019 • Flash flooding events are likely to increase in occurrence due to projected increases through the century in rainfall intensity and volume due to climate change, therefore more catchments and transport infrastructure are likely to be vulnerable to this type of flooding. • As in this case study, protection of infrastructure through creation or upgrading of flood storage reservoirs would be most appliable as part of wider urban protections, i.e. alongside housing flood protection, but may also be applicable to discrete parts of vulnerable infrastructure.
sections.adaptation_measures: Measures implemented • In 2014, the Environment Agency developed the Upper Mole Flood Alleviation Scheme to reduce the risk of flooding to over 1,400 homes and businesses in Crawley and Horley, and to GAL (2) . • The scheme consists of four flood storage reservoirs and is designed to provide a 1 in 50 year flood protection from fluvial flooding from the Gatwick Stream. • One of these four reservoirs forms the Clays Lake Scheme, which lies on the Standford Brook, a tributary of the Gatwick Stream. • The scheme involves the removal of an existing dam and its replacement with a larger dam • The larger dam will: o Increase the lake’s capacity from 10,000 cubic metres to nearly 400,000 cubic metres o Reduce the risk of flooding to more than 280 homes in the area and GAL (4,5) . • The dam is constructed to withstand a one in 10,000 year ‘Probable Maximum Flood’ and would only be used to store water above the normal lake level in times of flood, on a very infrequent basis (4) .
sections.financials: Funding • The Upper Mole Flood Alleviation Scheme, consisting of four flood storage reservoirs, was funded by the Environment Agency (2) . • GAL made a financial contribution of £4 million to the three Gatwick Stream reservoirs in line with the three water courses it is vulnerable to, of which Standford Brook (i.e., Clays Lake Scheme) is one (3) . Initial costs • The Upper Mole Flood Alleviation Scheme costs a total of £17 million (3) . • Further information is not publicly available. Ongoing costs • Information about the ongoing costs associated with the adaptation measure, including routine maintenance is not publicly available. Avoided costs • Information about avoided costs is not publicly available. Economic impacts • Information about economic impacts is not publicly available.
sections.resourcing: • The Flood prevention works at Clay’s Lake was carried out by Mackley Engineering (Lead Contractor) as part of Team Van Oord, and on behalf of the Environment Agency (5) . • Frog Environmental consultancy firm supported on silt management (5) .
sections.co_benefits: • This intervention can help reduce the impact on airfield operations across GLA during flood and storm events. • The 2013-14 storms and flood events led to the cancellation of 145 flights; impacting over 13,000 passengers’ journeys on Christmas Eve (3) . • Further information about avoided flight cancellations and passenger impact due to this adaptation measure is not publicly available.
sections.evaluation: • Information about how the adaptation measure was monitored and assessed is not publicly available. • Over time, the success of this intervention could be measured by reduced flood incidents and airport disruption during rainfall and flood conditions.
sections.challenges: • Information about the challenges faced during the implementation of the adaptation measure is not publicly available.
sections.lessons_learned: • Information about lessons learned is not publicly available.
sections.innovation_opportunities: • The Lead Contractor deployed an innovative solution to silt management. • Natural silt wattles and filters, such as those designed by Frog Environmental, are a silt control solution which help manage sediment and muddy water, which can help avoid siltation of watercourses and river structures and loss of river depth / capacity. • Natural silt wattles are used as: o Check dams in rivers, ditches, swales and gullies to reduce the speed of water and aid natural sediment deposition o Natural media to support silt trapping by containing the dirty water pumped from excavation o Controls for soil erosion o Surface water protection o Natural ground infiltration due to their low permeability • Unlike natural straw bales traditionally used, the wattles did not break down or degrade over time. This reduced maintenance and resource demand for the lead contractor during excavation construction (5) . Flood alleviation schemes such as flood storage reservoirs are a regular and established type of infrastructure protection and have a wide range of additional benefits to biodiversity that can in turn provide additional nature based flood solutions. Innovative nature based solutions have in recent years included: • Leaky dams (purpose-built natural dams - like those made by beavers - used to restore or mimic the natural flow of water through a landscape, reducing the risk of flooding, providing habitat drought resilience, filtering out pollutants, and increasing biodiversity) • Blockage of gulleys or watercourses in habitats at the head of catchments, to aid water retention, which can have co-benefits of restoring or enhancing habitats such as peat bogs • Softer engineered management of the flow of water in the upper parts of catchments, which can complement or be constructed alongside hard engineering solutions to slow and control runoff

hazard_cause: Storms and high winds, Surface water flooding

sections.challenge: • The Conwy Valley railway line is located on a flood plain near the Conwy Estuary, making it particularly vulnerable to flooding during severe weather events (1) . • These conditions have frequently washed away earthworks beneath the tracks, leading to prolonged closures and eroding passenger confidence in service reliability (1) . • In 2020 alone Network Rail reported that the impacts of embankment damage cost the taxpayer £1m and prevented passengers from using the railway (3) .
sections.applicability: The success of this project has demonstrated that these resilience methods can be applied across the UK and in other transport sectors, such as roads and canals, where embankments face similar flooding risks.
sections.adaptation_measures: Measure(s) implemented To address these challenges, Network Rail implemented resilience measures after two severe flood-related incidents within 18 months during 2019-2020 (1) . These events washed away sections of the earthworks under the tracks, with the second incident causing the line to be closed from March – August in 2019, and from February to October in 2020 (6) . Key interventions included: • Rock armour installation: Over a mile of embankment between Tal-y-Cafn and Llanrwst was reinforced with 16,000 tonnes of locally sourced rock armour. The rock armour was installed on the side of the track most prone to becoming a flood plain, as this slowed floodwater flow, protected the track from washouts, and reduced maintenance needs (2,6) . • Installation of culverts: Six 1.2 metre diameter culverts were installed underneath the railway to act as a flood alleviation device (6) . • Design refinement: Observing water movements during storms allowed Network Rail to optimise rock armour designs for enhanced durability and performance (5) . Organisation Network Rail Transport sub-sector Railways First order climate hazards (cause) Storms and high winds; Surface water flooding Second order climate hazard (effect) None identified Transport asset impacted Track Adaptation measure Earthworks Location  Conwy Valley, Wales Years of delivery 2019 - 2020 • Maintenance and adaptability: The design promotes natural vegetation growth over time, creating a sustainable, low-carbon, and visually pleasing solution that fits seamlessly into the landscape (5) . Other options assessed • Some rock armour had already been installed along the track prior to the 2019 flooding incident, and it was noticed that the ballast washout was not as severe in these locations as it was held in place by the stone (6) . • As the timescale to repair the railway was very short, Network Rail was unable to analyse other adaptation options and so rock armour was chosen as the adaptation design measure (6) .
sections.financials: Funding • Funding was provided by Network Rail and secured to ensure the delivery of the second phase of rock armour installation (5,6) . Initial costs • The total cost of the project was approximately £10 million, with £6 million allocated specifically to resilience measures (2) . Ongoing costs • Although some maintenance of the rock armour may be required to ensure its continued effectiveness, the only identified ongoing costs are associated with routine inspections of the asset, which would have already been undertaken on a regular basis prior to this installation. This means that no extra cost has occurred from this adaptation measure (5 and 6) . • Additionally, the culverts have a long design life, meaning that the first repair costs for these could be in 30 years’ time (6) . Avoided costs • By preventing embankment damage and washouts, the resilience measures have avoided over £8 million in losses, meaning the investment has already paid for itself (2) . Economic impacts Conwy Valley line following the 2019 flood Conwy Valley line since the resilience works • The project has improved the reliability of the railway, resulting in increased passenger numbers. This underscores the railway’s value in supporting economic travel and contributing to local communities (1) . • Repairs following extreme weather now take days rather than weeks or months, further minimising disruptions (4) .
sections.resourcing: • The rock armour installation was carried out by the contractor Alun Griffiths, however the installation was designed by Network Rail and modelled by JBA Consulting (6) .
sections.co_benefits: • Environmental: The use of natural, locally sourced materials minimised the carbon footprint, and the absence of concrete or steel allowed the installation to support diverse habitats over time (5) . • Social: The restoration of a reliable service has increased passenger numbers, re-established community trust in the railway, and supported economic and social mobility. The increase in passenger numbers was even recorded during the pandemic (1) . • Sustainability: Long-term performance improves as the installation becomes more vegetated and integrated into the natural environment (5) .
sections.evaluation: • Since its completion, the railway has performed well during storms, with no major disruptions due to flooding (1) . There have been at least three events whereby the flood water has overtopped the railway at Conwy Valley. However, due to the success of the installation, Network Rail have been able to re-open the tracks once the water has receded (6) . • Additionally, off the back of the success of the Conwy Valley rock armour installation, nearby sites such as Abergavenny and Welshpool, which were experiencing similar issues to the Conwy Valley site, also had rock armour installed which have since enabled the railways to reopen successfully following flood events (6) .
sections.challenges: The second phase of rock armour installation coincided with the COVID-19 pandemic, which limited the scope of work. Despite these constraints, Network Rail successfully completed the project, and even during partial installation, the measures effectively withstood severe flooding (5) .
sections.lessons_learned: • In 2019, Network Rail initially installed the rock armour on both sides of the railway, however, upon observing water flow during storms, the team later realised that the rock armour only needed to be installed on the side most prone to becoming a flood plain (6) . • It was observed that as the flood plain fills up and over the railway, the water speeds up and starts to mobilise and wash away the material beneath the track (6) . Once this was realised, Network Rail amended their design further to increase the amount of rock armour needed than first thought in 2019 (6) . • It was also realised, through these observations, that the rock armour had to be at the right height in relation to the track in order to slow the flow down (6) . • These learnings have been instrumental in enhancing the resilience of railway embankments in other areas (6) .
sections.innovation_opportunities: • This project showcases the potential for innovative, nature-based solutions in flood resilience alongside hard engineering. • Future opportunities could include integrating more advanced monitoring systems or developing modular designs for quicker deployment in high-risk areas.

hazard_cause: High temperatures, Urban Heat Island effect
hazard_effect: Road surface overheating, Increased energy demand
measure_description: Reflective CoolSeal coating applied to 100+ miles of residential roads.
case_study_text: Reflective CoolSeal coating applied to 100+ miles of residential roads to combat extreme urban heat island effect. Increases road reflectivity by 30% and reduces surface temperatures by 6 degrees C.

sections.challenge: • Phoenix, Arizona is the hottest city in the United States (17) . • In July 2024, daily average temperatures rose to a record peak of 47.8 ° C. This formed part of a record 81 days above 37.8 ° C, including 16 consecutive nights above 32 ° C (1) . • During this period, 466 heat-related deaths were confirmed in Maricopa County, Arizona’s biggest metropolitan area, with a further 191 under investigation (2) . The heatwave was accompanied by high humidity, which reduces the body’s ability to regulate heat, leading to heat-related illness. • Global temperature increases disproportionately affect cities because of the urban heat island (UHI) effect, where cities retain heat during nights more than rural areas. • UHI is caused by factors such as the use of building materials, which can absorb and retain heat to a greater degree than natural environments (3) . • Asphalt contributes to UHI due to its dark colour and is primarily used in road and pavement (4) . • Road surfaces represent 30-40% of Phoenix’s metropolitan area (5) and cover 5,000 miles of land (7) . • Black asphalt has a low albedo (surface reflectivity) of just 0.03-0.04, with a high thermal mass and storage capacity for heat. Consequently, asphalt pavements absorb heat during the day making the surrounding areas hotter by elevating both daytime and overnight surface temperatures through the release of this heat (23) . This increases UHI effects. • Higher temperatures contribute to increased energy consumption and GHG emissions in cities (23) .
sections.adaptation_measures: Measure implemented • In 2020, the City of Phoenix Street Transportation Department piloted a reflective coating for roads, called ‘CoolSeal’, aimed at reducing the heat impact on roads. CoolSeal reflects solar radiation more effectively than asphalt and reduces heat storage. It was applied in Phoenix to 36 miles of residential roads and a car park (5) . Organisation City of Phoenix Street Transportation Department; Arizona State University Transport sub-sector Roads First order climate hazard (cause) High temperatures (in city centre) Second order climate hazard (effect) Urban Heat Island (UHI) effect Transport assets impacted Road pavement Adaptation measure Cool pavement technology (CoolSeal) Location  Phoenix, United States Years of delivery 2021 – ongoing • CoolSeal was not applied to footpaths. Footpaths in Phoenix are typically made of concrete, which reflects heat comparably to CoolSeal (7) . • As of 2025, more than 100 miles of roads have been sprayed with cool pavement in Phoenix (7) . *Refer to case study ID 22 which highlights a cool pavements trial in Western Sydney, Australia which did not achieve the same results as the City of Phoenix. Other options assessed • The City of Phoenix were exploring adaptation measures that could be implemented as part of its Climate Adaptation Plan (7) . • Other options, such as permeable asphalt, could have been used, which reduce air temperature by allowing water to filter through pores and evaporate as temperatures increase (19) . • Permeable pavement is more expensive than cool pavement, costing USD$54 (£41.93) to USD$135 (£104.81) per square yard in materials (10) . • Permeable pavement also has a higher implementation cost as it involves replacing road surfaces. • The City of Phoenix learnt about the positive impacts of cool pavements during a Federal Highway Administration workshop (7) . • Roads are coated in CoolSeal, a liquid product which increases the reflectivity of road surfaces by 30% (13) and reduces surface temperatures by around 6 ° C (5) . • CoolSeal costs around USD$5 (£3.88) per square yard in material costs (8) . It is easy to implement as it is sprayed directly onto road surfaces. • Cool pavement increases the longevity of roads by reducing its thermal grade, which lowers maintenance costs (7) . • Cool pavements help reduce heat impacts on roads in the absence of tree cover, which can take 10-20 years to grow (7) . • Other cities, such as the City of Los Angeles have piloted cool pavements on its roads and had partially reduced UHI effects (7) .
sections.applicability: • This adaptation measure could be applicable to cities in the UK experiencing increased urban heat island effects due to intensifying summer heat. • In 2022 the UK summer heatwaves caused temperatures to exceed 40°C for the first time, contributing to wildfires in London and increased energy use. Hotspots in London were identified as 4.5°C warmer at night than the rural surroundings due to the urban heat island effect. • Heat-related deaths rose across the UK in the July record heatwave and an additional period of high temperatures in August, especially for over 65s (11) . • In London, heat-related deaths more than doubled from 2021 (158 estimated deaths) to 2022 (387 estimated deaths), primarily during these two periods of very hot weather (11) . • Application of CoolSeal throughout cities such as London may reduce urban heat and help alleviate the increasing heat stress (24) . • The measure may be applicable to other asphalt assets that contribute to the urban heat island effect.
sections.financials: Funding • Funding for the pilot programme was provided from the City of Phoenix’s existing pavement maintenance budget (7) , the Arizona Highway Revenue Fund (8) . • Additional funding was provided by the City Council with support from Arizona State University to increase the number of neighbourhoods that the project could be piloted in (7) . • Total annual funding for the project is USD $4.8m (7) (£3.73m). Initial costs • The estimated cost of the initial pilot was USD $3m (8) (£2.33m). Ongoing costs • Maintenance costs are not currently known and maintenance has not been required to date, as CoolSeal has a product life expectancy of 8 years (7) . Avoided costs • It is estimated that expanding the Cool Pavements Program throughout Phoenix could save USD $10m (£7.76m) in avoided residential air conditioning maintenance and replacement costs annually (14) . • Phoenix spends 0.28% of its city GDP on air conditioning repair and replacement, which is a higher proportion than any other major city in the world (6) . • It is estimated that widespread application of cool pavement in Phoenix may save 28m gallons of residential water a year, assuming a uniform cooling of 0.28 o C (14) .
sections.resourcing: • The Cool Pavement Program is managed and delivered by the Department of Transport within the City of Phoenix (7) . • Arizona State University (ASU) is responsible for ongoing performance monitoring (15) . • The City of Phoenix’s contractors were responsible for applying CoolSeal to the pavements (7) . • GuardTop, who manufacture CoolSeal, supplied the product to the contractors (7) . • GuardTop were also responsible for working with its contractors to turn the product into a spray to make application more efficient (7) .
sections.co_benefits: Community • The primary benefit of this programme was improved public health through reducing UHI effects. • As cool pavements are reflective, it also improves visibility in areas where it has been piloted. For example, reducing the need for lights in car parks. This may also enhance the public’s feelings of safety at night (7) . Decreased energy consumption and carbon reduction • If provided at scale, the adaptation measure would reduce the Urban Heat Island effect. This could reduce the use of air conditioning especially at night, lowering the cost of electricity and reducing GHG emissions (23) .
sections.evaluation: • The programme has reduced summer surface temperatures by 6 ° C in areas where cool pavement has been applied (16) . • Its success is also evaluated by the number of miles of cool pavement that have been implemented on Phoenix’s roads (7) . To date, 100 miles of Phoenix’s roads have been coated in CoolSeal (7) . • Increased adoption across other US cities is seen as a success metric. Cities piloting cool pavement include Davis and San Antonio, California (7) . *See ID 22 for evaluation of the CoolSeal product applied in a suburban trial
sections.challenges: • Implementation was a challenge identified during early project stages (7) . As CoolSeal requires time to dry, off-duty police officers were required to direct cars away from wet product. • Community buy-in was difficult due to the short-term disruption caused by the project (7) . This was navigated through an online public meeting, website and map to share project information (7) . • CoolSeal is currently only applicable to lower speed (i.e., 25mph limit) streets without compromising on skid safety (7) . *See ID 22 for challenges experienced on a different cool pavements trial.
sections.lessons_learned: • The mean radiant temperature (a measure of the average temperature of the surfaces that surround a particular point with which it will exchange thermal radiation) is increased compared to untreated streets, but is comparable to concrete sidewalks. This means that site selection is important to prioritise areas that are not anticipated to have people lingering on cool pavement during the day (7) . • The matt finish does not generally produce a substantial glaring effect (7). *See ID 22 for lessons learned on a similar pilot in Australia
sections.innovation_opportunities: • A version with a higher skid resistance would enable cool pavement to be applied to streets with a higher traffic volume (7) . • Innovation opportunities lie in combining cool pavement coatings with strategic tree planting and engineered shading to enhance cooling. • Advancements in high-albedo coatings, such as infrared-reflective pigments and phase-change materials, could improve durability while reducing glare. • Smart infrastructure, including temperature-responsive coatings and embedded sensors, can optimise performance and maintenance. • Community engagement in street selection and anti-vandalism treatments can improve public acceptance and longevity. • A holistic approach integrating these elements will maximize urban cooling benefits.

organisation: Major Road Projects Victoria (MRPV) Pound Road West Upgrade in Dandenong South

sections.challenge: Steel corrosion across road and rail is being exacerbated by the impacts of climate change (4) . Corrosion of reinforced concrete leads to costly maintenance, repairs and replacements, and indirect costs associated with operational disruption and safety hazards (12) . It is estimated that repairs and replacements associated with steel corrosion cost around AU $13 billion (~£6.4 billion) each year in Australia (3) . Corrosion of steel occurs when the relative humidity of the air is 70% to 80% and the air temperature is above 0°C (14) . Rates of corrosion are expected to significantly increase in coastal and inland regions in Australia due to climate change, with increases in moisture and temperature expediting the corrosion of steel in concrete structures (4) . Extreme weather events and flooding also promote the corrosion of steel reinforcement and place additional loading on infrastructure (4) . The flooding in Brisbane in 2022 resulted in infrastructure damage which was exacerbated by the corroded steel reinforcements (4) . Damage to public infrastructure from the flooding is estimated to have cost $492 million (13) .
sections.adaptation_measures: Measure implemented ‘Victoria’s Big Build’ is a project delivering more than 180 major road and rail projects, including the Pound Road West Upgrade in Dandenong South (1) . The Pound Road West Upgrade included the construction of a new bridge over the Cranbourne rail line, improving access to the freeway (2) . In the construction of the Pound Road West Upgrade, Glass Fibre Reinforced Polymer Rebar (GFRP) reinforcement was used in lieu of conventional steel to improve the infrastructure’s resilience to climate change impacts (3) . GFRP is composed of a polymer plastic matrix with embedded glass fibres. The polymer for GFRP rebar usually consists of a vinyl ester, epoxy or polyester thermosetting plastic. Steel is a metal alloy composed of iron with a small percentage of carbon. The material differences between GFRP and steel means their structural performance and durability differ when used as reinforcement in concrete (11) . OrganisationMajor Road Projects Victoria (MRPV) Transport subsectorRoads First order climate hazards (cause)Heavy rainfall; High temperatures; Flooding (fluvial, coastal, surface water) Second order climate hazard (effect)Steel corrosion Transport asset impactedBridge Adaptation measureGlass fibre reinforcement for concrete Location Victoria, Australia Years of delivery2021-2023 More than 1,000 6-metre-long bars of GFRP were installed to reinforce concrete panels for the bridge’s retaining walls (5) . A 6-kilometre-long shared user path constructed as part of the project also used GFRP as a sustainable reinforcement for pavements; this was awarded carbon credits as it was the first of its kind (12) . Other options assessed Several alternative reinforcement options were considered, including epoxy-coated steel, stainless steel reinforcement, and fibre-reinforced concrete additives (12) . However, GFRP was chosen for the following reasons: oSuperior corrosion resistance – unlike epoxy-coated or stainless steel, GFRP does not rust or deteriorate over time (12) . oCost-effectiveness in the long term – while the initial material cost is slightly higher than traditional steel, the elimination of corrosion-related maintenance offsets this (12) . oInstallation efficiency – the lightweight nature of GFRP reduces transportation costs and enables quicker installation - lighter panel design (12) . Using non-corrosive reinforcements eliminates corrosion-induced concrete spalling (deterioration e.g., cracking), which is the main vulnerability of reinforced concrete, significantly improving its durability in the face of a changing climate and reducing the need for repairs and associated emissions and waste (6) . While most steel-reinforced concrete bridges start to deteriorate after only 30 years, GFRP rebar has an expected life span of more than 100 years (12) . GFRP has significantly lower thermal conductivity than steel rebar, meaning it is able to cope better with extreme temperature variations. The thermal conductivity of V-ROD GFRP is less than 1 W/m.°C while the thermal conductivity of steel is 54 W/m.°C (12) . In terms of fire-resistance, GFRP has a critical temperature (the temperature at which the reinforcement loses enough tensile strength that the applied load can no longer be supported) of 325°C (4) . GFRP reinforcement mesh panels used in the Pound Road West Upgrade. GFRP reinforcement being laid on the shared user path in the Pound Road West Upgrade.
sections.applicability: GFRP could be an appropriate solution to the damage experienced in UK transport infrastructure from corrosion of steel-reinforced concrete: GFRP’s corrosion resistance makes it an excellent choice for bridges, tunnels, and coastal highways (12) . GFRP’s lightweight nature reduces structural loads, making it a viable solution for aging infrastructure in the UK (12) . The UK’s push for low-carbon construction aligns with GFRP’s lower environmental impact compared to steel production (12) . The MRPV have worked to implement innovative sustainability solutions like GFRP within their projects, starting with smaller-scale aspects such as bike trails and shared user paths, which, in turn, supports businesses in Australia whose products meet international standards but are not yet part of the Australian standards (12) . Companies are incentivised to use new, innovative products through carbon credits, which were achieved through the Pound Road West Upgrade (12) . Infrastructure projects in the UK could follow a similar approach. The GFRP supplied by Madewell Products aligns with international standards (12) : ACI CODE-440.11-22: Building Code Requirements for Structural Concrete Reinforced with GFRP Bars (American Concrete Institute) ACI 440.1R-15: Guide for the Design and Construction of Structural Concrete Reinforced with FRP Bars (American Concrete Institute) ACI 440.3R-12: Guide Test Methods for FRP Reinforcing or Strengthening Concrete Structures (American Concrete Institute) CSA S806-12: Design and Construction of Building Structures with Fibre-Reinforced Polymers (Canadian Standards Association) CSA S807-19: Specification for Fibre-Reinforced Polymers (Canadian Standards Association) Meanwhile, relevant standards in the UK include (12) : BS EN 13706: Reinforced Plastics Composites – Specifications for Pultruded Profiles BS EN 14649: Precast Concrete Products – Test Method for Strength Retention of Glass Fibres in Cement and Concrete (SIC Test) BS EN 15422: Precast Concrete Products – Specification of Glassfibres for Reinforcement of Mortars and Concretes BS EN 1169: Precast Concrete Products – General Rules for Factory Production Control of Glass-Fibre Reinforced Cement GFRP is most suitable and economical for harsh conditions where cracking and corrosion is prioritised over structural capacity, such as in coastal environments, splash zones, areas where there are high CO emissions, and where corrosive chemicals and de-icing salts are used (11) .
sections.financials: Funding The Pound Road West Upgrade formed part of the broader Suburban Roads Upgrade – South Eastern and Northern Roads project, which was jointly funded by the Australian Government (AU $1.14 billion / ~£560 million) and the Victorian government (AU $2.27 billion / ~£1.12 billion) (7) . Initial costs For a 16mm bar, the cost of GFRP is the same as a steel alternative (approximately AU $3.60 / £1.77 per linear metre). For larger bars, the cost of GFRP is higher, however, this is offset by reduced installation time and transport costs (12) . The GFRP supplied by Madewell Products totalled AU $33,000 (~£16,200) before tax for 1,100 bars, equating to AU $4.80 (£2.36) per linear metre (12) . The contractor, Seymour Whyte, was awarded AU $100-$150 million (~£49.1-£73.7 million) for delivering the Pound Road West Upgrade alongside a second project, the Frankston- Dandenong Road Bridge Upgrade (8) . Ongoing costs Maintenance costs are minimal since GFRP is non-corrosive, eliminating the need for future repairs related to rust or degradation (12) . Avoided costs It has been reported that repairs and replacements associated with steel corrosion cost around AU $13 billion (~£6.4 billion) each year in Australia (3) . Cost savings include corrosion-related repair savings, extended service life, and reduced maintenance (12) . There are also cost savings from a contractor perspective, with fewer labourers required to install GFRP as opposed to steel reinforcements due to the reduced material weight (4) .
sections.resourcing: The Pound Road West Upgrade was completed by Major Roads Project Victoria (MRPV) in 2023 as part of Victoria’s Big Build (2) . Victoria’s Big Build is a project being delivered by the Victorian Infrastructure Delivery Authority (VIDA) and the Suburban Rail Loop Authority (SRLA), working closely with the Department of Transport and Planning (DTP) (1) . MRPV is a VIDA project office. The Pound Road West Upgrade was delivered in collaboration with Seymour Whyte and Madewell Products (3) . Madewell Products provided the GFRP, while Seymour Whyte was the contractor on the project (8) . Madewell Products work closely with their two manufacturers, Dextra and Pultrall, based in Canada (12) .
sections.co_benefits: Carbon reduction Road freight is responsible for around 5% of global carbon emissions, of which, 12% is produced by construction freight; GFRP’s significantly lower weight means that more material can be transported by a single truck, saving hundreds or thousands of freight kilometres per project (6) . For example, the 550 GFRP reinforcement sheets used for the shared user path in the Pound Road West Upgrade were transported to the site on a small 3.5 tonne truck, while the equivalent amount of steel reinforcement needed would require three semi-trucks (12) . The manufacturing of GFRP produces 28% less emitted carbon than steel rebar; one metre of GFRP has a total emission factor of 1.7352 kgCO eq/m compared with 2.411 kgCO eq/m for one metre of steel rebar (3) . GFRP requires less concrete cover than steel reinforcements, contributing to overall carbon savings; GFRP requires around 20mm of concrete compared with 40-75mm required for steel rebar (12) . For the shared user path in the Pound Road West Upgrade, concrete cover was reduced by 50% (12) . Economic The use of GFRP contributes to longer service life of public infrastructure, saving repair and replacement costs associated with steel corrosion (9) .
sections.evaluation: To evaluate the long-term effectiveness of GFRP in the Pound Road West Upgrade, the following metrics are being monitored (12) : Structural integrity: Regular inspections are conducted to ensure GFRP is performing as expected under load and environmental conditions. Degradation & wear: Unlike steel, GFRP does not corrode, but monitoring ensures there are no unexpected material weaknesses. Load performance: Sensors may be used to assess how the reinforced structure responds to heavy vehicle loads over time. Lifecycle cost analysis: Future maintenance costs will be tracked and compared against projections to validate cost savings. Monitoring is likely to be carried out by the contractors, Seymour Whyte, and may include a 28-day test to observe crack widths (12) . Further evaluation includes: Feedback from construction workers was that GFRP reinforcement was a lot easier to carry and place than steel reinforcement (12) . The project was assessed by the Infrastructure Sustainability Council (ISC) and was awarded an ‘Excellent’ rating for sustainability (10) .
sections.challenges: Higher upfront costs Although lifecycle cost savings are substantial, initial material costs were higher compared to steel (12) . Industry familiarity Contractors and engineers needed training on proper handling and installation of GFRP bars (12) . Design considerations GFRP behaves differently from steel (e.g., lower modulus of elasticity), requiring design adjustments in some structural elements (12) . Supply chain and availability Ensuring reliable sourcing of high-quality GFRP materials was a key challenge. There are not many suppliers of GFRP in Australia and a lack of competition for Madewell Products (12) . Field bending Unlike steel alternatives, GFRP bars can’t be field bent (bending on a construction site to fit specific concrete structures). Having the bars bent to specification by the manufacturers requires a degree of forward-planning but allows a higher level of quality and assurance (4) . Dextra is currently working on technology to enable field bending of GFRP; this is expected to be completed in Q2 2025 and will significantly improve the feasibility of using GFRP in infrastructure projects (12) .
sections.lessons_learned: GFRP is most suitable and economical for harsh conditions where cracking and corrosion is prioritised over structural capacity, such as in coastal environments, splash zones, areas where there are high CO emissions, and where corrosive chemicals and de-icing salts are used (11) . More education and communication around GFRP is needed to ensure its benefits are realised. For example, on the Pound Road West Upgrade, designers initially assumed that the concrete cover thickness should be equal to that used for steel reinforcement due to a lack of awareness that less concrete cover is needed for GFRP (12) . The success of GFRP in projects like the Pound Road West Upgrade needs more communication within the infrastructure industry so that it can be used in other projects. Innovative solutions like GFRP are implemented successfully but are then not incorporated into projects that follow (12) .
sections.innovation_opportunities: Early studies have shown that GFRP could allow the production of concrete using seawater, sea sand and sea aggregates, reducing the need to use depleting resources like water, river sand and crushed rocks. Secondary benefits include: lower cement content, less transportation, and the ability to reuse the materials at the end of its life (6) .

organisation: Government of South Australia Fire Suppression Systems

sections.challenge: Bushfires in Australia can cause disruption and significantly damage road infrastructure – this can include structural damage, reduced visibility as a result of bushfire smoke, melted or cracked asphalt, and road obstructions (7) . A notable example of this was the Black-Summer bushfire season during 2019 - 2020. During this period almost 19 million hectares was burnt, impacting roads and causing widespread damage and numerous road closures due to fallen trees and burnt vegetation (8) . Work commenced in 2023 on the South Eastern Freeway Upgrade Project, whereby refit work would address elements which are at the end of their service life to improve safety, traffic management, and incident response capabilities (1) .
sections.adaptation_measures: Measures implemented When the Heysen Tunnels were first opened to road users in March 2000, they were constructed to the highest standard at the time. However, since they were opened, Australian and international standards relating to road tunnel safety have advanced significantly. The refit work will bring the infrastructure up to industry standards (2) . For example, although not mandatory, the Australian Government’s 2019 technical report on fire safety measures in tunnels highlighted the importance of tunnel ventilation, emergency communication services, such as radio networks, and fire suppression systems in addressing fires more quickly (9) . Key elements of the tunnel’s new fire safety system include: 23 new exhaust fans, 8 in the Crafers-bound tunnel and 15 in the Adelaide-bound tunnel to improve visibility and air quality. The exhaust fans will help to disperse smoke in the event of an emergency such as wildfire, as well as provide ventilation, control exhaust fumes and clear fog in the tunnel (1) . An automated fire suppression system capable of spraying water inside the tunnels for up to two hours during a fire. An emergency communication system which includes in-tunnel signage and help phones to improve safety and network reliability, as well as a public address radio rebroadcast system to allow emergency messaging to be sent directly to drivers' car radios in case of tunnel OrganisationGovernment of South Australia Transport sub-sector(s)Road First order climate hazard (cause)None identified Second order climate hazards (effect)Wildfire; Air pollution Transport asset impactedRoad Adaptation measureVentilation systems Location Adelaide, Australia Years of delivery2023 - 2025 emergencies (5) . Upgrades to the Intelligent Transport System (ITS) equipment including thermal incident detection systems, new and replacement CCTV, electronic speed and messaging signage and over height vehicle detection. Upgrades to the traffic management system to restrict access to the tunnels in the event of an emergency incident.
sections.applicability: This is an international case study; however, due to increasingly hotter and drier summers, the risk of wildfire outbreaks in the UK is rising. For example, the heatwaves in summer 2022 led to the occurrence of large grass fires in east London, destroying 20 homes. Additionally, in 2019 the UK’s biggest wildfire occurrence was recorded in Sutherland's Flow Country, affecting 20sq miles of peatland. Therefore, implementing adaptation measures to limit the effects of wildfires experienced by commuters could be beneficial to the transport sector in the longer term. As the risk of wildfire increases in the UK, learning from best practice approaches in other countries will be critical. The UK has no dedicated standard on fire safety in tunnels. The Road Tunnel Safety 2007 regulations references the importance of providing fire extinguishers and emergency phones in tunnels, however fire suppression systems are not mentioned (10) .
sections.financials: Funding In May 2024 both the Australian and South Australian Governments contributed a combined total of AUD$150 million to deliver the Heysen Tunnels Refit and Safety Upgrade (3) . Initial costs AUD$150 million was provided for the refit works (3) . Ongoing costs Not publicly available as the works have yet to be finished. Any ongoing costs such as maintenance have yet to be recorded. Avoided costs Although no costing numbers have been made publicly available, the general expected operational lifespan of the new systems and components exceed 20 years. Many of the systems have been future proofed to ensure component upgrades can be undertaken with minimal cost and disruption (2) .
sections.resourcing: McConnell Dowell Constructors (Aust) Pty Ltd were the managing contractor for the works and local sub-contractors and suppliers were used wherever possible. Although the 23 exhaust fans were built in Germany, their support frames were fabricated locally (1) .
sections.co_benefits: Economic benefits The South Eastern Freeway Upgrade is expected to support more than 315 full-time equivalent jobs per year over the construction period (3) . Further co-benefits information is not available in the public domain.
sections.evaluation: The project is currently nearing completion stage and has not yet had an opportunity to be evaluated; however, final system testing is taking place in January 2025. Additionally, the infrastructure and transport department chief executive has reported that with the completion of these refit works, this will make the Heysen Tunnels “the safest tunnels anywhere in the world at the moment” (5) .
sections.challenges: During construction at the Heysen Tunnels, temporary traffic restrictions were addressed by: Operating during off-peak times to minimise disruption to road users. Continuing to keep both tunnels open with temporary lane restrictions in place and speed restrictions of 40km/h (4) .
sections.lessons_learned: Information not available in the public domain.
sections.innovation_opportunities: AI-powered fire suppression and predictive maintenance systems to enhance safety and efficiency. Smart traffic management using AI and automated incident response drones to improve congestion and emergency handling. Sustainable solutions like solar-powered ventilation and fire-resistant materials to boost resilience. Modular, plug-and-play infrastructure to allow seamless future upgrades, with IoT sensors to enable predictive maintenance. Enhanced communication through AI-driven virtual assistance and AR-based emergency training to improve driver safety. Smoke-filtering barriers and fire-resistant coatings to help mitigate wildfire risks.
