sections.challenge: • Heat stress is the largest natural disruptor to communities in the Greater Sydney Basin. Increasing heat is recognised as the largest risk to local populations and economies across Greater Western Sydney (1) . Climate change is projected to increase this risk. • Black asphalt has a low albedo (surface reflectivity) of just 0.03-0.04, with a high thermal mass and storage capacity for heat. Consequently, asphalt pavements absorb heat during the day making the surrounding areas hotter by elevating both daytime and overnight surface temperatures through the release of this heat (1) . This increases Urban Heat Island effects. • Unshaded asphalt road surfacing (pavement) and intensifying day and nighttime summer temperatures both create risks to environmental and public health, including heat-related illness, which are exacerbated by the Urban Heat Island effect. Higher temperatures contribute to increased energy consumption and GHG emissions in cities (1) . • Approximately a third of the outer suburbs of Sydney are thermally dense black asphalt, which can reach a surface temperature of 75°C (2) . • For 10 days in the summer of 2018/19 Parramatta City communities experienced daytime maximum air temperatures of over 35°C (1) . • The adaptation measure of ‘CoolSeal’ was trialled to reduce surface and air temperatures in Western Sydney.
sections.adaptation_measures: Measure implemented • CoolSeal was applied to asphalt as a surface coat. It is a water-based asphalt emulsion with a high reflectivity of 33% (1) . • Cool pavement technology increases the albedo of the asphalt pavement, reducing the Urban Heat Island effect (1) . • The trial sought to understand the performance of this adaptation measure in the height of a Sydney summer (6) . Organisations Western Sydney University; Blacktown City Council; Campbelltown City Council; and Parramatta City Council Transport sub-sector Roads First order climate hazard (cause) High temperatures (in city suburbs) Second order climate hazard (effect) Urban Heat Island (UHI) effect Transport asset impacted Road pavement Adaptation measure Cool pavement technology (CoolSeal) Location  Western Sydney, Australia Years of delivery 2019 - 2021 • In March 2020, 14,700m of road and carpark surfaces across 12 sites in the Blacktown, Campbelltown and Parramatta suburbs of Western Sydney were coated in CoolSeal (1) and the effects on temperatures monitored. Other options assessed • Casting shade on sun-exposed asphalt pavements through tree canopies or engineered structures can provide surface cooling (1) . • The planting of trees that can develop wide dense crowns can provide thermal benefits through transpiring water and reducing re-radiation of solar energy by providing shade (1) . However, often it is not feasible to plant trees in congested cities and due to planting restrictions, they often cannot provide shade across entire asphalt surfaces (6) . • Engineered structures, such as green or solar roofs, as well as fabric sails, can provide shade to car parks or roads (1) . However, engineered structures are cost-prohibitive when shading thousands of square metres of ground (6) .
sections.applicability: • This adaptation measure could be applicable to cities in the UK experiencing increased urban heat island effects due to intensifying summer heat. • In 2022 the UK summer heatwaves caused temperatures to exceed 40°C for the first time, contributing to wildfires in London and increased energy use. Hotspots in London were identified as 4.5°C warmer at night than the rural surroundings due to the urban heat island effect. • Heat-related deaths rose across the UK in the July record heatwave and an additional period of high temperatures in August, especially for over 65s (6) . • In London, heat-related deaths more than doubled from 2021 (158 estimated deaths) to 2022 (387 estimated deaths), primarily during these two periods of very hot weather (6) . • Application of CoolSeal throughout cities such as London may reduce urban heat and help alleviate the increasing heat stress (5) . • The measure may be applicable to other asphalt assets that contribute to the urban heat island effect. Thermal imagery showing the variance in surface temperatures of CoolSeal and asphalt roads, and the impact of tree canopy. (6)
sections.financials: Funding • Funding for the trial was provided by Blacktown City Council, Campbelltown City Council and Parramatta City Council (1) . Initial costs • The market in Australia for the product is limited, resulting in an expensive application of AU$15 m (2) (£7.37m). However, once applied at scale, such as in Los Angeles, the application cost decreases rapidly because a semi-manual application is replaced with a mechanical application (6) . Ongoing costs • Coatings must be reapplied every 6 to 8 years due to degradation causing a decline in reflectivity (4) . Avoided costs • Information not available in interview or publicly.
sections.resourcing: • The trial was carried out as a partnership between Western Sydney University, Blacktown City Council, Campbelltown City Council and Parramatta City Council (1) . • The trial required staff from each council to personally consult with each household in the residential streets of the trial sites. They distributed questionnaires, feedback forms and provided information about the trial (1) .
sections.co_benefits: Community *See ID 19 for community benefits applicable through the City of Phoenix’s Cool Pavements Programme. Decreased energy consumption and carbon reduction • If provided at scale, the adaptation measure would reduce the Urban Heat Island effect. This could reduce the use of air conditioning especially at night, lowering the cost of electricity and reducing GHG emissions (1) .
sections.evaluation: • The trial involved an environmental monitoring program collecting data between February 2020 and March 2021 to measure the effectiveness of the CoolSeal on cooling. The program took measurements of surface, air and black globe temperatures (also known as wet bulb temperatures that combine ambient air temperature, wind and humidity) before and after the application of CoolSeal and on nine uncoated control sites (1) . • The trial showed CoolSeal significantly reduced surface temperatures. On average during warm summer days surface temperatures of the coated pavements were 6°C cooler than uncoated pavements, but maximum reductions of 12°C were also recorded. During the night, 1-2°C surface temperature reductions were realised. The collected data also suggested that the cooling ability of CoolSeal increased proportionally to increasing surface temperatures (1) . • In contrast to a lowering of surface temperatures, mean ambient air temperature during the day and night was not systematically reduced and no cooling effect was measured (1) . • On sunny days, black globe temperatures increased by 2.7°C on coated pavements compared to uncoated sites due to increased surface reflectivity, resulting in decreased thermal comfort of pedestrians (1) . • Community feedback indicated they wanted further involvement and to understand why their street was or was not selected for the trial (6). • Community feedback also indicated there is a perception of increased slipperiness for pedestrians and increased glare from the coated pavements (1) . *See ID 19 for evaluation of the CoolSeal product applied in a city centre trial.
sections.challenges: • Some of the public used the newly coated streets as a ‘canvas’ for road burnouts, creating safety issues and degrading the coatings (6) . As a result, at the end of the trial, the council painted over the CoolSeal road due to demands from residents (2) . *See ID 19 for challenges experienced on a different cool pavements trial
sections.lessons_learned: • Optimal surface preparation is essential for the longevity of the product, to increase the bond between the polymer and road surface (7) . • To have an effect on urban air temperatures and provide urban cooling, the product needs to be provided at scale (7) . *See ID 19 for lessons learned on a similar pilot in the United States.
sections.innovation_opportunities: • Innovation opportunities lie in combining cool pavement coatings with strategic tree planting and engineered shading to enhance cooling. • Advancements in high-albedo coatings, such as infrared-reflective pigments and phase-change materials, could improve durability while reducing glare. • Smart infrastructure, including temperature-responsive coatings and embedded sensors, can optimise performance and maintenance. • Community engagement in street selection and anti-vandalism treatments can improve public acceptance and longevity. • A holistic approach integrating these elements will maximise urban cooling benefits.

sections.challenge: • The Los Angeles Metro Rail is an urban rail transit system serving Los Angeles County, California, United States (5) . • Metro Rail is owned and operated by the Los Angeles County Metropolitan Transportation Authority (Metro) (5) . • Metro relies on a wide range of assets to provide service to riders. Assets affected by climate risks include (5) : • Public-facing infrastructure maintained by Metro: this includes buses (BRT and other bus routes), rail (light and heavy rail lines and stations), bikes and bikeshare locations, park & ride locations and customer service centers. • Non-public-facing infrastructure maintained by Metro: this includes communication systems and non-public facilities that support public-facing assets, such as bus depots. • Other infrastructure not maintained by Metro: this includes assets Metro relies on for service such as roads and bus stops. • Climate change poses risks to many of Metro's services, impacting the communities and businesses that depend on them. Risks include more frequent and intense heat waves, intense rainfall, flooding, storms, wildfires, and other hazards (5) . • By mid-century, some of Metro’s assets are projected to be at high or extreme risk from certain climate hazards. If not addressed, this could result in significant operational disruption, increased costs, or localised infrastructure damage (5) . Organisation LA Metro Transport sub-sectors Rail; Highways; Light Rapid Transit First order climate hazards (cause) Heavy rainfall; Sea level rise; High temperatures; Flooding – surface water Second order climate hazards (effect) Rockfalls, landslides, avalanches; Water damage; Wildfire Transport assets impacted Track; Overhead lines Adaptation measures Hardening/protecting infrastructure; Adjusting operations/changing behaviours; Relocating existing or designing new infrastructure Location  Los Angeles, USA Years of delivery Ongoing
sections.adaptation_measures: • LA Metro undertakes are range of climate adaptation measures categorised as either Physical Adaptations or Adjustments to Operations/Changes in Behaviour. Physical Adaptation: These involve engineering solutions like raising rail segments in flood zones or using natural infrastructure, such as wetlands for flood control and vegetation for cooling (5) . Specific examples of implemented or in progress physical adaptations are: • In February 2024, construction began on a barrier wall to shield train tracks from the ongoing risk of landslides in San Clemente. Between 2021 and 2024, the line here had been closed five times due to debris from the cliffs above encroaching on the train tracks (1) . • The Rory M. Shaw Wetlands Park is being constructed on a former landfill site to address flood risk, reduce stormwater pollution and increase water conservation. In addition, the park will increase recreational opportunities for local residents and create a new wildlife habitat. This will be achieved by converting the 46-acre site into a multi-purpose wetlands park (2) . • On the light rail system, high heat days can cause the overhead power wires to sag which delays services and can lead to disruptive dewirements. Metro trialled alternative wire tensioning technology to overcome this problem, moving from traditional balance weights to spring tensioners (5) . In 2024, LA Metro secured $10.7 million funding (£8.3 million) to upgrade 27.2 directional miles of overhead catenary system with the spring tensioner system that will prevent sagging during high and extreme-heat events thus mitigating localised and cascading operational impacts (8) . Adjustments to Operations/Changes in Behaviour: For risks like flooding and wildfires, changes in day-to-day operations or behaviours are effective: • Operations can be adapted by rerouting buses or providing better rider alerts. • Metro’s Bus Operation Control Center coordinates with operators when a detour is needed and has “blue water” detours for when a portion of a route has been flooded due to heavy precipitation (5). . LA Metro A Line Overhead Catenary System (OCS) Resiliency Project Riverine Flooding Exposure Map
sections.applicability: • The UK faces some of the same climate risks as Los Angeles, (LA), particularly in terms of flooding, coastal erosion, rising sea levels, and infrastructure vulnerability, making aspects of Metro’s approach adaptable to the rail sector in the UK. • As a public body, Metro takes a wide range of factors into account when deciding which climate adaptations to implement. Collaboration with Metro staff and external partners, along with a review of vulnerable assets, prompted the decisions to take adaptation measures. Each option is weighed for cost, feasibility, and effectiveness, and will depend on whether the response is short-term (to address immediate disruptions) or long-term (to prevent future risks). As the Metro system grows, climate considerations will be systematically integrated into decision- making (5) . • Where possible, choices are made that mitigate risk and deliver wider objectives, as was the case with the Rory M. Shaw Wetlands Park (8) . This approach is applicable to UK public authorities.
sections.financials: Funding • The LA Metro uses innovative financing known as Green Bonds to fund projects with significant environmental and climate benefits. Upon issuance of the bonds, the Green Bonds proceeds are deposited in the Construction Fund Account established by the Trustee under the Supplemental Trust Agreement for that particular bond issue. The Los Angeles County Metropolitan Transportation Authority (LACMTA) provides instructions to the Trustee, as needed, to invest all the unallocated bond proceeds in the Construction Fund Account in securities permitted by the California Government Code. LACMTA’s Debt Policy also provides guidelines on investment of bond proceeds (6) . • LACMTA has issued two series of Green Bonds in 2017 and 2019. The US $471.4 million (£365 million) of Proposition A First Tier Senior Sales Tax Revenue Bonds, Series 2017-A (2017 Green Bonds) and the US $418.6 million (£324 million) of Proposition C Sales Tax Revenue Bonds, Senior Bonds Series 2019-A (Green Bonds) financed the development and construction of certain projects of LACMTA’s electrified light and heavy rail public transport systems and the purchase of electric rail vehicles thus meeting Criterion 3 the Climate Bonds Initiative’s Low Carbon Land Transport Eligibility Criteria (7) . Initial costs • The initial cost of implementing these adaptation measures, such as elevating rail segments, installing flood barriers, or reconstructing existing infrastructure, can be high, with estimates for similar projects ranging from hundreds of thousands to millions of pounds per segment, depending on the complexity and scale of the work (4) . Ongoing costs • Ongoing costs typically include maintenance of the hardened infrastructure (e.g., checking levees, updating drainage systems), monitoring and improving the green infrastructure (e.g., vegetation maintenance), and potential operational costs like rerouting buses or updating digital signage systems for better communication during extreme weather events (4) . Avoided costs • The avoided costs are more difficult to estimate but can be significant. By proactively addressing risks, LA Metro likely reduced costs associated with frequent repairs, service disruption, and even legal liabilities related to unsafe infrastructure (4) .
sections.resourcing: • To address climate-related risks effectively, Metro collaborates with a range of external partners who share its vision for climate action (5) . • Key collaborators include the City and County of Los Angeles, Caltrans, and other local and state agencies (5) . • These partnerships are crucial because many of the climate risks Metro faces, such as flooding and wildfires, are regional issues that require coordinated efforts to address. For example, decisions made by regional agencies about land use and transportation planning directly influence Metro’s ability to mitigate GHG emissions and adapt to climate impacts (5) .
sections.co_benefits: Environmental • By integrating nature-based solutions, such as using green infrastructure for flood control and planting vegetation for shading, the adaptation measures helped improve urban air quality, reduced the urban heat island effect, and provided habitat for wildlife (5) . • Green infrastructure can absorb rainwater, reducing runoff and contribute to improved water quality (5) . Community • Passengers benefit from increased system reliability, with fewer disruptions caused by extreme weather (5) . • Nature-based solutions, like tree planting for shading, also enhance passenger comfort by reducing heat exposure on platforms and waiting areas, improving the overall transit experience (5) . • LA Metro actively develop adaptations with community benefits, e.g. the Rory M. Shaw Wetlands Park (8) .
sections.evaluation: • Project benefits are tracked annually for the first two years post implementation and then every five years after that and recorded in an environmental management system (8) . • For instance, GHG emissions and displacement are among the ten sustainability indicators that are reported annually in the Energy & Resource Report. This report will continue to track important elements such as pilot projects, technology assessments, financial analyses, decision-making outcomes, and ongoing planning efforts. It not only highlights the successes but also identifies areas where challenges or barriers still exist, providing an opportunity to reassess technology choices, mitigation measures, implementation timelines, and goals (5) .
sections.challenges: Metro faces several challenges in implementing the adaptation measures, including: Resource constraints and prioritisation • Metro has established ambitious goals related to climate resilience, which are reducing GHG emissions to 79% below 2017 levels by 2030, achieving zero emissions by 2050 and creating a climate-resilient organisation and transit system that is prepared, ready and able to provide consistent services to the people of LA County (5) . • One of the main challenges Metro faces is the allocation of finite resources. The agency has to balance its ambitious climate adaptation goals with other critical initiatives and day-to-day operations, which lead to competing priorities for financial, intellectual, and creative capital (5) . • Ensuring that climate resilience measures were integrated while still fulfilling service obligations and being fiscally responsible required careful coordination (5) . Decision-making silos • Different departments at Metro have their own priorities, and accessing the critical information needed for decision-making requires effective interdepartmental coordination (5) . • In some cases, decisions that benefited one department's goal could conflict with the broader objectives of Metro or other departments, making it difficult to achieve optimal results across the entire agency (5) . Emerging technologies and uncertainty • Technology changes alter the way people travel, as seen with the advancements in self-driving cars, scooter-share programs, GPS and other tracking and communication devices and the rise in telecommuting (5) . • There are big risks in selecting and implementing a suite of adaptation actions all at once (5) . • Many of the technologies required for climate adaptation demand new infrastructure, operating procedures, and financing models (5) . • With rapid advancements, Metro faced the risk of investing in technologies that could become obsolete or require costly adjustments (5) . • The uncertainty surrounding the full-scale implementation of these technologies added complexity to the planning and execution process (5) .
sections.lessons_learned: • Leadership and Collaboration: The importance of strong leadership and fostering a culture of climate action across all levels of the organisation is crucial. This includes having clear roles and responsibilities and active engagement with both internal and external stakeholders to ensure buy-in and effective collaboration. Cross-departmental coordination and leveraging existing governance structures are essential for overcoming silos and achieving goals (5) . • Securing Funding and Resources: Effective climate action requires adequate financial resources and prioritisation within existing budgets. Leveraging partnerships with external agencies and the private sector can help secure additional funding and maximise the impact of climate-related initiatives. These partnerships can help address shared regional risks like flooding or wildfire and contribute to more comprehensive solutions (5) . • The integration of climate knowledge into decision-making and the continuous monitoring of adaptation efforts can help other transport agencies improve their own climate resilience strategies (5) . • The emphasis on stakeholder engagement and continuous evaluation ensures that adaptation measures remain effective (8) . • Improvements to the post-event response can be made by increasing climate hazard-related insurance coverage and/or setting up a fund for emergency response expenses. In response to the recent wildfires in LA, Better Angels has launched a rapid-response Emergency Assistance Fund to support low-income families that have been impacted by the fires (3) .
sections.innovation_opportunities: • Metro has an extensive, multi-modal network which creates the opportunity to view climate adaptations on a holistic rather than individual project basis. For example, data networks that support all modes can be combined and hardened to create a single, resilient network.

sections.challenge: • Dania Beach, a coastal community of 32,000 in Broward County, Florida, is increasingly vulnerable to climate hazards such as sea-level rise and coastal storms. These threats significantly jeopardise the area’s marine infrastructure, roads and public transport networks (4) . • Projections of future sea-level rise highlight the potential for severe impacts, including substantial damage to commercial properties, with ripple effects extending across Broward County and the wider Florida economy. The total sum of cumulative damages from permanent progressive sea level rise is projected to be $1,167,300,000 (£905,007,690) between 2030 and 2070 (2) .
sections.adaptation_measures: Measures implemented Dania Beach has implemented multiple adaptation projects under the broader Resilient Florida Program (1) : • Replacement of wastewater lift stations with modern submersible systems, including upgraded controls, emergency generators and sewer improvements. Wastewater lift stations are critical components of sewage infrastructure, designed to pump wastewater from lower to higher elevations when gravity alone isn't sufficient. They help transport sewage to treatment facilities, especially in flat or low-lying coastal areas like Broward County. The replacement of traditional lift stations with modern submersible systems enhances resilience in several ways: • Flood Resilience: Submersible pumps are fully sealed and designed to function underwater, reducing the risk of failure during flood events. • Improved Efficiency and Durability: These systems are more energy-efficient and require less maintenance than older technologies. • Emergency Preparedness: The upgrades include emergency generators, ensuring continuous operation during power outages caused by storms or hurricanes. Organisation Resilient Florida Program Transport sub-sectors Maritime; Roads First order climate hazards cause Sea level rise; Coastal storms; Flooding - coastal Second order climate hazards effect Water damage; Storm damage Transport assets impacted Roads; Building and warehouses; Port Structures Adaptation measures Drainage System Location  Dania Beach, Florida, USA Years of delivery 2018 - ongoing • Sewer System Improvements: The modernisation efforts also enhanced the overall capacity and reliability of the wastewater management system, reducing the risk of overflows and contamination during extreme weather events. • Installation of stormwater drainage systems to reduce flooding (3) : • Drainage Infrastructure: New stormwater pipes and catch basins to improve water flow and prevent ponding. • Earthworks & Dewatering: Excavation and grading to optimise drainage, along with dewatering techniques to manage groundwater levels. • Resilient Roadway Design: Restoring roads and driveways with flood-resistant materials. • Tree Management: Removing and replacing trees to prevent root intrusion into drainage systems while maintaining urban greenery. Dania Beach is developing a citywide Stormwater Master Plan as systematic approach to identify problem areas, implement targeted infrastructure improvements, and best management practices to alleviate flooding conditions that will align with city, county, state and federal efforts (6) .
sections.applicability: • Upgrades to submersible lift stations can help prevent wastewater system failures in flood- prone, low-lying coastal regions, particularly during storms or sea-level rise. These modern systems are designed to handle higher water levels and ensure that the pumping infrastructure remains operational even during extreme events. • Drainage systems that are designed to cope with increased rainfall or rising sea levels can alleviate urban flooding through improved wastewater management. This includes re- engineering existing drainage to handle larger volumes of water and incorporating green infrastructure solutions such as permeable pavements, vegetated swales, and bio-retention areas that allow water to filter into the ground naturally. • These adaptation measures are highly relevant to specific coastal regions in the UK, especially those facing similar challenges of flooding, sea-level rise and increased storm frequency due to climate change. Areas of the UK with the highest levels of applicability include the Southeast Coast (e.g., Kent, Sussex, and Essex), East Coast (e.g., Norfolk, Suffolk), and Southwest Coast (e.g., Cornwall and Devon). Example of submersible waste water pumps Dania Beach Marina in Dania Beach, FL, United States
sections.financials: Funding • Funding for these adaptation measures comes from a mix of federal, state, local and private sources. • The Resilient Florida Program is primarily funded by the federal government while short-term actions, such as clearing storm drains and maintaining pump stations, are covered by local government budgets (1) . Initial costs • The initial cost of works at lift stations 4, 11 and 12 & Phase III was US $8.34 million (£6.6 million) (1) . • The initial cost of works for the SW 52nd Street & SW 34th Terrace Stormwater Improvements was US $2.51 million (£2 million) (1) . Ongoing costs • These adaptation measures incur ongoing costs, such as routine maintenance and potential future upgrades. However, specific cost details are currently unavailable. Avoided costs • Stormwater system enhancements are designed to reduce the damage and associated cost of flood events. There is no data at present on the costs avoided by these schemes.
sections.resourcing: (2) The implementation of these adaptation measures required collaboration amongst various stakeholders, including local businesses, government agencies, and private contractors to deliver the physical works.
sections.co_benefits: • Modern Submersible Lift Stations (2) • Reduced Maintenance Needs: Newer systems are more durable and require fewer repairs and therefore trips to site, reducing long-term costs and infrastructure failures. • Stormwater Drainage Improvements (3) • Resilient Roadways & Driveways: By restoring flood-damaged roads with flood-resistant materials, transportation networks remain accessible after extreme weather events. • Green Infrastructure Benefits: Some elements, such as tree replacements and vegetation management, help improve natural water absorption and reduce urban heat island effects. Community • With reliable wastewater and drainage systems, hospitals, emergency services, and evacuation routes remain accessible during extreme weather events. • The presence of backup power systems in wastewater treatment infrastructure ensures public health and sanitation services continue if the mains power supply is compromised.
sections.evaluation: • No public information is available.
sections.challenges: • Securing adequate funding for large-scale infrastructure projects, particularly in the face of limited budgets at the local and state levels, posed a significant challenge. Climate adaptation projects are expensive and require substantial upfront investment, which can be difficult to justify without clear, immediate returns (2) . Dania Beach is developing a Storm Water Master Plan that phases the required adaptations and investment to better manage funding constraints (6) . • Aligning efforts across multiple levels of government (local, county, state, federal) and private- sector stakeholders can be a complex and time-consuming process that requires effective project management and stakeholder engagement (2) .
sections.lessons_learned: (2) Lessons learned include: • Long-term planning and investment are crucial because climate risks are ongoing, and adaptation needs to be forward-thinking to remain effective and minimise repeated interventions and costs over time. • Economic modelling and damage assessments provide the data-driven foundation to make decisions that maximise the effectiveness of adaptation measures and ensure cost- effectiveness. • Establishing accessible data platforms can help businesses and policymakers understand vulnerabilities, quantify potential risks, and develop effective adaptation strategies.
sections.innovation_opportunities: Dania Beach is currently developing a new scheme as part of ongoing wastewater management that uses injection wells to feed stormwater into the ground as opposed to the previous approach of moving water to an area where it can be discharged (5) . This approach uses flood waters as an asset to recharge groundwater supplies rather than simply discharging the excess water without capturing the benefit of it.

organisation: Qatar's Public Works Authority Pumping station and outfall tunnel

sections.challenge: • Although Qatar is one of the hottest countries in the world, it suffers from chronic flooding around Doha, facing increasing climate-related risks . Heavy rainfall often disrupts lives and damages homes, businesses, and infrastructure . • The Musaimeer Pumping Station and Outfall (MPSO) project aims to collect and sustainably discharge stormwater and groundwater from a 270km² catchment area around the capital city to prevent flooding of Doha and the nearby Hamad International Airport and surrounding roads 1,2,7 . • The MPSO ensures the continued operability of roads, bridges, and other transportation systems in the vicinity by keeping groundwater and surface water levels under control . These measures reduce the risk of surface water flooding and ground instability that could undermine roads and rail  tracks .  Without  these  measures,  prolonged  flooding  could  lead  to  structural  damage, increased maintenance costs, and a decline in the reliability of the transportation network . • By ensuring a functional and safe transportation network, the Musaimeer project contributes to economic resilience and supports Qatar’s broader goals of sustainable development . The MPSO is a cornerstone of Qatar’s sustainable development strategy, directly contributing to several United   Nations   Sustainable   Development   Goals   (SDGs)   through   innovative   practices, environmental  stewardship,  and  robust  infrastructure  development .  For  example,  the  project directly  contributes  to  SDG  9,  “Industry,  Innovation,  and  Infrastructure”  through  its  focus  on building resilient and sustainable infrastructure .
sections.adaptation_measures: Measure implemented Southwestern Doha has experienced rapid growth in recent years, prompting the need to increase the area’s drainage capacity to cope with stormwater runoff and dewatering from construction activities . This project is part of Qatar’s National Vision 2030, which aims to strengthen the country’s climate resilience . A pumping station with capacity of 19.7m /s was installed south of Hamad International Airport in Doha, along with a 10km long under-seabed outfall tunnel with an internal diameter of 3.7m . The undersea outfall tunnel is designed to discharge stormwater and groundwater . These flood mitigation measures consider climate change projections and are designed to address flooding risks from a 1 in 50-year storm event . Organisation Qatar’s Public Works Authority Transport sub-sector Aviation; Highways; Railways First order climate hazards (cause) Heavy rainfall; Surface water flooding Second order climate hazard (effect) Water damage; Road pavement; Rail track; Structures Asset impacted Airport services Adaption measures Pumping station and outfall tunnel Location  Doha, Qatar Years of delivery 2018 - 2022 Other options assessed • The Hamad International Airport is located in the Municipality of Al-Wakrah, which has emerged as a rapidly growing hub for residential and commercial activity . Given the potential risks of flooding associated with a 25-year storm event and the projected population growth, the decision to invest in a stormwater pumping station was deemed financially viable by the State of Qatar . • During the concept design phase, 22 design alternatives were identified and assessed for stormwater and groundwater disposal from the Abu Hamour Tunnel Catchment . For example, several land-based discharge options were explored, including discharge to natural or man- made depressions . While technically feasible, these options were unsuitable for flood risk management and did not align with the project’s goals of providing sustainable drainage solutions . • The creation of evaporation lakes, relying on natural processes to manage stormwater, was considered but dismissed due to prohibitive costs related to land acquisition and the challenges of maintaining ecological balance . This option also lacked scalability to handle significant storm events . • The evaluation of the 22 design alternatives followed a rigorous methodology considering technical feasibility, environmental impacts, cost implications, implementation timelines, and regulatory compliance . Ensuring that the designs were practical and could meet the project’s operational needs was a key priority . Environmental impacts were assessed to evaluate potential ecological disruptions during construction and operation . Balancing capital expenditures with long-term operational costs was another important factor . Prioritising solutions that could be executed efficiently and aligning with Qatari environmental laws and international standards were critical components of the assessment . Each criterion was weighted equally, allowing for a balanced evaluation . • The design alternatives and the chosen design were provided through a comprehensive initial Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) for the Ministry of Municipality and Environment's approval . As the regulatory body, the Ministry of Municipality and Environment reviewed the EIA to ensure the final chosen design adhered to marine discharge legislation, verifying that the outfall complied with standards and maintained water quality . Their review focused on minimising ecological impacts, advocating for features such as diffuser systems to enhance pollutant dispersion effectively . • The MPSO project was selected for its energy-efficient and sustainable infrastructure design, focusing on reducing power consumption, especially during dry weather when power demand is low . Dispersion modelling demonstrated that the proposed outfall would minimise pollutant concentration and ecological impact . The location was selected for its accessibility and ability to integrate into existing infrastructure without extensive land acquisition . Additionally, the design adhered to environmental regulations, including provisions for sediment control and biodiversity protection . These measures align with Qatar’s broader sustainability goals .
sections.applicability: These insights can enhance the planning and sustainability of adaptation projects in the transport sector, including as part of wider urban planning and development. The UK faces significant challenges with flooding, which impacts the transport sector. Pumping stations can play a crucial role in managing these flood risks. The adaptation strategies outlined in this case study are relevant to large-scale infrastructure projects, as they can significantly enhance the resilience of transport assets and infrastructure. Specific elements of this case study may also be applicable, for example the co-benefit of water management and reduced costs for construction dewatering.
sections.financials: Funding The State of Qatar has funded the MPSO project, allocating resources from its annual infrastructure budget 7. The primary source of this funding comes from Qatar's hydrocarbon exports 7. This investment highlights a strategic use of hydrocarbon revenues to mitigate the potential impacts of flooding while supporting climate resilience 7. Initial costs The design and construction costs for the MPSO were nearly £219 million (QAR 993,716,110*) . Ongoing costs After three years of operation, the operation and maintenance costs were just over £2.6 million (QAR 12,015,609*) . Avoided costs One of MPSO’s objectives was to identify opportunities for the project to achieve cost savings without compromising on quality, health, safety, and environment . • Nearly £2.5 million (QAR 11.3 million*) was saved through the reuse of over 450,000 tonnes of spoil . • Just over £1 million (QAR 5,000,000*) was saved through the selection of an alternative Tunnel Boring Machine manufacturer . The MPSO is currently early in its Operation & Maintenance phase, and the estimation of avoided costs for climate risks impacting infrastructure is still under assessment 7. While a formal cost-benefit analysis is pending, it is reasonable to infer that avoided costs include 7: • Infrastructure repair and maintenance: Preventing damage to roads, buildings, and other infrastructure that would have resulted from flooding. • Economic disruptions: Avoiding losses from halted transportation, business operations, and delayed construction projects. • Public safety and health costs: Reducing risks of waterborne diseases and accidents associated with flooded areas. • Reduced construction costs: Managing saline groundwater levels has likely decreased dewatering expenses for ongoing and future projects in the region. Economic impacts • The MPSO project has generated significant economic benefits, contributing to Qatar’s sustainable development and resilience 7. One major benefit is flood mitigation and property protection . The project reduces flooding risks resulting from excessive rainfall events in vulnerable areas, minimizing damage to infrastructure, properties, and public utilities . This could be quantified by comparing historical flood-related damage costs, such as repair expenses and insurance claims, before and after the project's implementation . Furthermore, the project increases the long-term value of land and properties in the area by protecting them from flooding . This protection encourages further residential, commercial, and industrial development, simulating economic growth and enhancing the quality of life for residents . • The project enhances economic resilience by safeguarding key transportation and trade infrastructure 7. The MPSO ensures uninterrupted access to critical transportation hubs, supporting Qatar’s role as a regional trade and logistics centre . Reliable infrastructure is essential for maintaining economic activity and global competitiveness, and this project plays a vital role in securing these outcomes . • Another economic benefit is the reduction in construction costs . By managing saline groundwater levels, the pumping station lowers the expenses associated with dewatering during construction . • The project has also stimulated economic activity through job creation during its construction and operational phases . This not only supports local employment but also enhances the skills and expertise of Qatar’s workforce, fostering capacity for future infrastructure projects .
sections.resourcing: The MPSO project involved a joint venture between Hamad Bin Khalid Contracting Company and PORR Qatar Construction Company . It also included collaboration with Ashgal, the Public Works Authority in Qatar, and Mott MacDonald, who oversaw the design, implementation, and management of the project .
sections.co_benefits: • The MPSO project integrated sustainability co-benefits into its design, construction, and operational phases . These measures were intentionally planned to increase resource efficiency, reduce waste, and align with wider sustainability standards, including the United Nations’ Sustainable Development Goals . • An environmental co-benefit was achieved through the reuse of materials; notably, 100% of the marine material that was dredged was used for land reclamation in the Al Khor municipality . Additionally, steel and metal scraps were recycled through local facilities, including Qatar Steel . • The local community benefited as the project employed local people and companies for dredging, marine works, and maintenance services . Another co-benefit was the training provided to local university students by Mott MacDonald . Active travel also benefited, as excess concrete was repurposed for pedestrian pavements . • Additionally, by reducing saline groundwater levels, the project contributed to lowering the carbon footprint of future construction projects through decreased reliance on energy- intensive dewatering processes .
sections.evaluation: The MPSO project adhered to strict regulatory requirements, including those set by Qatari authorities and international environmental guidelines . The Project followed ISO 14001:2015 Overseeing construction in Doha Arial view of the Musaimeer Pumping Station and Outfall certification for its Environmental Management System (EMS) and was evaluated against the CEEQUAL sustainability rating scheme . During dredging operations, there were regular marine water quality assessments and turbidity monitoring as key environmental compliance efforts . The project achieved a CEEQUAL rating of Very Good and a 31% reduction in embodied carbon emissions . The MPSO has successfully operated through two rainy seasons to date, with no flooding events recorded in the area it serves . For example, evaluation of the MPSO’s operation during a storm in Doha in November 2023 found that the wet weather flow was successfully managed and almost within the design capacity . This suggests that the project has effectively mitigated the potential damages and disruptions that could have occurred without its implementation .
sections.challenges: • The MPSO project faced challenges such as limited space for storing excavated material from the 10km tunnel and high water pressure conditions requiring strict daily planning . • Limited laboratory availability for seawater sampling caused delays in environmental compliance . • The late finalisation of sustainability certification also led to project delays .
sections.lessons_learned: • Key lessons learned include integrating CEEQUAL certification objectives into the project brief to avoid delays and defining sustainability certifications during the tendering phase. • Early partnerships with accredited specialists are essential for projects near sensitive ecosystems. • Incorporating sustainability features like permeable pavements and green bridges helps to ensure compliance with certifications. • Identifying environmental KPIs in the contractor’s contract is crucial for accountability . • Investing in mobile onsite laboratories for real-time tests can be beneficial . It could also be beneficial to invest in mobile or modular onsite laboratories to conduct real-time tests for transport projects based on asphalt quality and emissions monitoring .
sections.innovation_opportunities: The efficiency and sustainability of MPSO could be further enhanced through innovative strategies for flow re-use and power consumption optimisation . MPSO already incorporates advanced energy-saving measures like Variable-Frequency Drives (VFDs), but there are additional areas where efficiency and sustainability enhancements are being actively discussed . One of the key opportunities lies in water reuse, which is a topic currently under consideration in Qatar . Technologies like membrane bioreactors (MBRs) and advanced filtration systems could be employed to treat stormwater to the necessary standards . This would align with Qatar’s broader goals of optimizing water resources in its arid environment . Such discussions are crucial given Qatar’s water scarcity challenges and the high energy costs associated with desalination .

organisation: NSW Government Climate resilient transport for Sydney Metro rail system

sections.challenge: Sydney has been experiencing the effects of climate change, with an increase in bushfires in forested areas and increased temperatures causing heatwaves and urban heat islands (1) . These impacts are expected to continue, with an average temperature increase between 1 o C – 1.8 o C projected by 2050, and 1.2 o C – 3.4 o C by 2090 (1) . The North West Metro line runs from Rouse Hill to Chatswood. The North West Metro has been impacted by these effects, with flooding, heat waves and other extreme weather events being recorded as the three most relevant climate risks to the sector (2) . These can cause issues such as discomfort for passengers due to high temperatures experienced on the Metro (2) .
sections.adaptation_measures: Measure implemented Climate risk was considered from the earliest stages of the project, using short (2030); medium (2070); and long-term (2100) time scales to model predicted low, medium, and high climate risks. Therefore, adaptation measures were implemented to support comfortable, reliable, and safe services for the next 100 years (2) . Ventilation systems were installed in the tunnels and stations to ensure customer comfort during heat waves. The Tunnel Ventilation System was made up of tunnel axial fans. The fans are designed to efficiently move air through tunnels and ventilation systems, to promote air circulation, remove stagnant air and facilitate the exchange of fresh outdoor air with indoor air – creating a comfortable indoor environment (14) . Although the primary objective of these fans is to decrease discomfort from high temperatures, should a fire occur due to high temperatures, they have been designed to withstand temperatures of 300 degrees Celsius for up to 2 hours (9) . To support the ventilation system, 17 individual compressed air systems were installed in separate plant rooms throughout the tunnel network. Each plant room is a dedicated space to contain the ventilation equipment (16) . Each compressed air system is made up of the following components to produce, store, and OrganisationGovernment of New South Wales Transport sub-sectorRail First order climate hazard (cause)High temperatures Second order climate hazard (effect)Fire Transport asset impactedRailway Adaptation measureVentilation systems Location Sydney, Australia Year of delivery2019 distribute compressed air (10) : oTwo compressors: Converts power to energy by compressing and pressurising air which can be released in quick bursts on command (15) . oTwo air receivers: Helps to store compressed air in a tank to meet peak demand events, ensuring a steady flow of air (15) . oSelection of pneumatic control panels: Helps to control the tunnel’s air flow (10) . Ventilation systems have also been installed as part of the Track Exhaust system (9) . This involved the installation of over-track exhaust systems, which can withstand the effects of fire without collapsing. These systems extract smoke and hot gases in the event of a fire, enabling the safe evacuation of passengers without the risk of smoke inhalation.
sections.applicability: This adaptation measure is applicable to the rail sector/underground metro system in the UK which is also vulnerable to overheating during heatwaves. The UK is similarly experiencing an increase in the frequency and intensity of heatwaves, which cause passenger discomfort in metro stations. These approaches are most applicable to the design of new metro systems, rather than retrofitting existing infrastructure.
sections.financials: Funding The Sydney Metro North West was fully funded by the New South Wales Government (4) . Initial costs The Sydney Metro North West cost £5.7 billion (3) . Ongoing costs Information not available in the public domain. Avoided costs Although no specific data is available on avoided costs with this project, more generally for the Sydney region the impacts from climate change have already cost the state an estimated £2.8 billion per year (6) . As a result, there is an increasing need to improve the resilience of physical assets, such as North West Metro, in order to reduce future damage costs (6) . Economic impacts The improved conditions of the Metro have supported over 12 million customer journeys, which have been undertaken within the first six months of the Metro’s opening (2) .
sections.resourcing: From 2011, the following companies and architects acted as the Technical Advisors for the Engineering, Rail Systems, Architecture ERSA (Error Resilient System Architecture designed to maintain functionality during failures (17) ), and Rapid Transit Integration (RTI) components (3) : AECOM WSP COX Grimshaw The team undertook design works to produce a concept design, reference design and contractual documentation for the project. Under separate contracts, AECOM was also engaged to provide the following services (3) : Environmental assessments Planning approvals Perform the role of Operations, Trains and Systems (OTS) Works Technical Requirements Manager, which ensures compliance against all contract requirements (18) . Delivery coordination Product assurance
sections.co_benefits: The fans were equipped with high efficiency anti-blocking rings to protect the fans from electromagnetic disturbances from the trains. These are devices which help to protect the fans from train induced pressures which can otherwise cause the fans to stall (9 and 13) . This allowed for safe operation while maintaining high efficiency, leading to lower energy consumption (10) .
sections.evaluation: The Sydney Metro North West has been in running since May 2019, and within the first six months of its opening, more than 12 million customer journeys were undertaken (2) . In the five years since its opening, the Metro North West Line has maintained an average Customer Satisfaction rating of 98% and a service running reliability of over 99% (5) . Due to the success of the Metro system, the network is being extended under Sydney Harbour, through new underground stations in the Sydney Central Business District, and beyond to Bankstown in the South West. It will also link the new Western Sydney airport with the rest of Sydney (2) .
sections.challenges: No challenges have been reported with the implementation of the project’s adaptation measures, which were incorporated into the design from the earliest stages of the project (3) .
sections.lessons_learned: Anti-stall rings installations All the fans installed on this project were fitted with high efficiency anti-stall rings. These were able to protect the ventilation fans from train induced pressures (9) . As a train travels along a tunnel it drives air down the tunnel. This creates pressure pulses on the ventilation fans, driving the fan to a higher pressure operating point (13) . If the operating point is beyond the fan’s pressure capability it can stall. Fitting the fans with an anti-stall ring can stabilise it and prevent this from occurring (13) . This has allowed the ventilation fans to operate safely at a low power consumption (9) .
sections.innovation_opportunities: Space limitations may be an issue, but it would be worth investigating whether axial fans or compressed air systems such as those used in North West Metro could be applied to existing stations/metro lines or infrastructure outside of metros which currently experiences uncomfortably hot temperatures.

organisation: Bradford Metropolitan Council Trees as stormwater attenuation and treatment pits
measures: Trees as stormwater attenuation and treatment pits

sections.challenge: The Bradford District has experienced significant flooding, with approximately 7,250 properties in the Bradford District at risk from surface water flooding for a rainfall event with a 1 in 200 chance of occurring (1)(2) . One particular town in the Bradford District, Keighley, experiences drainage-related issues, caused by strain on the combined sewer capacity. Between November and December 2015, river and surface water flooding primarily affected Keighley when a significant portion of the catchment's rainfall occurred in this area (1) .
sections.adaptation_measures: Measure implemented Six Field Maples with large soil volume tree pits were planted on North Street during road network alterations. These GreenBlue Urban ArborFlow tree pits were planted as stormwater attenuation and treatment pits to relieve pressure on the Yorkshire Water combined sewer system amid increased storm events and rainfall due to climate change (4)(5) . In the ArborFlow system the surface water runoff from the carriageway passes through a stone layer above the tree pit before filtering down through the Arborsoil Hydro soil mix and tree roots. The water then passes through a clean stone layer and underdrain to a stormwater drain (4) . This Sustainable Drainage Systems (SuDS) decreases the speed of water flow allowing the drainage system to cope with major storm events. The Arborflow tree pit also provides pollutant filtration, stopping road run-off pollutants from reaching the combined sewer network (4)(5) . The tree pits were estimated to accommodate a total storage capacity of 30.81cu.m before secondary infiltration by the surrounding rock layers or tree absorption. Based on this the joint tree pits should accommodate peak rainfall for three and a half hours for a 1 in 30-year storm surge event (4) . OrganisationBradford Metropolitan Council Transport sub-sectorRoads First order climate hazards (cause)Heavy Rainfall; Storms and High Winds; Flooding - surface Water Second order climate hazard (effect)None identified Transport asset impactedRoad pavement Adaptation measureTrees as stormwater attenuation and treatment pits (SuDS) Location Bradford, UK Year of delivery2018
sections.applicability: This adaptation measure is applicable to other transport sectors that are vulnerable to surface water flooding/heavy rainfall and are suitable for tree planting within and around the perimeter of the asset. It would be applicable to road pavement and foot or cycle paths, including the highways within other transport sector sites, such as approach roads and paths, and car parks. It would be applicable to other areas within the UK that experience surface water flooding, particularly in areas with poor drainage, where drainage systems become easily overwhelmed. For instance, Cardiff experiences flash flooding from severe rainfall exacerbated by non-porous infrastructure and strains on the drainage system (3) .
sections.financials: Funding Information not publicly available. Initial costs Information not publicly available. Ongoing costs No significant maintenance regimen required for successful implementation (5) . Avoided costs Information not publicly available.
sections.resourcing: City of Bradford Metropolitan District Council worked with SIG Geotechnical ltd and GreenBlue Urban (5) . SIG Geotechnical Ltd were the contractors on the project to install the SuDs, Bradford Metropolitan District Council acted as the landscape architect and GreenBlue Urban utilised SuDs, Keighley, North Street, 2018 Cross section of Keighley SuDs tree pit their designed tree pit system; ArborFlow (2)(3) .
sections.co_benefits: Environmental The implemented SuDS effectively remove surface run-off contaminants, so they do not pollute the combined sewer network (5) . The Field Maples planted contribute to improving biodiversity, reducing air pollution, reducing street temperatures and reducing noise pollution. Evaluation showed pollutants such as heavy metals and hydrocarbons have not affected the tree health nor limited growth of these tree species, allowing them to realise these co-benefits (5)(7) . There is a direct correlation between tree vigour and water ingress. Tree pits with the largest share of the surface water run-off have the trees with the fastest canopy growth, providing shade and supporting biodiversity (8) . Carbon reduction The ArborFlow tree pit system is manufactured from 100% recycled materials (6) .
sections.evaluation: In 2020, GreenBlue Urban and IROS ARB tested sap flow and vapour pressure deficit (VPD) on the trees. The results showed high sap flow and low VPD, showing strong tree vigour and indicating a very successful root establishment in the ArborFlow tree pits. The strong health of the trees is directly proportional to the efficacy of the trees in reducing air pollution, improving biodiversity, reducing noise pollution, providing cooling benefits, combating surface water flooding and reducing flow rates (5) . The healthy trees have been a key step in Bradford Metropolitan District Council’s goal of improving the district’s resilience to climate change (5) . In 2023 Manchester Metropolitan University students collected samples to determine the road run-off filtering efficacy of the tree pits. It evidenced that the SuDS tree pits were effective at filtering pollutants without any significant maintenance regimen (5) .
sections.challenges: Information not publicly available.
sections.lessons_learned: Field Maples are an effective choice of tree species for SuDS (7) . ArborFlow tree pits are evidenced to encourage tree health allowing effective drainage (7) . The tree pits are also effective at filtering out pollutants from surface water (5) .
sections.innovation_opportunities: Given the success of this adaptation measure, it would be worth determining where else in the UK, Field Maples and the ArborFlow system would be an effective addition to SuDS, and how advisable it would be to scale up this approach, in consultation with ecologists. The approach to evaluating the adaptation measure also demonstrates innovation, in terms of the detail of the information collected and the focus on the combined Field Maple/ArborFlow performance.

measure_title: Network Rail has implemented a range of adaptation measures to address climate hazards and
measure_description: Network Rail has implemented a range of adaptation measures to address climate hazards and

sections.challenge: Network Rail's drainage system is critical for managing water flow and preventing flooding on the UK railway network. Climate hazards such as heavy and intense rainfall lead to flooding, which can cause significant disruption to rail services. These hazards accelerate the deterioration of earthworks, put pressure on drainage systems and increase the likelihood of landslides and track damage. The impact of these climate hazards is substantial, affecting passengers, freight customers and local communities by causing delays, cancellations and safety risks. oFrom 2006/07 to 2020/21, over 322,000 weather-related incidents caused 26 million delay minutes resulting in over £1 billion in compensation payments. Within this, flood incidents cost £225 million in compensation , reaching £55 million in 2023/24 alone. oFrom 2021 to 2024, extreme weather, including severe storms and record temperatures (Britain's highest recorded temperature of 40.3°C in 2022), caused significant disruption, contributing 9.3 million weather-related delay minutes and £370 million in compensation over the period. The level of disruption experienced highlighted the need for proactive investment in resilience measures to mitigate financial and operational losses.
sections.adaptation_measures: Network Rail has implemented a range of adaptation measures to address climate hazards and enhance railway resilience. In response to heavy rain and surface water and fluvial flooding impacts, this has included: Drainage upgrades: Over 600,000 metres of the track drainage network will be built or rebuilt by 2029 to mitigate flooding risks. Addressing over 20,000 cuttings and embankments, with more than 300 miles strengthened through renewal and refurbishment, and over 900 miles undergoing planned maintenance. OrganisationNetwork Rail Transport sub-sectorRailways First order climate hazards causeHeavy rainfall; Surface water flooding; Fluvial flooding Second order climate hazard effectWater damage Transport assets impactedSignalling and telecommunications; Track; Structures (bridges) Adaptation measuresDrainage renewal and refurbishment; Slope stabilisation; Enhanced maintenance; Remote condition monitoring Location United Kingdom Years of delivery2014-2024 (Control Period 5 & 6) Expanding the deployment of advanced ‘smart’ movement sensors on cuttings and embankments to provide early warnings of instability, allowing engineers to respond proactively and potentially prevent full landslips. Installing CCTV at high-risk flooding locations to enhance monitoring and enable faster, more effective responses during adverse weather events. Slope stabilisation: Reinforcement of embankments and erosion control measures to reduce landslip risks. Enhanced maintenance: Increased maintenance of vulnerable track locations and overhead line equipment (OLE) systems to combat flooding impacts (alongside high-temperature impacts to OLE). Remote monitoring: Installation of Remote Condition Monitoring (RCM) systems for proactive asset management and early issue detection. Rock armour: Improved ballast protection to minimise washouts and accelerate post-flood recovery. Flood risk mitigation: Key sites like Dalmarnock, Drem, and Penmanshiel have been upgraded with attenuation systems, siphon chambers and new drainage infrastructure during CP5 (2014–2019). Project Examples : Anglian Route – Bacton Toe Drainage Adaptation Measures: Renewed toe drainage connecting the culvert to an existing system, included toe support for slope stabilisation, and cleared a watercourse section. Thrimby, Cumbria - North West & Central – River Leith Scour Adaptation Measures: A collaborative project to reduce flood risks and protect the West Coast Main Line by restoring 740 meters of the River Leith to a 1.01-kilometer meandering course, moving it from the railway. The work reconnected six hectares of floodplain, created wetland scrapes, and increased the river's length by 33%. North & East Route – Rotherham Central Station Adaptation Measures: Installed non-return valves, reprofiled retention chambers, contributed to a flood barrier on the Rotherham Canal, raising flood protection to a 1:100-year return period. Future work planned to slow upstream flows. Future priorities focus on integrating climate adaptation into policies including a £2.8bn investment to enhance the resilience of tracks, drainage and earthworks against extreme weather and supporting ongoing research and development to improve weather resilience. Network Rail is also developing long-term resilience strategies, delivering WRCCA Plans for CP7 (2024–2029) and advancing research on geospatial risks and interdependencies.
sections.applicability: The interventions deployed in this case study could be used in other transport sectors, such as roads, light rail and inland waterways, by implementing similar drainage systems, slope stabilisation measures and real-time monitoring technologies.
sections.financials: Funding Network Rail’s Control Period 7 (CP7) (2024–2029) funding totals £45.3 billion and is derived from government grants, track access payments and commercial income. It includes around £2.8 billion in activities and technology that will help it better cope with extreme weather and climate change. Initial costs The adaptation measures such as drainage upgrades, slope stabilisation, enhanced maintenance and RCM system installations required substantial investment. While CP6 (2019–2024) does not detail the precise cost of these measures, CP7 includes a £2.8 billion allocation specifically for drainage systems. Anglian Route – Bacton Toe Drainage - Cost: £125,000 Thrimby, Cumbria - North West & Central – River Leith Scour - Cost: £200,000 (co-funded by Network Rail and the Environment Agency) North & East Route – Rotherham Central Station - Cost: exact figure unavailable Avoided costs Investment in adaptation aims to reduce disruption costs, including delays, compensation payments and repair expenses. Network Rail’s 2024 annual report highlights significant progress and despite a trend of extreme weather events increasing in frequency and severity, delay minutes due to extreme weather have decreased from 4.8 million in 2013 to 3.4 million in 2023. Although 2021/22 and 2023/24 rank among the top five years for weather-related delay minutes, the long-term trend presents a relative decrease. Latest data shows a 3.5% reduction in average annual delay minutes , decreasing from 1.73 million (2006–2021) to 1.67 million (2006–2024), of which resilience efforts in mitigating the impact of increasingly extreme weather events are likely to contribute. Economic impacts Ongoing operating costs include routine maintenance, regular inspections and updates to RCM systems to ensure the sustainability of these adaptation measures. Despite these efforts, weather- related disruption continues to incur significant costs, estimated at £50–£100 million annually for delays and cancellations alone. When factoring in maintenance and repair expenses, the total financial impact of weather events is projected to range between £200–£300 million per year.
sections.resourcing: The project involved collaboration between various teams within Network Rail, including the Technical Authority (TA) Environment and Sustainability team, the chief engineer's team and the National Weather team. External consultants and contractors were engaged to provide specialist expertise and support.
sections.co_benefits: Observed Co-benefits: The adaptation measures for heavy rainfall and flooding impacts have yielded environmental and operational benefits, including improved water management, reduced flooding risks and enhanced safety and reliability for both passengers and freight users. Planned Co-benefits: Sustainability has been prioritised through the use of recycled materials and nature-based solutions, such as wetland restoration and rainwater capture, which enhance the overall benefits of the measures. Additionally, nature-based projects, such as the £200,000 wetland initiative to reduce scour on the River Leith in Network Rail’s Northwest & Central region, have been funded to enhance railway resilience. This project mitigated scour risks by redirecting the threat away from the railway line. It also improved flood resilience by reconnecting six hectares of floodplain and creating an additional 0.2 hectares of wetland scrapes for floodplain storage.
sections.evaluation: Network Rail evaluates their impact through regular inspections, performance metrics and RCM systems, though assessment of the effectiveness of projects is challenging due to the variability in weather event intensity and frequency, and other factors that may contribute to delay minutes and other metrics.
sections.challenges: The main challenges faced during the implementation of these adaptation measures included securing funding, coordination across different business units and ensuring the effectiveness of the measures in varying climate conditions. These were addressed by aligning adaptation efforts with long-term investment priorities, establishing clear leadership in overseeing climate change adaptation efforts, while advanced monitoring tools, such as RCM systems, helped adapt measures to evolving climate conditions.
sections.lessons_learned: Key lessons learned include the importance of comprehensive risk assessments, the need for ongoing maintenance and the value of real-time monitoring systems. These lessons can be applied to future adaptation projects in the transport sector.
sections.innovation_opportunities: There are opportunities for innovation in this space, such as the development of more advanced RCM systems for specific weather conditions and condition response of assets of concern, the use of AI for predictive maintenance and the integration of nature-based solutions for enhanced resilience.

measure_description: Balancing ponds managing drought and heavy rainfall events.
case_study_text: Constructed balancing ponds to control water volume entering drainage systems. Bundled with wider infrastructure programme kept costs minimal.

sections.challenge: • The River Crane flows from north to south to the east of Heathrow Airport. The river system is very prone to flash flooding during heavy rainfall events (7) . The majority of Heathrow Airport lies within the River Crane catchment. • The floodplain of the river contains a staff car park, customer park and ride, as well as a dual carriageway and causeway that serve as access routes to the airport. The airport terminals and runways are outside of the floodplain. • Surface water runs off the nearby, upstream dual carriageway during heavy rainfall, increasing the flooding downstream (7) . • Hardstanding areas and artificial drainage systems along the river result in rainfall reaching the channels very quickly, increasing the risk of flooding after heavy rainfall but also drought vulnerability during summer periods of reduced rainfall (7) . • Drought events result in a lack of water for fire-fighting and other essential airport services (7) .
sections.adaptation_measures: Measures implemented • Balancing ponds have been constructed to adapt to drought and heavy rainfall events (4) . • Balancing ponds control the volume and rate of rainwater entering a drainage system (4) . This reduces the risk of the system becoming overwhelmed and flooding after heavy rainfall, as well as alleviating water stress on airport services during drought periods (4) . • Approximately 57,000m of the 189,500m total water volume contained by the ponds can be controlled by Heathrow adjust the tilting weirs (1) . • Prior to storm events, the ponds can be drained. This allows the rainwater to flow through the river initially, before entering and filling the ponds once more (7) . • Sheet pile retaining walls have been installed around the edges of the balancing ponds for increased structural support (1) . • Tilting weirs have been constructed to replace the older penstock designs and improve water flow rate control (7) . Tilting weirs are commonly used in conservation areas to control water Organisation Heathrow Airport Ltd Transport sub-sector Aviation First order climate hazards (cause) Drought; Heavy rainfall; Flooding – fluvial; Flooding - surface water Second order climate hazard (effect) Water damage Transport assets impacted Access routes; Airport services Adaptation measure Balancing ponds Location  London, United Kingdom Year of delivery 2016 - 2022 levels, as the weir can be moved down to increase the flow and moved up to decrease it. They also help to reduce blockages by allowing debris to float over without interrupting water flow (7) . • A relief channel linking the balancing ponds and River Crane was created for water discharge (7) . • Gabions along the edge of the relief channel between the balancing ponds and River Crane provide structural support while using less concrete (7) . • Reed planting around the balancing ponds assists with filtration of water pollutants, strengthening of soil and enhanced performance of the system (4) . • A Moving Bed Biofilm Reactor (MBBR) biological wastewater treatment plant has been installed at the balancing ponds to remove runoff pollutants from the airport (3) . Other options considered • Space constraints in Heathrow Airport resulted in a Nature-based Solution (NbS) being preferable over larger grey infrastructure measures (7) . • A dam was considered but not implemented due to the associated costs not being justifiable to Heathrow (7) .
sections.applicability: • Further improvement of the tilting weir system over traditional penstocks will be useful for other sites and sectors (7) .
sections.financials: Funding • Heathrow Airport Ltd Initial costs • Sheet pile retaining walls (outer edges of the ponds) – approx. £2,100,000 (1) . • The balancing ponds were integrated into Heathrow Airport business development activities so additional climate adaptation costs were minimal (7) . • Other information not available during interview and not publicly available. Heathrow balancing ponds site map https://democraticservices.hounslow.gov.uk/documents/s135798/Hea throw%20Balancing%20Resevoir.pdf River Crane flood risk map with Heathrow Airport shown and the balancing ponds highlighted. https://storymaps.arcgis.com/collections/8b605637f79048519b3dcabe95 c4f582?item=5 Ongoing costs • Minimal water monitoring and sampling costs. • Heathrow Airport is working with Met Office representatives onsite to receive weather data prior to storm events and react accordingly (7) . • Detailed information around ongoing costs was not available during the interview or in publicly available information. Avoided costs • Information not available through stakeholder interview, however it was stated that Heathrow Airport has not yet experienced flood damage in the eastern catchment therefore avoided costs are unknown (7) .
sections.resourcing: • Heathrow Airport Ltd - Client (7) . • Atkins – Consulting engineers (1) . • Sheet Piling (UK) Ltd - Contractor (1) . • Jacobs – Design (7) . • Morgan Sindall Ltd – Sheet pile retaining walls (1) . • Veolia Water – MBBR principal contractor (3) . • Hanevo – MIECA contractor (3) . • Ecology Habitat Management – Ecological services (7) .
sections.co_benefits: Environment • When water collects in the balancing ponds, pollutants are naturally filtered out during the process of sediment settling at the bottom (4) . • Tilting weirs on the ponds alternate the discharge, allowing for only clean water to be released back into the River Crane (7) . • Heathrow work with the Environment Agency closely to dilute pollutants (including de-icer runoff from the airport) in the river and improve river health downstream (7) . • Since improvement of the water treatment works at the balancing ponds, recurring problems with sewage fungus downstream during the winter period have been prevented (6) . • Relief channels were designed to reduce rates of erosion on the River Crane (7) . • Balancing ponds are an example of a Sustainable Urban Drainage System and consider natural factors in the success of the design (4) . Community • Balancing ponds provide increased protection from flooding to the local roads and communities surrounding Heathrow Airport (4) . Biodiversity • The project earned the Wildlife Trust’s Biodiversity Benchmark Award (7) . • Biodiversity net gain was achieved. Ecology walks were conducted weekly and new species were found during surveys (7) . • Fish rescue area integrated in the relief channel to allow fish to return to the river following storm events (7) . • Installation of hives in the area to increase pollination (7) . • Gabions along the channel provided greater habitat areas for invertebrates (7) . Carbon reduction • The project consciously reduced the amount of concrete used in the construction of relief channels by integrating gabions into the structure (7) .
sections.evaluation: • The pond system has already balanced potential flood events successfully, reducing the impact from heavy rainfall (7) .
sections.challenges: • Maintaining consistent stakeholder engagement proved to be challenging. All parties needed to be involved to ensure success (7) . • The site is allocated as Green Belt and a Site of Importance for Nature Conservation (2) . Therefore, further considerations were required before construction activities. • Construction works on the middle pond were required to minimise silt disturbance to ensure water quality in the lower pond was not reduced (2) . • The materials storage area was raised above the water level, requiring all materials to be transported down an access ramp and loaded onto floating plant (2) . • Maintenance is required on balancing ponds to ensure invasive species such as algae are monitored and removed (4) . • These types of controls were new for airports in the UK and so had to be adapted (7) .
sections.lessons_learned: • A brownfield site guidance document being published in 2026 will inform the wider industry on lessons learned during this project (7) .
sections.innovation_opportunities: • Crane Valley Partnership conducted site tours alongside Heathrow Airport Ltd, inviting stakeholders from other UK airports, as well as utilities companies such as Thames Water (6,7) . Partnerships between these organisations can lead to further innovations in design options. • A new pond is being installed at Heathrow Airport soon with a 100 year lifespan. Updated technical standards will include climate considerations in the design (7) .
