sections.challenge: • An extended period of no rainfall in 2020 combined with a summer heatwave resulted in Heathrow recording a temperature of 37.8°C (1) . • The highest temperature ever recorded at Heathrow Airport is 40.2°C in July 2022 (4) . • Much of the grass in the green zones at Heathrow Airport died during the heatwave of 2020 (6) . • Potential hazards from this included the creation of dust, dry grass and increased bird numbers occupying the area of ground previously taken up by longer grass (6) . • Dust creation can occur because of vegetation dieback and winds eroding dry exposed soils (6) . • Dust conditions near or on the runways affect aircraft take-off and landing by impacting brakes, engines and visibility (6) . • Dry grass can become a potential fire hazard to aircraft, machinery and vehicles on the runways. • Increased bird numbers on the airport increases the risk of bird strike to aircraft taking off and landing (6) . • Heatwaves and drought events are projected to become more frequent and intense in Southeast England because of climate change (2) .
sections.adaptation_measures: Measure implemented • Planting more climate-resilient grass species was chosen as the adaptation measure (6) . • Most grasses in the UK have short root bases (6) . • Heathrow maintains the grass at 150mm – 200mm (6-8 inches) to deter most birds (3) . • Although a certain grass height needs to be maintained, longer root bases are an option to improve resilience to drought conditions (6) . This is because they enable the grasses to draw water from deeper below the surface (6) . • Researchers over the past decade have selectively bred more climate-resilient crops with longer root bases (6) . Organisation Heathrow Airport Ltd Transport subsector Aviation First order climate hazards (cause) Drought; High temperatures Second order climate hazards (effect) Air pollution (dust); Vegetation dieback Transport asset impacted Airport services Adaptation measure Climate-resilient grass Location  London, United Kingdom Year of delivery 2024 - ongoing • External researchers led the innovation, with Heathrow Airport adapting the results for use on- site in the green zones (6). • The chosen mixture was a combination of rhizomatous tall fescue and tetraploid perennial ryegrass which was designed to be drought, cold, heat, disease and salt tolerant (5) . Other options considered • Irrigation to prevent further dieback of grasses was evaluated and concluded to be too expensive and difficult to implement during a drought year (6) .
sections.applicability: • Has potential for applicability in other transport sectors where grassland dieback is an issue, for example railway and highway earth embankments. • Applicable across the UK but may be of most value in south and east England and other regions that are likely to face greater drought and heatwave risks. • Some of the challenges identified would not be an issue in other transport sectors, so a wider range of grass species could be used.
sections.financials: Funding • Funded by Heathrow Airport Ltd (6) . Initial costs • Information not available from stakeholder interview Ongoing costs • Replacing Heathrow’s green zones with the new grass species has been built into ongoing maintenance plans for the next 5-6 years, helping to reduce large upfront costs (6) . Avoided costs • Information not available from stakeholder interview

sections.co_benefits: Environmental • Nature-based Solution utilising specially bred grass species to reduce sensitivity to climate change (6) . • Longer grass roots can aid resilience to surface water flooding that can occur on the airfield, because they: o Help to increase drainage rates by drawing down rainwater directly into the gravels below the soil (6) . o Increase the break-up of soil and prevent it being clay, allowing water to be drawn downwards quicker (6) . Carbon reduction • Carbon sequestration of 8.96 t.CO2eq/Ha (5) . Economic • Reduced air traffic delays due to dust and dry grass on the runway and surround air should be experienced because of planting more climate-resilient species (6) .
sections.evaluation: • Condition surveys periodically conducted to assess the soil quality; results should be observed in 2-3 years (6) . • During the next drought or heatwave, a reduction in air traffic delays in comparison to 2020 would indicate success (6) .
sections.challenges: • The grass chosen must have a thick enough blade to stand vertically. If the blades of grass were too thin, they could fall over and thatch, creating a habitat for small mammals, birds and reptiles. This would then attract larger birds and increase the risk of bird strike for aircraft (6) .
sections.lessons_learned: • Nature-based Solutions take time to become established and display noticeable results (6) . • The benefits of this measure may not be observed for up to five years (6) . • Stakeholder engagement is important to ensure buy-in and educate individuals on the benefits that will be provided in time (6) .
sections.innovation_opportunities: • The research team in Nantes is continuing to develop more climate-resilient species of grass that can be utilised in the future (6) .

hazard_cause: Coastal flooding, Storms and high winds

sections.challenge: • Studies indicate that the historical erosion rate in the Gleason Beach area is approximately one foot per year, a rate that could increase to 1.5 feet (0.5 metres) per year by 2050 and 4.6 feet (1.4 metres) per year by 2100 due to rising sea levels (2) . • The impact of this climate hazard is substantial. Coastal erosion has already led to the collapse of several homes along the cliffs and continues to threaten existing structures. The erosion also poses a significant risk to SR 1, a vital transportation route for residents, tourists, and commerce (6) .
sections.adaptation_measures: Measures implemented The Gleason Beach Realignment Project saw the construction of a 3,700-foot (1,128 metres), two- lane roadway and 900-foot-long (274 metres) bridge span, starting south of the Gleason Beach area (5) . • Relocation of SR 1: A half-mile section of SR 1 was relocated up to 400 feet (122 metres) inland to avoid the eroding cliff. This inland realignment ensures the long-term stability and usability of the road, reducing the need for continuous emergency repairs (1) .  The location was chosen because to move the roadway further inland would potentially require the routing of SR 1 through Carmet, which would have adverse community effects (6) . o The existing highway was repurposed into part of the California Coastal Trail. • Bridge Construction: An 850-foot-long (259 metres) highway bridge over the Scotty Creek floodplain replaced the existing highway culvert, (a structure that channels water past an obstacle or to a subterranean waterway). o Due to the change in elevation from the northern portion of the roadway which is steeper in elevation, and the southern end which consists of a valley and floodplain; this area would need to be filled to provide stability for the roadway if a bridge were not proposed. The bridge span avoids impacts to wetlands, archaeology, and Scotty Creek and maintains access for the Gleason-Mann-Ballard ranch property (6) . Organisation California Department of Transportation (Caltrans) Transport subsector Highways First order climate hazards (cause) Coastal flooding; Storms and high winds Second order climate hazards (effect) Coastal erosion Transport assets impacted Road pavement; Bridges Adaptation measures Relocation of State Road 1 (SR 1); Bridge Construction; Habitat Restoration Location  Sonoma County, California Years of delivery 2021 o The bridge over the floodplain was selected not only for its role in relocating the road but also for its ecological benefits, as unlike the highway culvert, the bridge structure allows the natural flow of the creek to be restored, supporting the migration of endangered fish species like salmon and steelhead (1,5) . The bridge would minimise permanent effects to the coastal terrace prairie, thereby reducing adverse effects to this type of habitat along the floodplain. Other options assessed • One option assessed to reduce the risk of coastal erosion to the SR 1 was coastal armouring. Coastal armouring, like seawalls, would have protected the road in the short term but would have accelerated erosion and disrupted local ecosystems (5) . For example, attempts to stabilise the cliff with piles failed due to the strong erosive forces experienced in the area (5) . • Inland realignment was chosen instead of coastal armouring as it offers a more environmentally friendly and permanent way to protect infrastructure from the advancing shoreline.
sections.applicability: • The UK faces its own coastal erosion challenges, particularly along its low-lying coastline, including areas in East Anglia and parts of Scotland. Much like the Gleason Beach area, these regions are vulnerable to rising sea levels and coastal erosion, both of which threaten key infrastructure, including roads, railways, and housing. • The concept of repurposing vulnerable infrastructure for recreational and environmental benefits—such as the transformation of the relocated SR 1 into part of the California Coastal Trail—could also inspire similar initiatives in the UK. Overview of the Gleason Beach SR 1 Relocation Scheme showing the site pre-adaptation with the proposed works superimposed on top.
sections.financials: Funding • The project was part of the 2015 Federal Transportation Improvement Program (FTIP) . Subsequently, the 2016 State Highway Operation and Protection Program (SHOPP) funded this project in the 2017/18 Fiscal Year (1) . Initial costs • The initial cost was US $60.5 million (£45.38 million), which included US $22 million (£16.5 million) in federal funds (1) . Ongoing costs (2) The estimated ongoing costs total approximately US $10 million (£7.5 million): • US $3.2 million (£2.4 million) to purchase and clean up vacant parcels west of current Highway • US $1 million (£0.75 million) for future purchase of properties from willing sellers • US $150,000 (£112,500) to develop the California Coastal Trail west of the new Highway 1 and convert a section of the existing Highway 1 into beach access parking • US $1 million (£0.75 million) for the future installation and maintenance of a pedestrian bridge over Scotty Creek • US $4.65  million (£3.49 million) to purchase land westward of new Highway 1, restore Scotty Creek, and fund future property management Avoided costs • Information not publicly available. Economic impacts (1) • Traffic disruption and road closures caused by coastal erosion and flooding were a major economic concern. The realignment of SR 1 is expected to reduce or eliminate these disruptions, thus avoiding the associated costs of traffic delays, business disruptions, and tourism loss.
sections.resourcing: (5) • The project involved a range of stakeholders, including state and federal agencies, environmental organisations and local governments. • Caltrans was the lead agency for the project, overseeing planning, design, and implementation. • Agencies such as the California Coastal Commission and the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service were involved to ensure the project adhered to environmental regulations and supported habitat restoration goals. • Sonoma County officials and local community members were engaged throughout the process to ensure that the project met the needs of residents and businesses while balancing transportation and recreational objectives. • The Federal Highway Administration (FHWA) provided support and funding for the project. They collaborated with Caltrans on project oversight and compliance with federal transportation standards.
sections.co_benefits: (5) • Enhanced public access including parking, vehicle turnarounds and access to the beach, boardwalk and stairs through a new access point on the relocated highway. • Repurposing a section of the original highway into the California Coastal Trail. • The project included nature-based solutions by removing the culvert that had previously obstructed fish migration and restoring the natural hydrological flow of Scotty Creek. This was a fundamental component of the project, improving water quality, restoring habitat, and enhancing biodiversity in the area.
sections.evaluation: (1) • By relocating the road inland, the new infrastructure is no longer subject to the immediate risks of coastal erosion, potentially significantly extending its lifespan. This represents a major improvement in the performance and reliability of SR 1 as a critical transport route. • The inland alignment and new bridge minimise the need for costly and disruptive emergency repairs that were common in the past. Since 2004, Caltrans had performed several emergency repairs, such as retaining walls, pavement reconstruction and even minor realignment. These efforts were temporary measures to keep the highway open until this new alignment was in place. This improves overall performance by ensuring that the road remains operational with lower long-term maintenance costs.
sections.challenges: • Sensitive Ecosystems: The project involved working in environmentally sensitive areas, including the Scotty Creek floodplain and coastal ecosystems. Ensuring minimal disruption to wildlife, particularly endangered fish species like Salmon and Steelhead, was a significant challenge. To prevent the inadvertent entrapment of wildlife, including special-status species, during construction, all excavated, steep-walled holes or trenches more than 1 foot deep were covered at the close of each working day using plywood. If it was not feasible to cover an excavation, one or more escape ramps constructed of earthfill or wooden planks were installed (3) . • Unpredictability of Erosion: Coastal erosion, particularly in an area as dynamic as Gleason Beach, is difficult to predict with precision. This made the process of choosing a stable inland location for the road realignment challenging. Engineers had to factor in both immediate erosion risks and longer-term projections for sea-level rise, leading to uncertainty in the design process (3) . • Concerns about Land Use and Public Access: Some local stakeholders were concerned about how the realignment might impact public access to beaches or the natural aesthetic of the area. Ensuring that the relocation preserved or even enhanced public access to the coastline and beaches was essential for community buy-in (4) .
sections.lessons_learned: (2) • Successful adaptation projects require the collaboration of multiple stakeholders, including government agencies, local communities, environmental organisations, and contractors. In this project, coordination between state and federal agencies, along with public engagement, ensured the project met multiple objectives (e.g. infrastructure safety, environmental restoration and public access). • The integration of nature-based solutions, such as the restoration of the Scotty Creek floodplain and the removal of the culvert, proved to be highly effective in not only addressing climate risks but also improving local biodiversity.
sections.innovation_opportunities: There are opportunities to use alternatives to steel reinforcement in concrete structures, like the Scotty Creek highway bridge, to reduce the overall carbon footprint of construction.

organisation: The Panama Canal Authority Adapts Strategic Measures for Water Savings
measures: Restrictions on draft and daily transits
Suspending special lockages and hydraulic assistance

sections.challenge: The Panama Canal Authority (ACP) experiences severe drought conditions that have reduced water levels in Gatun and Alhajuela Lakes, vital for canal operations and water supply to 2.5 million people (2) . In 2023, rainfall dropped by 30%, leaving only 50% of the water needed for the 2024 dry season (2) . With each canal transit consuming significant freshwater, efficient water management has become essential to sustain operations and support local communities. The longer term solution is to build a reservoir along the Indio River to increase the availability of fresh water however, this will flood some communities such as Limon, displacing local communities (5) .
sections.adaptation_measures: Measures implemented Key actions include modifying the variable Fresh Water Surcharge (CAD) to stabilise Gatun and Alhajuela Lakes, cross-filling in Panamax Locks to reuse water between chambers and using water-saving basins in Neopanamax Locks. oFresh Water Surcharge - A variable component of the fee to transit the canal which is set at a level designed to preserve water levels in the lakes (2) . oCross Filling of Panamax Locks - This involves reusing water between the chambers of the Panamax Locks, which are one of the two sets of locks in the Panama Canal. By transferring water from one chamber to another, the ACP can reduce the amount of freshwater needed for each transit, thereby conserving water resources (6) . OrganisationPanama Canal Authority (ACP) Transport subsectorMaritime First order climate hazard (cause)Drought Second order climate hazard (effect)N/A Transport asset impactedPort services Adaptation measuresWater optimisation measures; Fresh Water Surcharge (CAD); Water-saving basins (WSBs) in Neopanamax Locks; Cross-filling in Panamax Locks; Restrictions on draft and the maximum number of daily transits; Minimising direction changes at Gatun Locks; Controlling and eliminating water leaks; Suspending special lockages and hydraulic assistance; Minimising electricity generation Location Panama City, Panama Years of delivery2023 - ongoing oWater Saving Basins (WSBs) - Basins designed to recycle water during the lockage process, which significantly reduces the amount of freshwater needed for each transit. Specifically, the WSBs in the Neopanamax Locks can recycle up to 60% of the water used by preventing the water being discharged into the sea (7) . Additional measures include restricting vessel drafts and daily transits, tandem lockages (transiting two vessels simultaneously) and operational changes that save 1.2 million cubic meters of water daily, equivalent to 80% of water consumption in Colón, Panamá and Panamá Oeste provinces (1) . Efforts also include minimising direction changes at Gatun Locks, addressing leaks, suspending special lockages and hydraulic assistance, and reducing electricity generation (2) . oSpecial Lockages – Transiting the canal outside of the normal schedule (2) . These measures are closely tied to the role of Gatun Dam, which forms Gatun Lake—a crucial water reservoir for canal operations. By conserving water in the lake, which supplies the locks and generates hydroelectric power, these steps help optimise water usage (2) .
sections.applicability: Given the unique nature of the Panama Canal, the specific adaptation measures adopted may not translate directly elsewhere. However, the principles behind them, i.e. the importance of proactive water management and the need for robust adaptation measures to ensure the sustainability of water resources, have wide applicability.
sections.financials: Funding The level of funding required for the adaptations is not publicly available but is secured through the ACP's revenue generation from tolls and other maritime services, as well as from the Panamanian Government's contributions and investments (2) . Initial costs The exact costs are not specified, but they are part of the ACP's capital investment program for fiscal year 2023, which consisted of 150 projects with a total expense of $565 million (£452 million) (2) . Ongoing costs In 2023, the ACP spent over $400 million, (£321m), on maintaining the canal. Specific areas of expenditure are not available (9) . Avoided costs The avoided costs can be estimated based on the water saved through the implementation of these measures, such as the use of water-saving basins (WSBs) and cross-filling in locks. The total volume of water saved between the Panamax and Neopanamax locks was 1,905.8 lock equivalents, (a lock equivalent is 98,420 cubic metres), in 2023 (2) .
sections.resourcing: (8) The ACP collaborates with several organisations to implement water-saving measures and advance hydro-environmental research. Key partners include the Center for Innovation, Research and Hydro-environmental Technology (CITEC), supported by the City of Knowledge Foundation, the National Secretariat of Science, Technology and Innovation (SENACYT), the National Authority for Government Innovation (AIG) and the Technological University of Panama (UTP). These partnerships focus on developing innovative solutions to enhance water management and ensure the canal's sustainability despite climate challenges.
sections.co_benefits: Economic (4) The economic impacts of the adaptation measures are measured and evaluated based on the water saved, the reduction in water-related costs and the continued operation of the Panama Canal. The severe drought affecting the Panama Canal has had significant financial repercussions. In early 2024, the ACP reduced ship crossings by 36%, leading to an estimated revenue decline of $500 million to $700 million, (£390m to £546m), for the year (3) . The canal reported a 9.5% increase in net income for the fiscal year ending September 2024, reaching $3.45 billion, (£2.69 billion). This growth was achieved through operational cost reductions of 5% and strategic measures to mitigate the drought's impact. Community (2) The adaptation measures have enhanced water retention and watershed resilience, ensuring sustainable water resources for the Panama Canal and local communities.
sections.evaluation: (2) The adaptation measures have been evaluated based on the water saved (1,905.8 lock equivalents in 2023), the reduction in water-related costs and the continued operation of the Panama Canal. The success of the adaptation measures is measured by the avoidance of disruption in similar weather conditions and the improved performance of the infrastructure.
sections.challenges: Before 2023, the canal only operated with water saving measures during the dry season. It has now become necessary to operate in this mode, with the restrictions it places on transits, at all times (2) .
sections.lessons_learned: The lessons learned include the importance of proactive water management and the significance of partnerships and local community engagement.
sections.innovation_opportunities: There are opportunities for innovation in water management and adaptation measures, particularly by increasing the volume of water recycling in the lockage process.

sections.challenge: Intense rainfall events were experienced in August 2004, May 2006 and May, June, July 2007 (1) , negatively impacting roads, pavements and nearby buildings in the Royal Borough of Kensington and Chelsea, and London Borough of Hammersmith and Fulham (3) . In June-July 2007 this area of London experienced 150-200% of the average rainfall expected for those months combined, based on 1961-1990 averages (12) . In this area (Counters Creek catchment) there is a combined sewerage system (collection of both ‘foul’ and ‘run-off’ flows) (1) . Combined sewerage systems are at higher risk of surcharge and flooding during and after heavy rainfall (2) due to the high volume of rainwater exceeding drainage capacity. Without efficient and suitably sized drainage, this surface water runoff floods the roads, pavements and nearby buildings (1) . During heavy rainfall events, the system is overwhelmed, and surface water accumulates in the area (2) . There is increased risk of flooding in the future due to population growth, urban creep and climate change, both due to increasing volumes and rainfall intensity (2) . The Counters Creek area lost almost 20% of its permeable green space between 1971 and 2007 due to urbanisation (4) . Greater protection from flooding would benefit an area containing over 1,000 properties in the catchment (1) .
sections.adaptation_measures: Measures implemented Installation of SuDS on the roadside to reduce surface water run-off (1) . The SuDS are expected to help mitigate urban creep in the catchment and decrease flood risk by mimicking natural drainage processes (2) . The SuDS design included use of Polypipe’s Permavoid geocellular system underneath permeable paving and in the construction of four storage tanks located underneath rain gardens (4) . The system is a sub-base replacement system which has been specifically designed and tested to provide stormwater treatment, retention, attenuation or infiltration at shallower depths ( 13) . OrganisationThames Water Utilities Ltd Transport sub-sectorRoads First order climate hazards (cause)Heavy rainfall; Flooding – surface water Second order climate hazard (effect)N/A Transport assets impactedRoad pavement; Structures (bridges, culverts) Adaptation measureSustainable Drainage System (SuDS) Location London, UK Years of delivery2007-2020 Rain gardens and tree pits were installed and formed part of the hydraulic system of the permeable paving (6) . Other options assessed Extensive catchment modelling of the sewerage system was undertaken (1) . Three Sustainable Drainage System (SuDS) pilots were conducted between 2014-2015 to understand the potential benefits (1) . Three different SuDS designs were installed and monitored: permeable block paving, permeable asphalt and planted gardens (1) . The information monitored over a two-year period included: performance, feasibility and maintenance requirements (1) . SuDS designs were evaluated based on engineering and local constraints of the street and potential storage capacity. The quantity of water entering the SuDS was a key factor considered (4) . Well-designed SuDS should wherever possible incorporate the four elements of water quantity, water quality, amenity and biodiversity (5) . Completed rain garden in a permeable parking bay ‘Polypipe Perm avoid system’ permeable parking bay during installation 80mm permeable block pavers 50mm thick laying course Filter geotextile 50mm thick bedding 200mm Type 1 Sub-base Impermeable geo-membrane with protection fleece 300mm thick heavy duty geocellular units 200mm thick proprietary pervious concreate (minimum) Warning grid Cross section of permeable parking bay
sections.applicability: This case study is of primary relevance to the Roads transport subsector, particularly in relation to local urban settings to support mitigation of heavy rainfall and surface water flooding. The case study is also of relevance to suburban and rural settings as approaches for local stakeholder engagement, the challenges experienced and lessons learnt would be equally applicable in those settings. There are lessons from the case study that would also be applicable to other transport subsectors where their assets include large, paved areas, for example: oRailways: Implementing SuDS along railway lines can help manage stormwater, prevent track flooding, and reduce erosion (5) . oAirports: SuDS can be used in airport design to manage large volumes of runoff from runways and taxiways, reducing flood risk and improving water quality (5) . oPorts and Harbors: SuDS can help manage runoff from paved surfaces, reducing pollution and improving water quality in adjacent water bodies (5) .
sections.financials: Funding Thames Water Utilities Ltd was the sponsor/funder (6) . Initial costs £550,000 for the ground investigation (8) . Implementation of five SuDS schemes cost approximately £5 million in total (14) . This implementation followed the three SuDS pilots mentioned in the “Adaptation measures” section. Ongoing costs Maintenance activities by the local authority, but cost information not publicly available. Avoided costs Information not publicly available. Sustainable drainage system design, including permeable parking bay and rain garden Arrows indicate direction of water flow – both horizontally underneath the parking bays and upwards to irrigate the rain gardens
sections.resourcing: Installation of SuDS (6) . oF. M. Conway Ltd – Design (Highways) oCappagh Contractors – Design (Manholes etc.) oMott MacDonald – Design oThames Water Utilities Ltd – Sponsor oLondon Borough of Hammersmith and Fulham – Partner oSEL Environmental Ltd – Polypipe installation oHammersmith Community Gardens Association – Rain garden installation
sections.co_benefits: Community Reduced impact from urban heat island effect, increased green infrastructure on public highways, increased capacity of the wider sewage network, traffic calming, footway renewal (6) . Permeable block paving parking bays provided a drainage solution without reducing the available parking on the affected streets (6) . Kerb openings to the sewer and geotextile filters installed in the permeable paving provided pollution control and reduced highways run-off (6) . Geotextile filters are a synthetic, permeable fabric that allow water to pass through but prevent pollutants from entering the sewer. Environmental Polypipe Permavoid cells upwardly irrigate planters and rain gardens located above, sustaining the plant growth, reducing the need for irrigation, as well as reducing flood risk (4) . Biodiversity Rain gardens and tree pits were installed in non-parking zones to increase green space and biodiversity without reducing parking availability further (6) .
sections.evaluation: Following the pilot, Thames Water conducted a survey of 1,000 properties previously at-risk and without protection measures installed, finding that just 200-400 were still potentially at risk of flooding. Thames Water therefore concluded that the scheme had been successful in providing protection and reducing the hydraulic sewer flood risk in the catchment area by over 50% (1) . Reducing this number will include further installation of localised devices in basements outside of the SuDS project, such as Flooding local improvement process (FLIP). FLIP is a pump and one-way valve that actively pushes wastewater from a property into the sewer network when the sewer reaches capacity. Depth monitors were installed in twelve sewer locations to assess and track long term performance of the network (1) . Depth monitors track the depth of sewage flow, triggering an alarm if the level rises above a determined threshold. The SuDS are still being monitored to evaluate flow rate and volume but as of mid-2020 the scheme had worked in reducing flood damage (6) . University College London managed the monitoring data following a handover of the project from Mott MacDonald to the local authority, with no further monitoring from Mott MacDonald (14) . Hydraulic performance was planned to be monitored by the local authority following the handover but has not yet been completed (13) .
sections.challenges: Surrounding ground conditions and proximity to basements reduced how much run-off could be addressed through infiltration into the SuDS (6) . Limited space in the on-street parking bays was a challenge in introducing SuDS (6) . Developing ways of working between Thames Water and local authorities as a partnership, to ensure that the needs of all parties involved were understood and addressed (13) . Stakeholder management with other utilities companies. The key requirements of other companies had to be considered (14) . For example, permeable paving had been constructed over other utilities, creating potential conflict with utility providers in the area that experienced reduced access for maintenance as a result (14) . Some members of the local community who hadn’t been flooded were being affected by construction (14) . The need to emphasise that the project is the construction of a new highway and not only a horticultural project to get community buy-in required additional time and resources (14) . Designs changed – permeable paving was the key intervention but other interventions were also tested during the proof of concept. As a result of community feedback, not all interventions were installed in all areas, resulting in differences between areas (14) .
sections.lessons_learned: Community engagement to create a sense of ownership and ensure local resident involvement in the schemes was important for success . Early engagement with residents and the council ensured that local concerns were addressed and ideas incorporated to understand the benefit that SuDS could bring (6) . Community engagement was led by Thames Water and although the plan evolved throughout, it didn’t drastically change as a result of speaking to local people (13) . On-site weekly meetings with the design team, contractors and council allowed for prompt design changes (6) . Regular independent health and safety inspections were undertaken, ensuring standards were maintained and interventions taken if required (6) . Design and delivery of the SuDS was done in 12 months, if more time had been available, additional ground investigations could have been conducted, potentially reducing the number of changes made on-site (6) . The project proved that these systems are flexible and can be installed almost anywhere (14) . If the project requires large diversions, it might not be worthwhile to implement this type of scheme (14) . Standardisation of the system is key to increase implementation rates as well as to reduce costs (14) . Cities such as Philadelphia and New York have done more work in this area and are good examples to follow (14) .
sections.innovation_opportunities: Developing a market-based approach to scaling up this type of adaptation measure could be beneficial to cities – The ‘buyers’ would be utilities companies in areas where SuDS need to be constructed, and the ‘sellers’ are Thames Water building climate resilience (14) . oUse credits to monetise this process in a closed market approach (14) . oThe United States is more advanced in this space as there are fewer challenges with municipal utilities and so provides examples of best practice (14) . Greater London Authority is partnering with Thames Water in developing a market-based approach to deliver similar projects, incentivising utility companies to refill holes with SuDS (11) . The use of real-time controls in SuDS, if implemented at scale, can improve control of the entire sewer network (14) . Improvements in the efficiency of monitoring methods from all implementing teams will aid in identifying opportunities for improvement in the SuDS system (14) .

sections.challenge: • Climate change and urbanisation are increasing surface water runoff in Sheffield (1) . For example, river catchments have been altered as the city has grown, overwhelming sewage treatment works during heavy rainfall (4) . • Rainfall in June 2007 was the highest experienced by Sheffield since 1882 (2) . • 3 days of rain resulted in the banks of the River Don rising between 1.5 and 1.8 metres, bursting and flooding low lying areas of the city centre (3,6) . • Floodwater and debris resulted in cancellation of the tram service, damage to 16 traffic signal controllers, and closure of 28 roads and the Sheffield railway station (2) . • Flood water affected 1,275 domestic properties and around 1,000 business buildings (2) . • 2 people died and over 1,000 were evacuated or unable to go back to their homes (3) . • Total cost of the damage was around £30 million (3) .
sections.adaptation_measures: Measure implemented • The Grey to Green project was designed to reduce the impact of future heavy rainfall events (3) and is being delivered in three phases. The project is currently in Phase 3. o Phase 1 “green street” project (2016) focused on 1.5km of continuous green infrastructure built along highways or former highways (7) in Sheffield city centre. The project runs along part of what was the former inner city ring road, which was a dual carriageway running around the city centre (5) . o Phase 2 (2020) extended the green corridor further through the city, incorporating additional SuDS, planting beds, and public art installations (7) . o Phase 3 (Ongoing) continues to expand the green infrastructure with further installation of rain gardens throughout other areas of the city centre, as part of further urban regeneration and urban realm improvements (7) . Organisation Sheffield City Council Transport subsectors Rail; Roads First order climate hazards (cause) Flooding - fluvial; Flooding - surface water; Heavy rainfall Second order climate hazard (effect) Water damage Transport assets impacted Bridges; Buildings and stations; Foot or cycle paths; Road pavement; Signs and signals; Signalling and telecommunications; Rail track; Trains; Tramways Adaptation measure Sustainable Drainage System (SuDS) Location  Sheffield, United Kingdom Years of delivery 2014 - ongoing • 60% of the former grey surfacing in the project area has now been converted to green spaces (7) . • Simon Ogden, the former Head of City Regeneration at Sheffield City Council, played a pivotal role in the Grey to Green project. He was inspired by the work of Nigel Dunnett, who had worked on the ‘Super bloom landscape’ (Tower of London) and meadow-style planting (Olympic Park) projects in London (3) . • Grey to Green contains a series of rain gardens along a mile-long ‘green street’ that function as Sustainable Drainage Systems (SuDS) (1) . These areas act to slow down the rates of flow by capturing the water and allowing it to percolate slowly into the soil beneath or evaporate into the atmosphere (3) . • The project site slopes steeply in places, so the scheme is split into 25 ‘cells’ separated by small dams. Each cell fills up before overflowing to the next during periods of heavier rainfall (3) . • A draw down system consisting of perforated collection pipes, control chambers and perforated distributor pipes moves water through the system (1) . • Mulch in shallow vegetated channels (called ‘swales’) absorbs the energy from surface water runoff and reduces local accumulation of sediments (1) . This reduces the flow rate and runoff more efficiently than traditional flood defences (3) . • “24,000 bathtubs” of water are stopped from entering the city sewage treatment works each year as a result of the SuDS (4) . • In spring 2020, 75,000 plants and 56 large trees were planted (3) . • The scheme can be viewed as low maintenance due to: o Dense swards of flowers allowing little chance for weeds to gain a foothold o Prairie plants being carefully selected to thrive in a scheme-specific engineered soil (3) • The scheme requires one major intervention per year, when the plants are cut back around the time of the first frost event (3) . • Grey to Green complements the raised flood embankments that form the riverside Lower Don Flood Alleviation Scheme. Other options considered • Rather than building traditional ‘grey’ flood infrastructure across the city centre, Sheffield City Council looked to implement a Nature-Based Solutions scheme (7) . • This is because the main reason for the 2007 flooding was determined to be the amount of hard built surfaces in the catchment on the flood plains (7) as well as urban runoff running quickly into the river valleys from hard surfaced areas across most of Sheffield, including the city centre (7) . • The planning and landscape architecture departments of Sheffield City Council did not have the capacity to undertake a green initiative so a partnership with the University of Sheffield was formed (5) . • Student design projects were developed to support feasibility studies, producing options and visuals of what a green street might look like. The resulting concepts were used for consultation with residents and businesses, as well as to support funding applications. • Techniques and solutions for cost effective, but also highly diverse and functional greening of urban infrastructure, are continuously developed and assessed in partnership with the City Council and the University of Sheffield (7) .
sections.applicability: • This is applicable to all transport sectors that lie within or downstream of urban areas. It’s particularly applicable to infrastructure that follows river valleys, as the railways in Sheffield do. • Given the unique challenges posed by urban runoff and flooding, planning and development of such infrastructure may require collaboration with local authorities and other landowners. This partnership can help ensure that measures are in place to mitigate flood risks and manage runoff effectively, thereby protecting the transport infrastructure and enhancing its resilience.
sections.financials: Funding • Funding has been provided by South Yorkshire Mayoral Combined Authority, the European Regional Development Fund and Sheffield City Council (1) . Although, EU funding was for economic development and not climate adaptation (7) . • Small financial contribution from the Canal and Rivers Trust for Phase 1 (1) . Grey to Green Phase 2 project site: before and after on River Sheaf Walk. Completed Grey to Green project site in Castlegate area of Sheffield. • Small financial contribution from Yorkshire Water for Phase 2 (1) . Initial costs • Phase 1 cost roughly £3.6 million (1) . • Phase 2 cost roughly £6.3 million (1) . • Phase 3 currently ongoing – final costs not yet known. Ongoing costs • Low maintenance inputs required (1) . • Costs saved from maintaining grey infrastructure have been ringfenced for the future of Grey to Green maintenance requirements (7) . • Further details were not available from publicly available information or from interviews. Avoided costs • Reduction in maintenance costs from the replacement of bituminous surfacing, gulleys and traffic management equipment (1) . • Further details were not available from publicly available information or from interviews.
sections.resourcing: • South Yorkshire Mayoral Combined Authority (Sheffield City Region Investment Fund previously) - Funder (1) . • European Union Regional Development Fund (ERDF) programme - Funder (1) . • Sheffield City Council – Funder, client, lead designer (1) . • AMEY – Highway designer (1) . • Robert Bray Associates – SuDS consultation and flow modelling (1) . • Nigel Dunnett, University of Sheffield – Planting consultation (1) . • North Midland Construction – Primary contractor (1) . • Ashlea - Softworks contractor (1) . • Green Estates - Softworks maintenance (1) . • Turner and Townsend - Project and cost management (1) .
sections.co_benefits: Community • Improved appearance and appeal of the city centre (3) . Surveys with Sheffield residents have found that people are actively choosing to go to this area (7) and 16% of users surveyed have diverted their daily journey to include the space (7) . • ‘Immersive horticultural experience’ for the local community (3) . • Increased gathering spaces, footpaths and sculpture areas (1) . • Trips to the scheme are now included in local school curriculums (7) . • 98% of the green street users surveyed wanted to see more green spaces in Sheffield (7) . Environmental • The project was entirely focussed on the use of nature-based solutions, primarily rainwater capture (1) . • Reduced urban heat island effect (1) . Through shading and evaporative cooling, green spaces have been observed to reduce temperatures from 55°C to 20°C (7) . • Filtering of rainwater pollutants, resulting in improved water quality and river health downstream (1) . • Specially designed flowerbeds collect pollutants from the road such as oil, heavy metals and microplastics and prevent them from entering the River Don or sewage treatment works (4) . Biodiversity • Increased insects and pollinators in the area following completion of the scheme (3) . • Natural source of winter feed for the local bird population (3) . • A BREEAM study undertaken by ecology consultancy ECUS concluded that biodiversity increased by 561% in the area after Phase 2 of the project (1) . Carbon reduction • Reduced traffic in the area due to the wider road remodelling including rain gardens, reducing lane width, and construction of a new outer ring road for the city of Sheffield (1) . • Promoted active travel through the introduction of foot and cycle paths (1) . Studies have concluded that up to 20,000 pedestrian users per day use the Grey to Green streets (7) . • Reports suggest a CO reduction of 230 tonnes from sequestration by trees and plant life (6) . Economic • Opened up the Castlegate area of Sheffield for increased investment (1)(3) . • £1 million of investment has been brought into the area (7) . • At least 540 jobs have been created from the overall project as a result of businesses relocated to previously vacant office buildings, warehouses and Castle House, a listed building in the area (6) .
sections.evaluation: • The rain gardens’ effect on drainage was modelled and it showed that river discharge from a 1 hour, 1-in-30-year rainfall event was reduced from 47.3 litres/sec to 9 litres/sec (3) . • For a 60-minute-long,1-in-100-year event, the discharge was modelled to reduce from 69.6 litres/sec to 9.2 litres/sec (3) . • Even after adding in an extra 30 percent allowing for the effect of climate change, the discharge was still estimated to be just 12.1 litres/sec for a 1-in-100-year event. (3) . • This project has been so successful that it is now embedded as the default approach in the city. A similar system to improve the main shopping area of Fargate is currently being installed, as well as an urban park on the site of Sheffield Castle (7) and Pounds Park, a recently opened urban park as part of the Heart of the City regeneration project (7) . • The scheme has been so successful that it has inspired an £80 million SuDS project in Mansfield by Severn Trent Water (3) . • The scheme has attracted international visits from town planners including The Netherlands, Sweden, South Korea, Japan and the USA (3) .
sections.challenges: • Only half of the highway was re-profiled due to service depth difficulties (1) . • Specialist horticultural skills are required to maintain the specific plant species and SuDS chosen for this type of project, creating a potential barrier to wider adoption of this measure elsewhere. Local authorities are often concerned about the required knowledge and personnel required to maintain the scheme long-term (7) . • Initially local businesses were concerned that the area would appear like a “wild landscape”. Additional reassurance was required to achieve buy-in (7) . • Biodiversity net gain requirements can be a challenge for a scheme when deciding on which plants will be used. For instance, the native flora of the British countryside was not always the best choice for use in a city context with synthetically made soil substrates (7) . Specialists were required to assess potential plant species and ensure the most appropriate were chosen. • Salt runoff from de-icing activities in the city is an issue for plant health in the scheme (7) . Monitoring of plant and soil health is required to ensure this does not become detrimental to the project success.
sections.lessons_learned: • Multiple smaller green spaces around an urban area can have a similar impact to one large green space in mitigating the effects of flooding and urban heat island effect (7) . • Utilising students and researchers from academia can give schemes greater credibility (7) . • Locally based research and demonstrations of the proposed schemes were important for community and business buy-in (7) . • The project has been successful due to the focus on the quality of design, plants chosen and maintenance activities required for the whole system. Other schemes have failed due to a focus on the engineering works and not the plants being used or maintained, resulting in plants dying after a few years and capital being wasted (7) .
sections.innovation_opportunities: • In a similar project in Hull, warnings are given prior to storm events to allow stormwater planters to be drained before refilling during heavy rainfall. Automated systems in planters could connect to weather data to automatically empty storage tanks without human operation 6) . • Sheffield Grey to Green is the longest urban green corridor and the largest retrofit grey to green project in the UK, providing examples of best practice to further projects around the country (7) .

organisation: Santa Barbara County Montecito's Randall Road Debris Basin

sections.challenge: • The San Ysidro Creek in Montecito, California, has experienced destructive debris flows following extreme rainfall events, with five notable events recorded since 1825 (1) . • Debris flows are geological phenomena in which water-laden masses of soil and fragmented rock flow down mountain sides, funnel into stream channels, damage and entrain objects in their paths, and form thick, muddy deposits on valley floors (9) . • In January 2018, devastating debris flows in Montecito, caused by heavy rains post-wildfire conditions, resulted in 23 fatalities, destroyed roughly 470 structures (houses and outbuildings) and inundated Highway 101 with mud (4) .
sections.adaptation_measures: Measures implemented • The Randall Road Debris Basin was constructed uphill of the previously affected area, to capture debris during heavy rainfall events, preventing it from flowing downstream into populated areas (4) . • The basin is an artificially landscaped depression in the land that spans approximately six acres with the capacity to retain debris and water generated during intense storms (4) . • This measure was selected due to its proven effectiveness in similar contexts and its ability to provide immediate protection (4) . • The project was initiated as a direct response to the 2018 disaster and the increasing frequency and severity of climate-related events in the region (4) . • The basin reduces sedimentation in downstream waterways and features habitat restoration through the replanting of native species (7) . • Hourly rainfall intensities between 5 and 15 mm/h are sufficient to trigger debris flows at many of the sites, and observed debris-flow volumes range from a few hundred up to almost one million cubic meters (11) . • The basin is constructed with a spillway, a structure used to control the release of water downstream. The spillway ensures that water does not damage other parts of the structure not designed to convey water (12) . Organisation Santa Barbara County Transport subsector Roads First order climate hazards Flooding – fluvial and surface water Second order climate hazards Rockfalls, landslides, avalanches; Wildfire Transport assets impacted Roads; Buildings Adaptation measure Debris basin Location  California, USA Years of delivery 2018 - 2022 Other options considered • Several measures to mitigate the risk of debris flows have been implemented along the San Ysidro creek. • In 2019, two metal debris nets were installed in the canyon higher up the San Ysidro Creek (2) . The nets have been vetted as a proven mitigation system and part of an overall system to stop momentum and volume of potential debris flows, and the damage of large debris impact. They are a measure that form part of the preparedness and risk management for catastrophic debris flow within Californian canyons (10) . • In 2023, plans were approved to modify a smaller and pre-existing debris basin, approximately 3 miles north of the Randall Road site, to improve flood control functionality (3) .
sections.applicability: • Debris basins are an effective adaptation for (a) locations subject to intense rainfall triggering debris flows and (b) where sufficient land is available to construct the required features. • Within the UK, this measure would be applicable to landscapes where high energy upland rivers have the history and ability to dislodge and carry either heavy boulders and other large debris downstream, causing damage to transport infrastructure, or catchments where significant quantities of smaller debris has the potential to impact transport infrastructure. This could include locations where debris flows onto the infrastructure itself, for example where rivers burst their banks onto roads and railways, or in situations where debris blocks culverts and the backing up of flood flows causes flooding of transport infrastructure. • Given the topography of the UK, debris basins are most likely to be relevant in upland areas of Scotland and Wales as well as upland hills in Northern Ireland and England. These areas are also most likely to see a larger increase in projected winter rainfall across the UK. • The Randall Road Debris Basin was in part constructed in response to wildfire followed by heavy rainfall, the combination of which will have led to less stabilisation and less slowing of rainfall over land due to vegetation loss in the fire. This debris basin measure may be applicable to upland areas of the UK that may become more prone to wildfires due to climate change, for example upland moorland and heaths.
sections.financials: Funding • The US $18 million (£14.21 million) project was funded with a US $13 million (£10.26 million) grant from the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) and US $5 million (£3.95 million) from Santa Barbara County (4) . Initial costs • The initial US $18 million (£14.21 million) budget included US $13 million (£10.26 million) allocated for the purchase of six properties on Randall Road and one across San Ysidro Creek on East Valley Road and a US $5.2 million (£4.1 million) contract for the excavation work (4) . • In 2020, the County eventually concluded the purchase of five of the Randall Road properties, plus 1760 East Valley Road, for US $8.3 million (£6.55 million) (4) . • The County also paid US $400,000 (£315,640) in title and escrow costs (4) . Ongoing costs • Maintenance and monitoring costs are expected, but not publicly detailed. • The Randall Road Basin stands as the largest and most costly debris basin project currently underway in Montecito and Carpinteria (4) . • However, it is not the only one. The County is actively pursuing an additional US $9 million (£7.1 million) in federal and state funding to upgrade the spillways at the existing San Ysidro debris basin and those at Cold Springs and Romero (4) . Avoided costs • By preventing downstream damage and fatalities, the basin significantly reduces potential recovery costs. The 2018 Montecito debris flow caused over US $204 million (£161 million) in property damage, destroying 160 buildings and damaging 462 others (5) . • The mudslides also cut off the main route of transportation for commuters, tourists and commercial traffic. Many businesses closed or reduced their operating hours during this period. It is estimated that the closure of US-101 cost local workers an upper bound of US $25-30 million (£20-23 million) in lost wages (5) .
sections.resourcing: • The basin required land acquisitions from local property owners and consultation with engineering and environmental firms for design and construction (4) . • Engagement with state and federal agencies was critical in securing funding and regulatory approvals (4) .
sections.co_benefits: Environmental • The basin reduces sedimentation in downstream waterways and features habitat restoration through the replanting of native species (7) . Community • Enhances public safety by mitigating debris flow risks (4) . Economic • Protects property and reduces economic disruption from potential debris flows (4) .
sections.evaluation: • There have been no major storm events to test the effectiveness of the debris basin since completion in October 2022.
sections.challenges: Obtaining the land for the debris basin was the key challenge. Community resistance from one property owner delayed the full implementation of the scheme and the matter was ultimately resolved through a legal action by the County enforcing the purchase of the final plot of land (8) .
sections.lessons_learned: • Early community engagement is critical to address concerns and build support (4) . • Partnerships between local, state and federal agencies facilitate funding and technical expertise (4) . • Incorporating environmental considerations can enhance co-benefits and streamline regulatory approval (4) .
sections.innovation_opportunities: • Future projects could incorporate advanced monitoring systems and data analytics to predict and respond to debris flow risks more effectively, including through public and emergency services alert systems. • Nature-based solutions, such as reforestation in upstream areas, could complement infrastructure-based measures to provide holistic watershed management.

sections.challenge: The Dawlish railway connects communities, businesses and visitors in 50 towns and cities in the South West with the rest of the UK (1) . The railway frequently experiences flooding due to powerful sea waves, leading to service disruptions and costly repairs (2) . In February 2014, very strong winds combined with high seas severely damaged the railway. The storm breached the sea wall, washing away 80 metres of track and station platforms at Dawlish railway station (3) . This resulted in an eight-week closure and £35 million in repair costs (2) with the immediate response including measures such as a temporary sea wall made from shipping containers, rebuilding and fortifying the breach, repairing dozens of sites along a four mile stretch of railway, installing a temporary signalling system, and extensive repairs to Dawlish station (3) . With rising sea levels, the risk of disruption will only increase. Projections indicate that by 2040, extreme weather events could disrupt the line for 40 days annually, increasing to 120 days per year by 2100 (8) .
sections.adaptation_measures: Measure implemented As part of the South West Rail Resilience Programme, a new sea wall was developed to protect the railway between Exeter and Newton Abbot following the 2014 storm (1) . The new wall was split over two phases, with the first running for approximately 400m from Colonnade underpass to Boat Cove (9) . The project’s second phase included 415 metres of new promenade and other accessible public areas between Coastguards and Colonnade breakwaters, which opened to the public on 25 th May 2023 (1) . The new wall added 4.2 metres to the current 3.8 metre promenade, bringing the total height to 8 metres and included a recurve at the top to help deflect waves back to the sea and reduce ‘overtopping’ of water onto the track (1) . It was built using low-carbon concrete and consists of 164 front panels, 203 pre-cast blocks, and 189 recurve units (1 and 5) . This modern engineering solution enhances the railway’s resilience against future flooding events leading to fewer line closures and a quicker recovery after large storm events to enable a normal service to resume (1) . OrganisationNetwork Rail Transport sub-sectorRailways First order climate hazards (cause)Sea level rise; Storms and high winds Second order climate hazard (effect)Flooding Transport asset impactedTrack Adaptation measureNew sea wall Location Dawlish, UK Years of delivery2020 - 2022 To construct the sea wall, an innovative WaveWalker barge was deployed as opposed to a more traditional Jack-up barge. Its design made it adept to working in hostile conditions and it could easily manoeuvre through waves and varying water depths, enabling the work to be completed throughout adverse weather (7) . Other options considered Network Rail worked with leading coastal, marine and railway engineers to investigate the best long-term solutions to improve the resilience of the section of railway, and undertook a full option-selection process considering a range of potential solutions including combinations of the following measures (1) : oOffshore breakwaters oRevetment oRaised sea wall with high level promenade oRaised sea wall with low level promenade. The different sea defence options were assessed against four criteria (10) : oTo evaluate the overtopping resilience at the railway oTo evaluate the overtopping resilience for passengers at Dawlish station oTo evaluate the overtopping resilience for pedestrians on the promenade oTo evaluate the resilience of the integrity of the structures The final proposed option was a new, taller sea wall with a high-level promenade (1) .
sections.applicability: As a UK case study, other coastal railways and critical transport assets facing similar risks from extreme weather and rising sea levels may benefit from similar resilience measures. Key examples include: South Coast rail links (e.g., Brighton to Portsmouth) exposed to coastal erosion. Scottish coastal routes impacted by storm surges and high winds. Flood-prone transport infrastructure in East Anglia and the Thames Estuary.
sections.financials: Funding The project was Government-funded, ensuring a resilient railway while enhancing public spaces, such as a new fully accessible promenade (1) . Initial costs The Department for Transport committed £80 million for a new sea wall from Coastguards Breakwater to Boat Cove, completed in two phases (4) . Ongoing costs Information not publicly available. Avoided costs Information on avoided costs is not publicly available. However, the new sea wall successfully withstood five-metre waves and 80mph winds during Storm Ciarán in November 2023— conditions identical to the 2014 storm (6) which had cost more than £35 million in repairs (1) . In addition, utilising the ‘WaveWalker’, an innovative eight-legged walking jack-up barge, for the construction of the sea wall saved an estimated £7 million (7) . Economic impacts The project boosted the local economy by £15 million, with Network Rail and BAM Nuttall prioritising local labour, materials, and accommodation (1) .
sections.resourcing: Network Rail collaborated with world-leading coastal, marine, and railway engineers to determine the best long-term resilience strategy (1) . HR Wallingford, a global leader in water-related infrastructure solutions, modelled potential resilience strategies (1) . Network Rail commissioned the engineering company CH2M Hill (now a part of Jacobs) to conduct a study into the future options for the route between Exeter and Newton Abbot (2) .
sections.co_benefits: Carbon reduction The sea wall was built using low-carbon concrete from Hanson UK, which replaces traditional cement with a steel by-product. This reduced carbon emissions by two-thirds, saving over 1,130 tonnes of CO₂ compared to standard concrete (5) . Community The fully accessible promenade is more spacious, safer, and a level surface to improve accessibility for users. It is also higher than the previous promenade, which prevents it from being inundated with beach material, therefore creating a more accessible area. Additional seating has been provided and pigeon roosts removed, leading to a cleaner promenade and improved water quality (1) .
sections.evaluation: The new sea wall successfully withstood five-metre waves and 80mph winds during Storm Ciarán in November 2023—conditions identical to the 2014 storm that previously devastated the railway (6) . The structure performed as designed, deflecting waves and safeguarding both the town and railway.
sections.challenges: Key challenges during construction included: Working adjacent to a live railway on a beachfront (7) . Ensuring the new structure integrated with the existing sea wall (7) . Engineering challenges surrounding the Grade II listed Dawlish station building; Dawlish Water stilling basin, where the stream runs out into the sea; and the town gateway (1) . Managing temporary works to prevent the existing sea wall from collapsing (7) .
sections.lessons_learned: Due to the difficult coastal environment, traditional construction methods such as the traditional jack-up barge were ineffective (7) . To overcome these challenges, an innovative solution—the WaveWalker—was deployed; this was the first time this type of barge was used to upgrade the UK rail network and allowed the team to work across high tidal ranges (1) . This spider-like machine can manoeuvre through waves and adapt to varying water depths, allowing uninterrupted work even in hostile conditions (7) . Unlike conventional methods restricted by tides and weather, the Wave Walker enabled continuous construction, saving £7 million and three months in construction time (7) .
sections.innovation_opportunities: Future coastal infrastructure can be enhanced through the following: oSmart sea walls with AI-driven monitoring contributing to predictive maintenance. oNature-based solutions like artificial reefs which can work to further protect coastlines and support marine life. oLow-carbon materials such as self-healing concrete which can repair its own cracks, therefore reducing maintenance requirements. oAutonomous construction and modular prefabrication which can improve efficiency and safety oIntegrating tidal energy and multi-use public spaces which can maximise environmental and social benefits. These innovations will drive resilience, sustainability, and long-term cost savings.

sections.challenge: • The New Cross Gate Depot is a key train maintenance and stabling facility for London Overground trains. The depot is situated in an urban area where excessive rainwater runoff could place a strain on the local wastewater infrastructure. • Without a system to manage rainwater runoff from the depot’s large buildings efficiently, the depot’s facilities and surrounding infrastructure were at risk of water pooling and drainage overload (4) . • Rainwater harvesting systems typically reduce the risk of surface water flooding caused by heavy rainfall and improve the quality of water entering combined sewers (3) .
sections.adaptation_measures: Measure implemented • To address the challenge of rainwater runoff from buildings, a rainwater harvesting system with underground rainwater storage tanks was incorporated into the depot design (4) . • In addition to addressing the challenge of water runoff from buildings and the system helps to maintain legal compliance for water discharge to the public sewers (4) . • The system was incorporated into the initial design and construction of the depot, keeping costs within the agreed capital budget (4) . • The design included additional equipment for waste water processing, to treat trade effluent from heavy cleaning activities, e.g. graffiti removal from trains, for reuse in carriage washing alongside the harvested rainwater (4) . • Harvested rainwater is used in the train cleaning carriage washer with up to 50% recycled water usage, which in 2023-04 amounted to over 23,000 cubic metres (4) . • Rainwater harvesting significantly reduces depot water consumption and supports the train cleaning process without adding to the burden on the local resources (4) . Organisation Transport for London (TfL) Transport sub-sectors Rail First order climate hazards (cause) Heavy rainfall; Flooding – surface water Second order climate hazards (effect) Water damage Transport asset impacted Buildings and warehouses; Track Adaptation measure Rainwater harvesting and reuse system integrated into the design and construction of a rollingstock depot Location  London, UK Years of delivery 2009 – 2010 Other options considered • This information is not publicly available.
sections.applicability: • Rainwater harvesting systems can be implemented in a variety of commercial and industrial settings in the UK, especially those with large roof areas and high water demands (e.g., transport depots and other industrial premises containing water intensive processes) (4) . • Smart systems for storing and releasing water before storms can reduce flood risks. In this way rainwater harvesting systems can contribute to the reduction of peak run off flows in areas prone to intense rainfall (4) .
sections.financials: Funding • Funding was allocated from TfL's internal infrastructure budget, as part of the depot construction cost (4) . Initial costs • This information is not publicly available. Ongoing costs • This information is not publicly available. Avoided costs • Historically, the rainwater harvesting system has provided over 80% of the depot’s water needs for train washing which results in a significant reduction in water and sewerage costs (2) . • In addition, the combined water management system can treat and recycle certain trade effluents from the depot, thereby avoiding the need for offsite disposal and its associated cost (4) .
sections.resourcing: • The depot was constructed by a joint venture between Balfour Beatty and Carillion PLC (4) . Rainwater Harvesting at Willesden depot Rainwater Harvesting at Willesden depot
sections.co_benefits: Environmental • Significant reduction in mains water consumption by the depot (4) . • Compliance with environmental regulations concerning water discharge (4) . • Capture and use of rainwater within the depot reducing runoff from the site (4) . Community • The system captures and reuses rainwater that would otherwise enter the public drainage system, helping to reduce pressure on local sewers during heavy rainfall. This minimises the likelihood of urban flooding, which can affect surrounding homes, businesses, and roadways (4) . Economic • Cost savings from reduced water consumption and avoided penalties for water-related infractions (4) .
sections.evaluation: • Success is evaluated based on the amount of water harvested and reused, which contributes to cost savings (4) . • There have been no reports of flooding or significant water-related infrastructure damage at the depot since its construction in 2010 (4) . • The system has successfully reduced the depot’s water consumption, making it more sustainable and resilient (exact volumes pending data from TfL) (4) . • The depot Facilities Manager routinely monitors depot water consumption and the pump hours run by the harvesting equipment to determine water volumes saved and used (4) .
sections.challenges: • The project benefited from the fact that the adaptation was part of the original design for the depot. There were the typical challenges related to site development, such as ensuring the soil was suitable for the installation of storage tanks. However, no significant barriers to designing and installing the rainwater harvesting system were encountered (4) . • Understanding the amount of water processed requires a calculation based on pump running time and performance. Data on running times is gathered manually from four pumps to drive the calculation. This makes the monitoring process labour intensive and time consuming (4) .
sections.lessons_learned: • System design: The integration of rainwater harvesting from the outset of construction proved to be more effective than retrofitting similar systems (4) . • Data management: Data collection from the current system is a manual process and future installations would benefit from automatic and remote data collection capabilities (4) .
sections.innovation_opportunities: • Integrating with green infrastructure: Combining rainwater harvesting systems with green infrastructure solutions could enhance the environmental benefits of such systems, such as creating green roofs or natural filtration systems (4) . • Smart water management: Exploring smart technologies that allow for more efficient water management, such as predictive models to optimise water release based on weather forecasts (4) .
