sections.challenge: • Around the world, modern urban environments are facing the challenge of flood resilience as sea levels rise, including in major cities such as London (1,8) . • The Thames Estuary in central London has multiple causes of flooding, including high tides, storm surges from the North Sea, increased river flood flows, and heavy rainfall (5,6,11) . • The need for flood defences on the Thames was underscored by a tidal surge at high tide in 1953, termed the North Sea Flood, which caused over 300 people to lose their lives in the UK (1,2,6,10,13) . o The North Sea Flood was estimated to have caused £50 million in damages, the equivalent to £5 billion today (14,16,17) , in part because the tidal surge caused the collapse of 100 metres of sea wall in East London (16) . o A report on the North Sea flood published by Sir Hermann Bondi in 1966 concluded that a flood barrier near Woolwich was required, as well as increases to the height of river banks downstream (13,17) . • Construction of the Thames Barrier began in 1974 and it became operational in 1982 (1,2) . • The Thames Barrier, located at the Estuary, was constructed following previous severe flooding and is designed to mitigate the risk of high tides occurring at the same time as storm surges (2,11) . • The Barrier prevents the storm surges and tidal flooding of the River Thames from damaging thousands of buildings and a significant proportion of London’s underground tube network, which would result in billions of pounds of damage (13,14) . • The Thames Estuary is important for London’s transport network because it enables the transportation of passenger vessels and commercial cargo along the River Thames (9) . Between 3,000 and 5,000 vessels travel through the Barrier each month (14) . • Climate change is increasing the risk of flooding due to more frequent and extreme weather events, increasing sea levels, and increasing levels of river discharge in fluvial flood condition (1,2,5,8,9,11) . Organisation Environment Agency Transport sub-sectors Rail; Maritime; Roads First order climate hazards Flooding – coastal; Flooding – fluvial; Heavy rainfall; Sea level rise Second order climate hazards Storm damage; Water damage Transport assets impacted Buildings and stations; Canals and inland waterways; Port structures; Road pavement Adaptation measures Tidal barriers Location  London, England Years of delivery 1974 to present • Climate change projections indicate that the current Barrier could be overtopped in 2070 due to sea level rise (1,2,5,6,10) . • The Environment Agency’s Thames Estuary 2100 (TE2100) Plan includes plans to manage the current and future flooding risks facing the Thames Estuary through upgrades to the Barrier over time (1,2,5,8,10,13) .
sections.adaptation_measures: Measure implemented • The design and construction of the Barrier was preceded by commissions into flood protection studies by the UK Government following the storm surge in 1953 (2) . • The Thames Barrier is comprised of 10 steel gates (6) . The four main gates are each 61.5 metres wide and 15 metres tall, with each gate weighing around 3,300 tonnes (2,3,4,6,7,10,13) . Each gate can hold back water loads weighing up to 9,000 tonnes (4) . • The gates are designed to withstand bombs and they are also fail-proof to electricity outages, for example there are three-backup power generators that could power the Barrier for a month if the National Grid lost power (14) . • Under normal conditions the main gates are open, which means they lie flat on the bed of the River Thames and allow ships and river traffic to travel unrestricted through the Barrier (2,4,6,7) . • In the event of an incoming tidal surge, each gate is rotated upwards in-turn to form a solid steel flood defence (2,5,6) . Once activated, the gates prevent water flowing upstream towards the centre of London (4,5,6) . • The Environment Agency receives forecasts of potential tidal surges up to 36 hours in advance from a range of sources including weather ships and satellites (3) , and it has a team of in-house flood forecasters that monitor the tides for 24 hours per day all year round (12) . • The Thames Barrier extends across the River Thames, spanning 520 metres (1,3) . It was designed to protect central London from flooding of the River Thames caused by tidal surges (1,6) , including the protection of critical transport networks such as the London Underground (2) . • Over an area of 125km , the Thames Barrier protects: 116 railway and tube stations, including 25 mainline train stations and 54 underground and Docklands Light Railway (DLR) stations; over 300km of major roads; 420,000 properties; 226 schools; more than a dozen hospitals, fire stations, and police stations; and nearly 1.5 million people who reside below the average high tide (1,2,9,13) . • The capital value of residential property assets that the Barrier protects amounts to around £321 billion (1,9,13) . • The Barrier was originally designed to withstand a 1-in-100-year flood up to the year 2030 (13) .Due to rising sea levels, the TE2100 Plan is required to upgrade the Barrier to protect against the impacts of climate change on rainfall, flooding, and sea level rise (17) . • The first stage of upgrades to defences downstream of the Barrier, primarily raising some defences by 30 to 60cm, depending on location, are due to be completed in 2040 (5) . • By 2050, the TE2100 Plan states that that some downstream defences will need to be 50cm higher to protect against higher regular tide heights (5) . Other options considered • Other options to increasing the level of defence of the Thames Barrier included raising the height of the banks of the River Thames and the installation of a moveable barrier on a barrage or a fixed barrier with locks at different locations along the Thames (15) . • These options were not pursued mainly due to the requirements for large navigation openings, at least 1400ft across, to aid with the navigation of commercial boats and vessels through the flood defence (15) . The Thames Barrier has four openings, each 2000ft across that enable navigation (15) . • The TE2100 Plan recommends actions for the Environment Agency and other stakeholders to take in the short term (the next 25 years), medium term (the subsequent 15 years), and long term (the period to the end of the century) (3,5) . • A decision on the option for the end of the century will be taken in 2040 by the Environment Agency in consultation with its partners (13) . • As well as sea level rise, economic and commercial considerations inform decision-making around planned upgrades to the Thames Barrier (10) .
sections.applicability: • Due to its scale, this adaptation measure would be applicable to other major urban and transportation hubs of a similar scale of economic vulnerability to the city of London. • Elements of this adaptation measure that could be applicable to other locations include the ability to use the river under normal river conditions, and implement moving barriers that close the river temporarily, during the highest tides. • The rotating lifting barriers element of the structure could be not only installed in other navigable rivers but could potentially also be installed within transport infrastructure such as highways that lie in low lying gaps in coastal areas (between hardstanding areas or natural topographic gaps) that become increasingly frequently inundated by rising sea levels or high fluvial flood levels, as a more permanent alternative to temporary flood barriers. • Flood defence schemes that allow navigation in ‘normal’ conditions have been installed for other vulnerable cities of significant importance such as Venice, Rotterdam (the Maeslantkering barrier) and in the Netherlands (the Oosterscheldedam). • Flood schemes that were designed and installed some decades ago (1980s) may be applicable to other moving, lifting barriers in similar situations. • While sea level will rise across the UK and storms will affect all coasts, the east of England can be particularly vulnerable to storm surges due to the funnel shape of the North Sea, within which a northernly wind and low pressure weather system can force higher levels of water progressively south from Scotland and into estuaries and low lying land. This was the primary cause of the extensive flooding in 1953 on the east coast of the UK and the Netherlands coasts. For this reason, a movable large barrier has been well suited to London.
sections.financials: Funding • The British Government paid for the construction of the Thames Barrier (19) . Initial costs • It took eight years to construct the Thames Barrier and cost £535 million, the equivalent of £2.4 billion in 2024 prices (10,13,14) . Ongoing costs • Once a month the Barrier is operated for maintenance and to test its functionality (3) . It costs around £6 million each year to operate and maintain the Barrier (17) . Avoided costs • Information not publicly available.
sections.resourcing: • An engineering consultancy firm, namely Rendel, Palmer and Tritton, led the design of the Thames Barrier (1,13) . • The Thames Barrier is operated and maintained by the Environment Agency (3) , primarily by a team of in-house electricians and engineers (10,12) . • 80 members of staff are needed to operate and maintain the Barrier (17) .
sections.co_benefits: Biodiversity • When not in use, the gates lay horizontal on the bed of the River Thames which allows for the natural flow of the river and maintains the river ecosystem habitat for aquatic life (18) . Community • Without the Barrier, the flood defence walls in London would need to be as tall as streetlamps, and so the Barrier preserves the unobstructed view of the River Thames for residents and tourists (17) . Economic • The Barrier protects London’s major businesses, including tourism, which safeguards the regional economy from sea level rise and fluvial flooding (9,13) .
sections.evaluation: • Between 1982 and April 2024, the Thames Barrier was activated as a flood defence 221 times; more than half of these instances (119) were to prevent tidal flooding, and the remaining 102 activations were to alleviate fluvial flooding (2,3,10,13) . • In the 2013/2014 tax year the Thames Barrier was used (closed) 50 times over a three-month period, marking the busiest time in its operation due to the frequency and intensity of storms (1,10,11,13) . • The increased use in this period was due to heavy rainfall and the risk of tidal flooding (6,12) . • The use of the Barrier during this time meant that no properties in London were flooded (1,13) due to regular maintenance and inspections of the Barrier (11) .
sections.challenges: • The design and construction of the Thames Barrier faced many engineering and operational challenges including deep water, high currents, and large ships requiring access to the Thames (1) . • There were also contractual challenges during the construction of the Barrier due to a peak inflation rate of 24% in the 1970s which meant contractors would not agree to a contract with a fixed price (15) . • Due to climate change, the Thames Barrier will be required to close more often due to more frequent storm surges, flooding events, and increasing sea levels (1,2) .The challenges due to the Barrier being required to close more frequently are that: o  The Barrier can become less reliable due to more wear and tear on the mechanisms (5,9) . o Shipping movements are temporarily affected until the gates are opened by the next low tide (5) . • A challenge is that changes or improvements to flood defences in other parts of the country can affect decision-making about upgrades to the Barrier under the TE2100 Plan (11) .
sections.lessons_learned: • The Thames Barrier itself cannot mitigate against the risk of inland fluvial flooding due to heavy rainfall, and so the TE2100 Plan is considering the use of Sustainable Drainage Systems (SuDS) such as permeable pavements and road surfaces along the River Thames (18) . A lesson learnt is that existing major infrastructure may need further substantial upgrades and adaptation over time, for example as sea levels continue to rise. • A new Thames Barrier is likely to be required by the end of the century, in Kent and/or Essex, and its design and construction will require close partnerships with relevant Local Authorities (20) . This shows that options previously considered but discounted can start to become viable (such as river walls considered in the 1970s) as part of overall continued adaptation. • The TE2100 Plan introduced Riverside Strategies, to be produced by LAs as part of local planning by 2030, to ensure upgrades to flood defences are planned in-line with the visions LAs have for stretches of the Thames (21) . • The Riverside Strategies are intended to create additional benefits to flood defences through upgrades under the TE2100 Plan (21) . For example, improved access to the river for recreational purposes and the creation of new wildlife habitat (20,21) . • A positive lesson learnt is that the Thames Barrier has been valuable in protecting London from storm surges and an appropriate adaptation measure for this scale of protection. It has also enabled continued commercial navigation.
sections.innovation_opportunities: The TE2100 Plan includes an end-of-the century upgrade option that would involve the installation of locks, which would allow boats and ships to pass through the Barrier whilst it is activated (5,10) .

sections.challenge: • The city of Berlin has experienced rising temperatures, severe storms and heavy rainfalls due to climate change (1) . • Berlin’s efforts in climate adaptation have been made a legal obligation with the Berlin Energy Turnaround Act of 2016 (1) . • Measures to adapt to climate change have been set out in the integrated Berlin Energy and Climate Programme 2030, including several strategies for the transport sector, as well as greening commitments to help the city become more resilient to rising temperatures (2) . The measures included are based on recommendations made following a research project ‘Adapting to the Impact of Climate Change in Berlin’ (1) . • The Berlin tram system is an important part of the transport network, providing an easy way to travel around the city on 22 different lines and nearly 400 stations (13) . However, as with many transport sectors, it is expected that each sector will be exposed to climate risks such as drought, flooding, and severe storms, as the impacts of climate change intensify (14) .
sections.adaptation_measures: Measure implemented The Berlin Public Transport Authority (BVG) first introduced green light tram tracks in 1995 which involve the planting of vegetation along the tramways (3) . BVG conducted a pilot project featuring tracks planted with sedum, the results of which led BVG to stipulate that this model would be used as standard in its network for future projects and implement over 22,000 metres of similar track in several projects (10) . Green tracks can be planted with either sedum or grass, and the benefits of implementing green tracks include (11) : • Urban water retention: During the summer months, green tracks are able to retain about 90% of rain that has fallen onto the track. Any remaining run off water is also less polluted as it is filtered by the vegetation (4) . Organisation City of Berlin Transport sub-sector Rail First order climate hazards (cause) Drought; Flooding - surface water; Heavy rainfall; High temperatures Second order climate hazards (effect) Air pollution (dust); Urban Heat Island (UHI) effect; Water damage Transport asset impacted Tramways Adaptation measure Green track Location  Berlin, Germany Years of delivery 1995 - ongoing • Reduced heat island effect: The Urban Heat Island effect is often exacerbated by the heat absorption of buildings; however, plants, such as those planted along the tramways, have a low heat storage capacity in comparison and so they contribute to a cooler environment (4) . • Track temperature reduction: Vegetation provides the joint benefit of reducing heating and offering heat protection by covering the track underneath. When compared to ballasted tracks which can rise to 50 – 60 o C, vegetation systems only rise to 25 – 30 o C, which will help to mitigate the impacts of increasing temperatures (4) . • Air pollution reduction: Green tracks help to uptake particulate matter, therefore reducing fine dust concentrations in the air and contributing to urban fine dust management, creating a safer environment for citizens to live and work in (4) . • Noise pollution reduction: Assuming wheel and rail contribute equally to the sound, a vegetated track can reduce noise pollution by 3 dB(A) when compared to a ballasted track. This can be particularly beneficial for trams passing through residential areas in the evenings or early mornings (4) . Other options considered • Information not publicly available.
sections.applicability: • There are existing tram systems in several key cities such as Blackpool, Edinburgh, London, Manchester, Nottingham, and Sheffield, and ongoing discussions around building new tram systems in cities such as Leeds. • The use of green tramways could be applicable to the UK although robust maintenance strategies would be required in consideration of the risks from trespassing vehicles and emergency vehicles accessing the tramway. • The use of green tramways is more likely to be applicable to new tramways rather than existing ones, as the costs of adding planting to existing lines would be high.
sections.financials: Funding • Although providing green tramways is an ongoing initiative in Berlin and so each project will have different financial support, one recently finished green tramway project called the Adlershof II line was funded by the Berlin Programme for Sustainable Development (BENE) Tram in the city center Green tramways Green tramways which is made up of contributions from the European Regional Development Fund and the State of Berlin (7) . Initial costs • A specific value is not publicly available; however, the costs of different vegetation types is largely comparable. The cost will differ depending on the chosen vegetation establishment method, such as seeding or rolling turf (6) . Ongoing costs • In order to introduce green tram tracks, the tramway will need to be covered with a substrate and vegetation layer. The substrate will need to be replaced every 2-5 years and this therefore only allows for temporary planting (6) . • Maintenance costs will be required as vegetation will require fertilisation, regular mowing, and watering during drought periods unless an automated sprinkler system is installed (5) . Avoided costs • Storm water fees, which are in place in most municipalities in Germany, are charges based on a property’s contribution to total runoff to help fund rainwater management and encourage sustainable practices (15) . A storm water fee of 1.897 € (£1.59) per m² sealed area has been charged in Berlin since 2009. Should this fee also be charged for tracks, Berlin’s transport company, BVG, could save around 5,300 € (£4,433) of storm water fee per greened kilometre double track (4) . • Avoided costs may arise from reduced maintenance requirements; greening systems reduce the risk of rail fractures and reduce the need for tamping of ballast beds (4) . • No further information on avoided costs is available. Economic impacts • Information not publicly available.
sections.resourcing: • Limited information on resourcing is publicly available, however, as of 2014, the Berlin tram system had implemented over 36 kilometres of ‘RHEDA CITY GREEN’ track, a system offered by the company RAILONE.
sections.co_benefits: Biodiversity • Urban flora can provide additional habitats for organisms such as insects, which can help to boost their chance of survival in an otherwise highly degraded environment, therefore increasing biodiversity (3) . Community • Implementing green tracks can provide an urban space with a green area, which can be particularly beneficial if an urban space is lacking in green areas (4) . • Greening tracks has been observed to provide a calming effect for tram drivers, who have found driving over green tracks less tiring and exhausting for the eyes (4) . Economic • As with train tracks, tram tracks can also buckle in direct sunshine and intense heat (12) . Plant cover can provide protection from the harmful effects of direct sunlight which therefore helps to increase the durability of the track structure, therefore helping to prevent the need for repairs (3) . • Studies have indicated the visual importance of green spaces in shopping areas, where it was found that 12% of customers are willing to pay higher prices in greened business areas (4) .
sections.evaluation: • The ecological, economic, and urban design benefits green tram lines provide to urban areas has seen Germany increase their green tram tracks to 676km in 2022 (9) . • This corresponds to an area of over 169 ha or 1.69 million m² (9) . • The success of this trend can also be found worldwide with places such as Belgium, China, Spain, and Australia all adopting green tram lines (9) .
sections.challenges: Tram tracks are often embedded or level with the ground which can make it easy for vehicles to drive onto the tramway (8) . • This can churn up the grass tracks which will require maintenance, and should the vehicle become stuck this can cause an obstruction (8) . • Tramways are very location specific due to the risk of trespassing vehicles, and therefore prevention methods, robust risk strategies, and maintenance programmes should be developed before vegetation can be implemented (8) .
sections.lessons_learned: • Emergency vehicles must be considered when designing green tramway tracks as it is not suitable for these vehicles to drive on vegetated tracks. Therefore, alternative routes for emergency service vehicles may need to be considered (5) . • In Germany, emergency vehicles have priority of the road and can therefore cross tram tracks in an emergency. • If it is essential to allow passage of emergency vehicles on the tracks outside the crossing, then the additional maintenance costs for the vegetation as a result should be taken into consideration (5) .
sections.innovation_opportunities: • The benefits from green tramways could be enhanced by being incorporated into a wider programme of biodiversity enhancements and planting, particularly in urban environments. This would encourage larger-scale programmes demonstrating nature-based solutions, urban greening, and provision of community facilities.

organisation: Department of Transport and Main Roads

sections.challenge: • Between 2009 and 2013, Queensland experienced closure of 23-62% of their roads due to an increase in the frequency and severity of storms. Approximately 26%, or 8,741km, of the state- controlled network required full or partial reconstruction during this period (1) . • There were multiple flooding events across southern Queensland in 2021-22 (4) . • Flood damage to roads particularly affects areas with expansive soils as these are prone to shrink-swell activity (1) . • Infrastructure was damaged frequently due to flooding, leading to disruptions in service and substantial economic costs. Adaptation was required to reduce vulnerability and improve long- term resilience (1) .
sections.adaptation_measures: Measures implemented • Foam-bitumen stabilisation was chosen for both Camp Cable Road and Stegemann Road as the pavement treatment to enhance flood resilience. • Foamed bitumen stabilisation is a road construction technique whereby bitumen is used to bind the existing or imported granular material to produce a flexible pavement material for use in base and subbase pavement layers, and in particular for road rehabilitation (6) . • Foamed bitumen is a mixture of hot bitumen, water and air. In Queensland typical quantities are 97.5% bitumen, 2% water and 0.5% foaming agent (6) . • Foamed bitumen stabilisation injects small amounts of air and cold water into hot bitumen, the sticky dark substance typically used for road surfaces. The bitumen then expands and forms a water-resistant layer. The result is a stronger yet flexible road surface or pavement that is better able to withstand flooding (7) . • Foamed bitumen stabilised roads were able to reopen to traffic shortly after the floodwaters subsided, as reported by the Queensland Department of Transport and Main Roads (8) . Other options considered Organisation Department of Transport and Main Roads Queensland Transport subsector Roads First order climate hazards (cause) Flooding – surface water; Heavy rainfall Second order climate hazard (effect) NA Transport asset impacted Road pavement Adaptation measure Foamed bitumen stabilisation Location  Queensland, Australia Years of delivery 2017 - present • In response to the large scale economic impacts of multiple severe flooding events across southern Queensland in 2021–22, the Department of Transport and Main Roads, (TMR), is making a range of improvements to minimise future flood damage and reduce road closures including (4) : o Strengthening and altering levels of road surfaces to make them more flood resistant o Stabilising slopes to prevent erosion and landslips o Installing larger culverts to improve flood capacity o Drainage and floodway enhancements to improve run-off and mitigate erosion and scouring o Batter protection to reduce the impacts of high-velocity flows on bridges and floodways o Floodproof covers for Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS) equipment • Foamed bitumen is one of the materials considered for paving repairs alongside traditional materials such as asphalt, concrete, unbound pavements and stabilised pavements (5) . • This material was chosen over other options such as traditional asphalt or concrete due to its superior performance in flood conditions. It balances initial construction costs with long-term durability and lower maintenance needs (1) . Foamed bitumen has also provided a flexible and fatigue-resistant stabilisation treatment suitable for Queensland’s conditions (2) . • The cost of implementing foam-bitumen stabilisation for these roads was higher initially compared to conventional treatments, but the long-term cost savings from reduced repairs and maintenance justified the investment (1) .
sections.applicability: • As in Australia, the UK's transport infrastructure, including roads, bridges, and railways, especially in flood-prone areas, faces increasing risks from climate change. Adaptation measures, such as foam-bitumen stabilisation, could help to improve resilience during extreme weather events. • Foam-bitumen is often used to stabilise gravel roads by binding the materials together, increasing the strength and durability of the surface. It works well for roads with lower traffic loads and areas where cost-effectiveness is important (6) . Within the UK this could make it particularly useful in locations such as rural areas with high rainfall where roads provide a Foamed Bitumen Stabilisation works Foamed Bitumen Stabilisation works critical link for communities but are hard to access, for example areas of Wales, Scotland, Northern Ireland and upland northern England.
sections.financials: Funding • The funding for the adaptation measures came from government sources, such as the TMR in Queensland and local authorities like Logan City Council, with additional support from disaster recovery programs like the National Disaster Recovery and Relief Arrangements (NDRRA) (1) . Initial costs • The initial costs involved higher upfront investments due to the engineering and material requirements for foam-bitumen stabilisation. Actual expenditure data is not publicly available (1) Ongoing costs • These include regular maintenance and monitoring costs to maintain the infrastructure’s resilience to flooding and extreme weather. Data is not publicly available (1) . Avoided costs • Avoided costs were captured through Life-Cycle Costing (LCC), highlighting significant savings on repairs and disruptions, especially during flood events. Data not publicly available (1) .
sections.resourcing: • TMR played a central role in driving the adaptation efforts across Queensland’s transport infrastructure, including Camp Cable Road and Stegemann Road (1) . • TMR developed updated engineering guidelines and design standards to incorporate resilience against extreme weather, flooding, and other climate hazards (1)(9) . • Logan City Council was involved in the adaptation planning for roads prone to flooding, especially Stegemann Road. The council worked on developing resilience classifications for the road network (10) and adopted smart city technologies for managing flood risks (1) . For example, Logan City Council uses the Flooded Road Smart Warning System to send real-time alerts to the public disaster dashboard on the Council’s website and notify council staff and emergency services (11) . • A collaborative approach was used to share knowledge between government bodies and technical organisations. This included the PIARC (World Road Association) workshop held in 2017, which facilitated the exchange of adaptation strategies (1) .
sections.co_benefits: Environmental • One of the co-benefits observed was the improved environmental resilience of the road infrastructure, which reduced the need for frequent repairs and mitigated the environmental impact of regular road resurfacing (1) . • The stabilisation helped minimise the road's contribution to soil erosion and runoff during heavy rains (1) . It enhances the soil’s load-bearing capacity and is more effective in stabilising a wide range of soil types, especially in road construction (13) . • The use of local materials for road treatments helped reduce the environmental footprint by cutting down on transport emissions and contributing to local economies (1) . Community • By implementing resilient measures, the roads were able to withstand flooding, which led to fewer road closures and less travel disruption, benefiting both local communities and freight hauliers that rely on these key transport routes (3) . Economic • The treatment contributes to reduced long-term maintenance costs and enhances road safety during extreme weather events (1) .
sections.evaluation: • Post-flood assessments at Camp Cable Road and Stegemann Road showed that these roads performed significantly better than traditional untreated roads during extreme conditions (1) . • Camp Cable Road on the Mt Lindsey Highway was inundated by 3 metres of floodwater in 2017 but the foamed bitumen pavement was found to be completely intact when the waters receded (1) .
sections.challenges: • The upfront investment for foamed bitumen stabilisation was higher than traditional road treatments, creating budgetary challenges, especially in regions with limited funding for climate adaptation (1) . • Cost per lane-km of foam stabilised is NZD $150,000 (£66,585), while costs for other stabilisation methods such as slag, basalt, greywacke, 2% lime, 4% lime, and 2% cement are lower, between NZD $100,000 (£44,380) and NZD $150,000 (£66,585) (12) .
sections.lessons_learned: • Proactive investment in climate resilience measures, like foamed bitumen stabilisation, helped avoid significant costs and disruption during extreme weather events (1) . • While the initial costs were high, the long-term benefits, such as reduced repair needs and fewer road closures, justified the investment. A cost-benefit analysis proved valuable for demonstrating the financial advantages of these measures (1) .
sections.innovation_opportunities: • There are opportunities to reduce waste and emissions in the repair and construction of roads by: o Incorporating recycled materials as a substitute for sand and aggregates in road materials, e.g. crushed glass o Recycling existing road materials in situ and reincorporating them into the new road
